questions.question: Chemistry does not play a major role in:
questions.question_raw: Chemistry does not play a major role in:

options.label: B
options.text: Large scale production of a variety of fertilizers.

options.label: C
options.text: Design and synthesis new materials having specific magnetic, electric and optical properties.

questions.explanation:
Chemistry does not deal with explaining superconductivity.
*In superconducting materials the characteristics of superconductivity appear when the temperature is lowered below a critical temperature.
*The onset of superconductivity is accompanied by abrupt changes in physical properties which are more related to phase transitions of the material.
These aspects of studies in properties of materials are better related to studies in the fields of Physics, even though principles involved in Chemistry & Physics go hand to hand.

questions.question: Matter can be classified at the macroscopic or bulk level as:
questions.question_raw: Matter can be classified at the macroscopic or bulk level as:
questions.explanation: At the macroscopic level, the matter is classified as a mixture or pure substances. A mixture contains two or more components (in any ratio ) present in it, for example -- sugar solutions in water, tea, air, alloys like brass, bronze, etc. Pure substances (viz. copper, silver, gold, water, glucose, etc.) have a fixed composition. The constituents of pure substances can not be separated by simple physical methods.

questions.question: In a homogeneous mixture, the components are:
questions.question_raw: In a homogeneous mixture, the components are:

options.label: A
options.text: do not mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.

options.label: B
options.text: do not mix with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.

options.label: C
options.text: completely mixed with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.

options.label: D
options.text: completely mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.

correct_option_texts: completely mixed with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.
questions.explanation:
In a homogeneous mixture,
 the components are mixed together completely.
 has a uniform composition throughout.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about pure substances is correct?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements about pure substances is correct?

options.label: D
options.text: Pure substances do not have fixed composition.

questions.explanation: Pure substances have a fixed composition. These may be both (i.e. either elements or compounds). The law of constant composition holds good for such substances.

questions.question: Particles of compounds are:
questions.question_raw: Particles of compounds are:

options.label: C
options.text: molecules of two or more atoms of different elements

correct_option_texts: molecules of two or more atoms of different elements
questions.explanation: Compounds are made when, atoms of two or more different elements combine together in a fixed ratio to form molecules (viz. water, ammonia, carbon dioxide, glucose, etc. ). Thus the particles of the compound consist of molecules.

questions.question: Fill up the blanks with a suitable word in the statement, " Physical properties are those properties which are measured or observed ________ changing the identity or the composition of the substance ."
questions.question_raw: Fill up the  blanks  with a suitable word in the statement, " Physical properties are those properties which are measured or observed ________ changing the identity or the composition of the substance ."
questions.explanation: A substance is identified by its composition, hence for measuring physical properties of a substance its identity must be retained, (or should not be changed) Hence, the given statement, " Physical properties are those properties which are measured or observed without changing the identity or composition of the substance." is correct.

questions.question: SI units for Base Physical Quantities of length, mass, and current are:
questions.question_raw: SI units for Base Physical Quantities of length, mass, and current are:
questions.explanation:
The International System of Units ( abbreviated as SI ) is a modern form of the metric system and is the most widely used form of measurements.
It comprises of a coherent system of measurement built of seven base units.
The base units for length, mass, and current in this system of measurements are, " meter, kilogram, ampere" symbolized respectively as m, kg, A.

questions.question: SI unit of density is:
questions.question_raw: SI unit of density is:
questions.explanation:
Since density = mass / volume and SI unit of mass is kg and that of the volume is $m^3$.
$\therefore$ using these SI units the unit of density is derived through dimensional analysis:
density = mass / volume
= kg / m^3 
or, = kg m^-3

questions.question: The Kelvin scale is related to celsius scale as:
questions.question_raw: The Kelvin scale is related to celsius scale as:
questions.explanation:
The relation between the Kelvin scale & Celcius scale of temperatures is -
K = °C + 273.15
Such a relationship is based upon the experimental findings and subsequent conclusion of Charle's law.
The Kelvin scale is also termed as an "absolute scale of temperature."
It is interesting to note that temperature below 0^oC ( ie. negative values ) is possible in the Celcius scale but in Kelvin scale, the negative temperature is not possible.

questions.question: In scientific notation for numbers, any number can be represented in the form, N $\times$ 10^n where,
questions.question_raw: In scientific notation for numbers, any number can be represented in the form, N {tex}\times{/tex} 10n where,

options.label: A
options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 10 to 100.

options.label: B
options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 0 to 1.

options.label: C
options.text: n is an exponent having positive values only and N can vary from 1 to 10.

options.label: D
options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values. N represents a number that can vary from 1 to 10, but not 10 itself.

correct_option_texts: n is an exponent having positive or negative values. N represents a number that can vary from 1 to 10, but not 10 itself.
questions.explanation: Scientific notation is conveniently used to represent extremely large or even very small numerical values. It is an exponential notation in which any number can be represented in the form, N $\times$ 10^n, where n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary between 1 and 10, but not 10 itself.

questions.question: Precision and Accuracy refer to _______ of various measurements for the same quantity repeatedly and agreement of particular value to the _________ of the result, respectively.
questions.question_raw: Precision and Accuracy refer to _______ of various measurements for the same quantity repeatedly and agreement of particular value to the _________ of the result, respectively.
questions.explanation:
Precision means the closeness of all the data values measured while accuracy means the closeness of a particular value with the real value.
For example,
when the reported values are 2.01, and 1.99 gas the result and its true value is 2.00 g.
These values are both precise and accurate.

questions.question: Significant figures are:
questions.question_raw: Significant figures are:

options.label: B
options.text: meaningful digits which are known with certainty

options.label: D
options.text: first three digits after the decimal point

correct_option_texts: meaningful digits which are known with certainty
questions.explanation: Significant digits are certain digits that have significance or meaning to certain digits in a number. Such digits help scientists to use them with more precise information about measurement and other numeric data. These digits also help them in rounding off a very large or very small number

questions.question: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.question_raw: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio  by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.explanation:
Gay lussac’s law states that when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
Mathematically, the law is expressed as,
[V/n] = k
where V represents the volume of the gas, n denotes the number of moles of the gas & k is the proportionality constant.
Thus, the volume ratio must be related to the relative number of molecules.
This relationship was crucial in establishing the formula of simple molecules at a time when a distinction between atoms and molecules was not clearly understood.

questions.question: According to Avogadro Law, equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure should contain _______ number of molecules.
questions.question_raw: According to Avogadro Law, equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure should contain _______ number of molecules.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law states that " equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules."

questions.question: Molecular mass of a compound is the ________ of atomic masses of elements present in a molecule.
questions.question_raw: Molecular mass of a compound is the ________ of atomic masses of elements present in a molecule.
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of the elements present in a molecule.
For example ; Molecular mass of CO_2 = $\Sigma$ atomic mass of one carbon atom & atomic mass of two oxygen atoms
= [ 12 + 2(16) ]u
= 44u.

tests.testPaperName: Basic concepts of chemistry Test-02
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Some Basic Concepts of Chemistry

questions.question: One mole is the amount of a substance that contains as many particles or entities as there are atoms in:
questions.question_raw: One mole is the amount of a substance that contains as many particles or entities as there are atoms in:

options.label: A
options.text: exactly 12 g (or 0.012 kg) of the $^{14}C$ isotope

options.label: C
options.text: exactly 12 g (or 0.012 kg) of the $^{13}C$ isotope

questions.explanation:
One mole is the amount of a substance that contains as many particles or entities as there are atoms in exactly 12 g of the $^{12}C$ isotope.
A mole of a substance always contains the same number of entities, no matter what the substance may be. In order to determine this number precisely, the mass of carbon -12 atom was determined by mass spectrometer and found to be equal to 1.992648 $\times$ 10^-23g. Knowing that one mole of carbon weighs 12g. the number of atoms in it is equal to: 6.0221367 $\times$ 10^23

questions.question: A given compound always contains exactly the same proportion of elements by weight. This law is stated by:
questions.question_raw: A given compound always contains exactly the same proportion of elements by weight. This law is stated by:
questions.explanation: The observation was first made by French chemist Joseph Proust, based on certain experiments conducted between 1798 and 1804. Proust made the above statement known as " Proust's law" or "Law of definite composition" or " Law of constant composition".

questions.question: Avogadro's constant is the number of entities (viz. atoms, molecules or ions) present in ________ mole of a substance.
questions.question_raw: Avogadro's constant is the number of entities (viz. atoms, molecules or ions) present in ________  mole of a substance.
questions.explanation:
Avagadro's constant represents the number of entities (viz, atoms, molecules, or ions ) present in 1 mole of a substance.
The constant is denoted by N = 6.022 $\times$ 10^23

questions.question: An empirical formula represents the simplest
questions.question_raw: An empirical formula represents the simplest

options.label: A
options.text: ratio of masses of various elements present in a compound

options.label: B
options.text: whole number ratio of various atoms present in a compound

options.label: C
options.text: average fractional ratio of various atoms present in a compound

options.label: D
options.text: whole number ratio of the elements present in a compound

correct_option_texts: whole number ratio of various atoms present in a compound
questions.explanation:
In chemistry, the empirical formula of a compound is the simplest positive integer ratio of atoms present in a compound.
However, this differs from the chemical formula which expresses the number of atoms in each molecule of a compound.
An empirical formula makes no mention of the arrangement of atoms in a compound.
It is standard for many ionic compounds like CaCl_2 or for macromolecules such as silicates etc.

questions.question: Molecular formula represents:
questions.question_raw: Molecular formula represents:

options.label: A
options.text: average fractionall ratio of various atoms present in a compound

options.label: B
options.text: whole number ratio of different types of atoms present in a molecule of a compound

options.label: C
options.text: the exact number of different types of atoms present in a molecule of a compound

options.label: D
options.text: ratio of masses of various atoms present in a compound

correct_option_texts: the exact number of different types of atoms present in a molecule of a compound
questions.explanation:
Molecular formula is the exact no. of atoms present in a molecule of a compound.
Molecular formula of a compound is related with its empirical formula as,
Mollecular formula = (Empirical formula)_n
where n represents a positive integer.

questions.question: According to the law of conservation of mass, a balanced chemical equation:
questions.question_raw: According to the law of conservation of mass, a balanced chemical equation:

options.label: A
options.text: the same number of electrons of each element on both sides of the equation

options.label: B
options.text: the same ratio of atoms of each element on both sides of the equation

options.label: C
options.text: the same number of protons of each element on both sides of the equation

options.label: D
options.text: the same number of atoms of each element on both sides of the equation

correct_option_texts: the same number of atoms of each element on both sides of the equation
questions.explanation:
According to the law of conservation of mass,
Total mass of reactants = total mass of product
$\therefore$ The number. of atoms of each element in reactants = number of atoms of that element in products.

questions.question: Molarity is defined as:
questions.question_raw: Molarity is defined as:

options.label: A
options.text: the number of moles of the solute in 1 litre of the solution

options.label: B
options.text: the number of moles of the solute in 1 m^3 of the solution

options.label: C
options.text: the number of grams of the solute in 1 litre of the solution

options.label: D
options.text: the number of moles of the solvent in 1 litre of the solution

correct_option_texts: the number of moles of the solute in 1 litre of the solution
questions.explanation:
Molarity is the most widely used method of expressing the strength or concentration of a solution.
It is denoted by ' M '.
Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute present per litre of solution.
Mathematically,
Molarity (M) = Number of moles of solute / Volume of solution in Litres

questions.question: The calculation of masses or, (sometimes volumes also) of the reactants and the products involved in a chemical reaction is called:
questions.question_raw: The calculation of masses or, (sometimes volumes also) of the reactants and the products involved in a chemical reaction is called:
questions.explanation:
Stoichiometry is a method to express quantitative aspects of a chemical reaction.
Usually, the masses of reactants as well as those of products in a chemical reaction are calculated using a corresponding balanced chemical equation.
It is convenient and hence desirable to calculate volumes of gaseous reactants and products.

questions.question: Molecular mass of glucose (C_6H_12O_6) is
questions.question_raw: Molecular mass of glucose (C6H12O6) is
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of Glucose is calculated using the relation:
Molecular mass
= $\sum$ (6*atomic mass of C, 12* atomic mass of H, 6* atomic mass of O) u
$\therefore$ substituting the respective atomic masses we get,
The molecular mass of glucose ( C_6H_12O_6)
= [6(12.0107) + 12(1.008) + 6(15.9994)]
= 180.162 u.

questions.question: Choose the most appropriate answer amongst the options given below for the statement - A solution of the desired concentration is prepared by diluting _______.
questions.question_raw: Choose the most appropriate answer amongst the options given below for the statement - A solution of the desired concentration is prepared by diluting _______.
questions.explanation:
Stock solution can best be described as a concentrated solution of known accurate concentration that will be used for future laboratory use.
Since large amounts of solutes are used for preparing stock solution a more accurate concentration of it can be achieved quite easily, and as such the chances are slim to get erroneous results of the related experiments.
In addition, stock solutions are generally more stable as compared to a working solution since they usually do not support bacterial growth.
Multiple working solutions can be prepared by dilution of stock solution using easy calculation and process.

questions.question: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of a solvent is called________.
questions.question_raw: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of a solvent is called________.
questions.explanation:
Molality is defined as no. of moles of solute present per kg of solvent. It is denoted as " m ". Mathematically,
Molality (m) = [number of moles of the solute] / kg of solvent
Since this mode of expressing the strength of a solution involves (weight/weight) relationship of solute and solvent, the molality of the solution is not affected by variation in temperatures of the solution.

questions.question: Molality (m) of 3 M aqueous solution of NaCl is: (Given: Density of solution $=1.25 {~g} {~mL}^{-1}$, Molar mass in ${g} {mol}^{-1}$ : ${Na}-23, {Cl}-35.5$)
questions.question_raw: Molality (m) of 3 M aqueous solution of NaCl is: (Given: Density of solution {tex}=1.25 {~g} {~mL}^{-1}{/tex}, Molar mass in {tex}{g} {mol}^{-1}{/tex} : {tex}{Na}-23, {Cl}-35.5{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Since Molarity represents a number of moles of solute per litre of solution.
$\therefore$ moles of solute = 3 mols.
As, density of the solution is given
= 1.25 g/ mL
$\therefore$ Mass of solution = 1250g
Mass of solvent = [1250 - 3 (58.5)] g
= (1250 - 175.5) g
= 1074 .5 g
= 1.0745 Kg
Molality
= Number of moles of solute / Kg of solvent
$\therefore$ Molality = $(\frac{3 mol}{1.0745 kg})$ = 2.79 m

questions.question: Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is :
questions.question_raw: Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is :
questions.explanation:
Molarity of a solution
(M) = $\frac{mass of solute}{molar mass of solute } \times $$\frac{1000}{volume of solution in mL } $ 
∴0.2​$=\frac{w}{40 } \times $$\frac{1000}{500} $
so, w = 4gm.
Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is 4 gm.

questions.question: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Mass percent of the solute is _______.
questions.question_raw: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Mass percent of the solute is _______.
questions.explanation:
Since , mass % =[{(mass of solute) $\times$) / (mass of solution ) } $\times$100]
$\therefore$ Substituting the given values we get,
Mass per cent
= [ ( 2 g ) / { (2 g of A ) + 18 g of water } ]
= [ ( 2 /20 ) x 100 ]
= 10%

questions.question:
Consider the equation, Fe + H_2SO_4 $\rightarrow$ Fe_2(SO_4)_3 + H_2
This equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the equation, Fe + H2SO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2(SO4)3 + H2
This equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.explanation:
A balanced equation represents the same number of atoms of each element on both sides (i.e. LHS & RHS) of the equation. The balanced equation is complied by introducing the coefficients as (2, 3, 1, 3) respectively. Thus the balanced equation for the given reaction is
2Fe + 3H_2SO_4 $\to$ Fe_2 (SO_4)_3 + 3H_2

questions.question:
Consider the chemical equation, ----- KOH + ----- H_3PO_4 $\longrightarrow$ ----- K_3PO_4 + ----- H_2O.
The equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the chemical  equation, ----- KOH + ----- H3PO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} ----- K3PO4 + ----- H2O.
The equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.explanation: The given equation gets balanced by inserting the coefficients (3,1,1,3) in blank spaces from LHS to RHS. Thus, the balanced equation for the given reaction is 3KOH + H_3PO_4 $\longrightarrow$ K_3PO_4 + 3H_2O.

questions.question:
Consider the chemical reaction gven as,
--CO (g) + -- $H_2$(g) → -- $C_8$H_18(l) + -- $H_2O$.
This equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces
questions.question_raw:
Consider the chemical reaction gven as,
--CO (g) + -- {tex}H_2{/tex}(g) → -- {tex}C_8{/tex}H18(l) + -- {tex}H_2O{/tex}.
This equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces
questions.explanation:
The balanced equation for the given equation is,
8CO(g) + 17H_2(g) $\longrightarrow$ C_8H_18(l) + 8H_2O

questions.question:
Consider the unbalanced chemical equation ,
-- Al + -- $O_2$ → -- $Al_2O_3$.
The equation for the above reaction can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the unbalanced  chemical equation ,
-- Al + -- {tex}O_2{/tex} → -- {tex}Al_2O_3{/tex}.
The equation for the above reaction can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.explanation:
The unbalanced chemical reaction is 
Al(s) + O_2​(g) $\longrightarrow$ Al_2​O_3​(s)
Balance Al atoms by adding 2 to Al on LHS
2Al(s) + O_2​(g) $\longrightarrow$ Al_2​O_3​(s)
Balance O atoms by adding $\frac 32$​ to O_2​ on LHS
2Al(s) + ​$\frac 32$O_2​(g)$\longrightarrow$ Al_2​O_3​(s)
Multiply the whole equation with 2.
4Al(s) + 3O_2​(g)$\longrightarrow$ 2Al_2​O_3​(s)

questions.question:
^-- $Ba_3N_2$ +^- $H_2O$ → ^-- Ba $(OH)_2$ + ^-- $NH_3$ .
The above equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces from LHS to RHS respectively.
questions.question_raw:
-- {tex}Ba_3N_2{/tex} +- {tex}H_2O{/tex} → -- Ba {tex}(OH)_2{/tex} + -- {tex}NH_3{/tex} .

The above equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces from LHS to RHS respectively.
questions.explanation:
The given equation gets stoichiometrically balanced when the coefficients ( 1, 6, 3, 2 ) starting from LHS to RHS are inserted in given blank spaces.
Thus, the balanced equation for the reaction is,
Ba_3N_2 + 6H_2O $\longrightarrow$ 3Ba(OH)_2 + 2NH_3

questions.question: The gram molar mass of CO_2 is:
questions.question_raw: The gram molar mass of CO2 is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of CO_2 = $\Sigma $ (atomic mass of C, 2*atomic mass of O)
= [12+2(16)] u
Since gram molar mass
= Molar mass expressed in gms.
$\therefore$ gram molar mass of CO_2
= 44 g

questions.question: The molar mass of Al_2O_3 is:
questions.question_raw: The molar mass of Al2O3 is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass
= $\Sigma$ [ 2 $\times$ (atomic mass of Al) 3 $\times$ (atomic mass of O)]u
= [(2 $\times$ 27) + (3 $\times$ 16)]u
= ( 54 + 48 ) u
= 102 u.

questions.question: The molar mass of C_6 H_10 O_5 is
questions.question_raw: The molar mass of   C6 H10 O5  is
questions.explanation:
Molar massof C_6 H_10 O_5
= Sum of the atomic masses of six C atoms , ten H atoms & six O atoms
thus , substituting the required atomic masses of
 C= 12 ,
H = 1 , &
 O = 16 ,
 we get ,
Molar mass of C_6 H_10 O_5 
 = [ 6(12)+10(1)+5(16) ] u
= (72 + 10 + 80 ) u
=162 u

questions.question: Atomicity of glucose is ________.
questions.question_raw: Atomicity of glucose is ________.
questions.explanation: Atomicity is defined as the number of atoms contained in 1 molecule of the compound. As glucose contains 6 carbon atoms, 12 hydrogen atoms, and 6 oxygen atoms, thus the atomicity is 24.

questions.question: The molar mass of AgBO_3 is
questions.question_raw: The molar mass of AgBO3 is
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of AgBO_3 = [atomic mass of Ag + atomic mass of B + 3 (atomic mass of O))] u
Substituting the atomic masses of Ag, B & O as 107.9, 10 & 16 respectively. we get -
Molar mass of AgBO_3 = [107.9 + 10 + (3 $\times $ 16)]u
= 165.9 u

questions.question: How many atoms of Oxygen are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avogadro’s Number = 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 atoms/mol)
questions.question_raw: How many atoms of Oxygen are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avogadro’s Number = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms/mol)
questions.explanation:
The problem is based upon the concepts of Avogadro's law. Accordingly, one mole (ie 18g) of water contains
= 6.02 $\times$ $10^{23}$ molecules of water
Again, since the number of oxygen atoms in one molecule of water (H_2O)
= 1 atom 
$\therefore$ the number of oxygen atoms in 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 molecules of water
= 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 atoms

questions.question: Given that Avagadro’s Number = 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 atoms/mol, the number of hydrogen atoms in 18g of water is.
questions.question_raw: Given that Avagadro’s Number = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms/mol, the number of hydrogen atoms in 18g of water is.
questions.explanation:
Since,18g H_2O
= 1mol water containing 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 molecules of water (H_2O) & the number of H atoms in 1 molecule of hydrogen (H_2) = 2
$\therefore$ the number of H atoms in 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 molecules
= (6.02 $\times$ 10^23 $\times$ 2)
=1.204 $\times$ 10^24.

questions.question: There are _____m in 2000 mm?
questions.question_raw: There are _____m  in 2000 mm?
questions.explanation:
Since 1 m = 1000 mm.
or, 1 mm = $\frac{1}{1000} m$ 
$\therefore$2000 mm = [ $\frac{1}{1000} \times 2000 $ ] m = 2m

questions.question: There are ____ L in 0.05 ml?
questions.question_raw: There are ____ L  in 0.05 ml?
questions.explanation:
Since , 1L = 1000mL 
or , 1mL = $\frac{1}{1000} L$ .
$\therefore$ 0.05mL = $\frac{1}{1000} \times 0.05 L$ = 0.00005 L .

questions.question: The molarity of NaOH in a solution prepared by dissolving its 4.0 g in enough water to form 250 mL of the solution is,
questions.question_raw: The molarity of NaOH in a solution prepared by dissolving its 4.0 g in enough water to form 250 mL of the solution is,
questions.explanation:
Since Molarity = $[ \frac { moles of solute * }{ volume of solution( mL ) } \times 1000 ] M $
$\therefore$ substituting the given values, we get -
Molarity (M) = $\frac{ 0.10 }{ 250 } \times 1000 M$ = 0.4M
* moles of solute ie. NaOH = $\frac{4}{40} $ mole
= 0.1mol

questions.question: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.question_raw: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.explanation: The occurrence of a chemical reaction is always associated with the breaking and formation of bonds of the reactant and products along with rearrangement of their atoms, hence the statement at option 1 is most appropriate i.e. " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds a chemical reaction takes place."

questions.question: We have to prepare a Litre solution of 0.2 M NaOH from the available 1M solution. What volume of 1M NaOH is required to be taken?
questions.question_raw: We have to prepare a Litre solution of 0.2 M NaOH from the available 1M solution. What volume of 1M NaOH is required to be taken?
questions.explanation:
Since the total strength of a given solution = total strength of the prepared solution
$\therefore$ Apply the relation -
M_1 $\times$ V_1 = M_2 $\times$ V_2
where , M1= molarity of solution given= 1.0M ,
V_1 = volume of solution given= ?
& M_2= molarity of solution to be prepared = 0.20 M (given)
V_2 = volume of solution to be prepared = 1000 mL (given)
Substiuting the given values we get ,
1.00M $\times$ V1 = 0.20 $\times$1000
V1= ( 0.20 $\times$ 1000)/1.00
V1 = 200 mL

questions.question: The mass of 1 molecule of O_2 is
questions.question_raw: The mass of 1 molecule of O2 is
questions.explanation:
Since, as per Avogadro's law,
1 mole of oxygen contains 6.023×10^23 molecules.
the mass of 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 molecules of O_2 = 32 g
$\therefore$ mass of 1 molecule of O_2 = $\frac{ 32 }{ 6.022 \times 10^{23}} g$ = 5.32 $\times$ 10^-23 g

questions.question: The mass of 1 mole of O_2 is _________.
questions.question_raw: The mass of 1 mole of O2 is _________.
questions.explanation:
Calculations:
Mass of 1mole of O_2
= the mass of 6.022 $\times$ 10^23 molecules of O_2 (as per Avogadro's law)
= molar mass of O_2 in grams (i.e. gram molar mss)
= 32.0 g

questions.question:
For the reaction
2C_4H_10 (g)| + 130_2 (g) $\rightarrow$ 8CO_2 (g) + 10H_20(l) 300 g of C_4H_10 is burnt in 1000 g of O_2.
The yield of H_2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction
2C4H10 (g)| + 1302 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 8CO2 (g) + 10H20(l) 300 g of C4H10 is burnt  in 1000 g of O2.
The yield  of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are
questions.explanation:
Calculations 
Moles of C_4H_10 given
= $\frac{300}{58}$
= 5.172 mol 
Moles of O_2
= $\frac{1000}{32}$ moles
= 31.25 mol.
As per stoichiometry of the reaction,
since, 2 mol of C_4H_10 requires 13 mol of O_2 
so 5.172 mol of C_4H_10 require mol of O_2
= $\frac{5.172X 13}{2} $ moles 
= 33.618 moles 
O_2 we have is only 31.25 mol which lesser amount (ie. 33.618 mol ) than what is required, and hence would be consumed first.
Thus, O_2 is is limiting reagent.
Again, 13 mol of O_2 gives 10 mol of H_2O 
$\therefore$ 31.25 mol will give$\frac{ 10 \times 31.25 }{13} $ mol of H_2 O
= 24.038 mol of water
= (24.038 $\times$ 18)g of water
$\approx$ 432 g of water.

questions.question: How many molecules of H_2O are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avagadro’s Number = 6.02 $\times$ 10^23 atoms/mol)
questions.question_raw: How many molecules of H2O are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avagadro’s Number = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms/mol)
questions.explanation:
Since, the number of molecules contained in one mole of a substance
= Avogadro's number (N)
= 6.022 $\times$ 10^23 molecules, and, gram molar mass of water = 18 g 
$\therefore$ 18g H_2O = 1mole water = 6.022 $\times$ 10^23 molecules of water

questions.question: For the reaction Fe_2O_3 + 3CO $\longrightarrow$ 2 Fe + 3 CO_2 the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction Fe2O3  + 3CO {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Fe + 3 CO2 the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is:
questions.explanation:
Based on the given equation it is clear that 1 mole of Fe_2O_3​ i.e ferric oxide is reduced by 3 carbon monoxide.
1 mole of any compound occupies 22.4l of volume. 
So, the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is = 3 $\times$ 224.4dm^3 = 67.2dm^3.

questions.question:
For the reaction
Fe_2O_3 (s) + 3 CO (g) $\rightarrow$ 2 Fe (g) + 3 CO_2,
224 g of CO is available to react with 400 g Fe_2O_3, the yield of iron and CO_2, are ----------- and ------------respectively:
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction
Fe2O3 (s) + 3 CO (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 Fe (g) + 3 CO2,
224 g of CO is available to react with 400 g Fe2O3, the yield of iron and CO2, are ----------- and ------------respectively:
questions.explanation:
Calculations :
 Convert the amounts given into number of moles , 
 Moles of CO
 = ($\frac{224}{28}$) 
 =8 moles
 Moles of Fe_2O_3
 = ( $\frac{400}{159.69}$ ) 
 = 2.50 moles.
 Stoichiometric calculations ,
 3 moles of CO is needed for 1 mole of $Fe_2O_3$
 $\therefore$ 8 moles of CO will require
 = $\frac{8X1}{3}$ = $\frac{8 \times 1}{3}$ mole
 = 2.66 mole of Fe_2O_3,
 Thus it is inferred that a lesser number of moles of Fe_2O_3 has been taken to react & hence Fe_2O_3 is limiting reagent.
 Again , 1 mole of Fe_2O_3 produces 2 mole of Fe
 $\therefore$ 2.5 mole of Fe_2O_3 will produce
 = ( 2.5 $\times$ 2 ) moles of Fe
 = 5 moles of Fe
 = ( molar mass of Fe $\times$ 5 ) g
 = $( 55.845 \times 5 ) g$
 = 279.23 g of Fe
 or $\approx$ 279 g of Fe.
 Further , 1 mole of Fe_2O_3 produces 3 mole of CO_2
 $\therefore$ 2.5 mole of Fe_2O_3 will produce
 = ( 3 $\times$ 2.5 ) moles of CO_2]
 7.5 mole of CO_2 
 $\therefore$ mass of CO_2 
 = ( 7.5 $\times$ molar mass of CO_2 ) g
 = $\ ( 7.5 \times 44 ) g$
 =330g.of CO_2

questions.question:
For the reaction
2N_2H_4(l) + N_2O_4(l) $\rightarrow$ 3N_2 (g) + 4H_2O (l),
Which is the limiting reagent for this reaction?
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction
2N2H4(l) + N2O4(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3N2 (g) + 4H2O (l),
Which is the limiting reagent for this reaction?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction,
2N_2H_4 (l) + N_2O_4 (l) $\rightarrow$ 3N_2 (g) + 4H_2O (l)
as per its stoichiometry, if only the right and exact amount of N_2O_4 is taken, the reaction may not go to completion. As such it would be consumed first & is, therefore, the limiting reagent.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2 N_2H_4 (l) + N_2O_4 (l) $\to $ 3N_2(g) + 4H_2O(l), if 160 g of N_2H_4 is mixed with 160 g N_2O_4, the yield of H_2O is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) {tex}\to {/tex} 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Calculations:
Moles of N_2H_4
=$\frac{160}{32}$ 
= 5 moles 
Moles of N_2O_4
= $\frac{160}{92}$
= 1.739 moles
As per stoichiometry of the given reaction,
2 moles of N_2H_4 require 1 mole of N_2O_4,
$\therefore$ 5 mole of N_2H_4 will need 2.5 moles of N_2O_4
. But we have only 1.73 mole of N_2O_4 so it would be consumed first.
Thus N_2O_4 is the limiting reagent for this reaction.
Again, since 1 mole of N_2O_4 yields water
= 4 mole of water
$ \therefore$ 1.73 mole of N_2O_4 will yield water
= 6.92 moles
$\equiv $ (6.92 $\times$ 18) g
= 124.56g of water

questions.question: How many atoms of hydrogen are in 67.2 L of H_2 at STP?
questions.question_raw: How many atoms of hydrogen are in 67.2 L of H2 at STP?
questions.explanation:
One mole of any gas at STP has a volume of 22.4 L.
The number of moles = $\frac {67.2} {22.4}$ = 3 moles of H_2
One mole of any substance contains Avogadro's number of atoms = 6.022 $\times$ 10^23 atoms.
So multiply the number of moles $\times$ number of atoms/mole = 1.8066 $\times$ 10^24 atoms of H_2.

questions.question: Chemistry does not play a major role in:

options.label: A

options.label: D

questions.explanation:
Chemistry does not deal with explaining superconductivity.
*In superconducting materials the characteristics of superconductivity appear when the temperature is lowered below a critical temperature.
*The onset of superconductivity is accompanied by abrupt changes in physical properties which are more related to phase transitions of the material.
These aspects of studies in properties of materials are better related to studies in the fields of Physics, even though principles involved in Chemistry & Physics go hand to hand.

questions.question: One mole is the amount of a substance that contains as many particles or entities as there are atoms in:

A mole of a substance always contains the same number of entities, no matter what the substance may be. questions.question: According to Dalton’s atomic theory
questions.question_raw: According to Dalton’s atomic theory

options.label: A
options.text: Atoms are made of electrons and protons

options.label: C
options.text: Atoms are made of electrons, neutrons and protons

options.label: D
options.text: Atoms are made of neutrons and protons

questions.explanation:
Dalton’s atomic theory stated that:
 All the matter is made of atoms which are tiny particles and indivisible.
 All the given atom of the element is identical in mass, size, shape, and in other properties.
 All different elements have different types of atoms and also different in their mass, size, shape, and in other properties.
 All the atom of an element cannot be made or destroyed.

questions.question: Cathode rays or cathode ray particles are:
questions.question_raw: Cathode rays or cathode ray particles are:
questions.explanation: Cathode rays - In 1897, British physicist J. J. Thomson showed the rays were composed of a previously unknown negatively charged particle, which was later named the electron.

questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.question_raw: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.explanation:
Total energy of the electron, E = −3.4 eV
Kinetic energy of the electron is equal to the negative of the total energy.
K = −E = − (− 3.4) = +3.4 eV
Hence, the kinetic energy of the electron in the given state is +3.4 eV.

questions.question: Charge on the electron was determined by
questions.question_raw: Charge on the electron was determined by

options.label: D
options.text: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube

questions.explanation:
In 1909, Robert Millikan and Harvey Fletcher conducted the oil drop experiment to determine the charge of an electron. They suspended tiny charged droplets of oil between two metal electrodes by balancing downward gravitational force with upward drag and electric forces.
The experiment helped earn Millikan a Nobel prize in 1923

questions.question: In the Thomson Model of Atom:
questions.question_raw: In the Thomson Model of Atom:

options.label: A
options.text: electrons swarm like flies around nucleus

options.label: C
options.text: electrons are embedded in a positively charged pudding or water melon

options.label: D
options.text: electrons move in circular orbits around nucleus

correct_option_texts: electrons are embedded in a positively charged pudding or water melon
questions.explanation:
J. J. Thomson, who discovered the electron in 1897, proposed the plum pudding model of the atom in 1904 before the discovery of the atomic nucleus in order to include the electron in the atomic model.
 In Thomson's model, the atom is composed of electrons surrounded by a soup of positive charge to balance the electrons' negative charges, like negatively charged "plums" surrounded by positively charged "pudding".
 The 1904 Thomson model was disproved by Hans Geiger's and Ernest Marsden's 1909 gold foil experiment.

questions.question: Rutherford’s $\alpha$–particle scattering experiment supported the discovery of:
questions.question_raw: Rutherford’s {tex}\alpha{/tex}–particle scattering experiment supported the discovery of:
questions.explanation:
Rutherford's model of an atom: Ernest Rutherford was interested in knowing how the electrons are arranged within an atom. Rutherford designed an experiment for this. In this experiment, fast-moving alpha ($\alpha$)-particles were made to fall on a thin gold foil.
On the basis of his experiment, Rutherford put forward the model of an atom, which had the following features:
 There is a positively charged centre in an atom called the nucleus.
 Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus. The electrons revolve around the nucleus in well-defined orbits.
 The size of the nucleus is very small as compared to the size of the atom.

questions.question: The correct expression for mass number A is:
questions.question_raw: The correct expression for mass number A is:

options.label: A
options.text: A = number of protons (Z)+ number of neutrons (n)

options.label: B
options.text: A = number of nucleons (Z) + number of neutrons (n)

options.label: C
options.text: A = number of protons (Z)+ number of electrons(n)

options.label: D
options.text: A = number of electrons (Z)+ number of positrons (n)

correct_option_texts: A = number of protons (Z)+ number of neutrons (n)
questions.explanation:
Mass number. The mass number (symbol A), also called atomic mass number or nucleon number, is the total number of protons and neutrons (together known as nucleons) in an atomic nucleus.
mass No. = no. of protons + no. of neutrons.

questions.question: Isobars are the atoms with
questions.question_raw: Isobars are the atoms with

options.label: A
options.text: same atomic number but different mass number

options.label: B
options.text: same mass number but different atomic number

options.label: C
options.text: same atomic number but different number of neutrons

options.label: D
options.text: same number of neutrons but different mass number

correct_option_texts: same mass number but different atomic number
questions.explanation: Isobars are atoms (nuclides) of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons. Correspondingly, isobars differ in atomic number (or a number of protons) but have the same mass number.

questions.question: Isotopes are the atoms with
questions.question_raw: Isotopes are the atoms with

options.label: A
options.text: same mass number but different atomic number

options.label: B
options.text: same atomic number but different number of electrons

options.label: C
options.text: same atomic number but different mass number

options.label: D
options.text: same number of neutrons but different mass number

correct_option_texts: same atomic number but different mass number
questions.explanation: The atoms of a chemical element can exist in different types. These are called isotopes. They have the same number of protons (and electrons), but different numbers of neutrons. Different isotopes of the same element have different masses.

questions.question: Photoelectric effect established that light
questions.question_raw: Photoelectric effect established that light
questions.explanation: The emission of free electrons from a metal surface when light is shone on it is called the photoemission or the photoelectric effect. This effect led to the conclusion that light is made up of packets or quantum of energy. Einstein already associated the light quantum with momentum. This strongly supported the particle nature of light and these particles were named photons. Thus, the wave-particle duality of light came into the picture. Einstein won the Nobel Prize for Physics not for his work on relativity, but for explaining the photoelectric effect.

questions.question: Emission spectrum of a material results from the material's (atom or molecules)
questions.question_raw: Emission spectrum of a material results from the material's (atom or molecules)

options.label: A
options.text: transition from normal to excited state

options.label: C
options.text: transition from excited to normal state

correct_option_texts: transition from excited to normal state
questions.explanation: The emission spectrum of a chemical element or chemical compound is the spectrum of frequencies of electromagnetic radiation emitted due to an atom or molecule making a transition from a high energy state to a lower energy state. The photon energy of the emitted photon is equal to the energy difference between the two states. There are many possible electron transitions for each atom, and each transition has a specific energy difference. This collection of different transitions, leading to different radiated wavelengths, makes up an emission spectrum.

questions.question: Absorption spectrum, seen as dark lines in an otherwise continuous spectrum, for the given material results from the material's (atom or molecules):
questions.question_raw: Absorption spectrum, seen as dark lines in an otherwise continuous spectrum, for the given material results from the material's (atom or molecules):

options.label: A
options.text: absorbing selected wavelengths from an input of continuous spectrum

options.label: B
options.text: absorbing all wavelengths from an input of continuous spectrum

options.label: C
options.text: absorbing selected wavelengths and returning to normal ground state

options.label: D
options.text: absorbing all wavelengths and returning to normal ground state

correct_option_texts: absorbing selected wavelengths from an input of continuous spectrum
questions.explanation: In an absorption spectrum, portions of a continuous spectrum (light containing all wavelengths) are missing because they have been absorbed by the medium through which the light has passed; the missing wavelengths appear as dark lines or gaps. Black lines indicating where no light gets through to the element.

questions.question: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the H_2 molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produced emit electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies. The hydrogen spectrum consists of
questions.question_raw: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the H2 molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produced emit electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies. The hydrogen spectrum consists of

options.label: A
options.text: several lines named after their discoverers

options.label: B
options.text: a continuous spectrum that reduces to zero at small wavelengths

options.label: C
options.text: a continuous spectrum that reduces to zero at large wavelengths

options.label: D
options.text: several series of lines named after their discoverers

correct_option_texts: several series of lines named after their discoverers
questions.explanation: The emission spectrum of hydrogen consists of several series of sharp emission lines in the ultraviolet (Lyman series). in the visible (Balmer series), and in the infrared (Paschen series, Brackett series, etc,) regions of the spectrum. These series are named after their discoverer.

questions.question: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n1 = 1, 2, 3... and n2 = n1 + 1, n1 +2, ... , and is the wavenumber)
questions.question_raw: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n1 = 1, 2, 3... and n2 = n1 + 1, n1 +2, ... , and is the wavenumber)
questions.explanation:
In an amazing demonstration of mathematical insight, in 1885 Balmer came up with a simple formula for predicting the wavelength of any of the lines in atomic Hydrogen in what we now know as the Balmer series.
Three years later, Rydberg generalized this so that it was possible to determine the wavelengths of any of the lines in the hydrogen emission spectrum. Rydberg suggested that all atomic spectra formed families with this pattern (he was unaware of Balmer's work).
It turns out that there are families of spectra following Rydberg's pattern, notably in the alkali metals, sodium, potassium, etc., but not with the precision the hydrogen atom lines fit the Balmer formula, and low values of n_2 predicted wavelengths that deviate considerably.
Rydberg's general equation is as follows:
$\overline \nu = {1\over \lambda}=R_h\left ({1\over n_1^2}-{1\over n_2^2}\right)$
where
 R_H is the Rydberg constant and is 109,737 cm^-1
 n_1 and n_2 are integers (whole numbers) with n_2>n_1

questions.question: Consider the ground state of Cr atom (Z = 24). The numbers of electrons with the azimuthal quantum numbers, l = 1 and 2 are, respectively:
questions.question_raw: Consider the ground state of Cr atom (Z = 24). The numbers of electrons with the azimuthal quantum numbers, l = 1 and 2 are, respectively:
questions.explanation:
Azimuthal quantum number l = 1 is for p and l = 2 is for d.
Now Cr has configuration $1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6, 3s^2, 3p^6, 3d^5, 4s^1 $
Hence there are 12, p-electrons and 5, d-electrons

questions.question: Mg^2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.question_raw: Mg2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.explanation:
Isoelectronic species refers to the elements that have the same number of electrons.
Mg^2+ is 10 electron species $(1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6)$. Its configuration is like that of Ne.
Thus it is isoelectronic with any element having 10e^- or we can say 8e^- in its valence shell.
Atomic number of Sodium (Na) is 11 after loosing one electron it became Na^+ and have 10 electrons.
Thus Mg^2+ is isoelectronic with Na^+.

options.label: D
options.text: there are no uncertainties at all

correct_option_texts: the associated uncertainties are hardly of any real consequence
questions.explanation:
The effect of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle is significant only for motion of microscopic objects, it is negligible in case of macroscopic objects. For example, we apply the concept of uncertainty to an object of mass 1 milligram.
$\Delta x \times \Delta v_x = {h\over 4\pi m}$
$={ 6.626\times 10^{−34}\over 4\times 3.1416\times 10^{−6}}≈10^{−28}m^2s^{−1}$
The value of $\Delta x\Delta v_x $is very small and insignificant in this case. Therefore, when we deal with heavier objects the associated uncertainties have no real consequences and can be neglected.

questions.question: Quantum mechanics is a theoretical science that deals with the study of the:
questions.question_raw: Quantum mechanics is a theoretical science that deals with the study of the:

options.label: A
options.text: motions of the macroscopic objects that have only particle like properties

options.label: B
options.text: motions of the macroscopic objects that do not have both observable wave-like and particle like properties

options.label: C
options.text: motions of the microscopic objects that have only particle like properties

options.label: D
options.text: motions of the microscopic objects that have both observable wave-like and particle-like properties

correct_option_texts: motions of the microscopic objects that have both observable wave-like and particle-like properties
questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics, science dealing with the behaviour of matter and light on the atomic and subatomic scale. It attempts to describe and account for the properties of molecules and atoms and their constituents—electrons, protons, neutrons, and other more esoteric particles such as quarks and gluons.

questions.question: According to the quantum-theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and ml. These are referred to respectively as:
questions.question_raw: According to the quantum-theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and ml. These are referred to respectively as:

options.label: A
options.text: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and spin quantum number.

options.label: B
options.text: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.

options.label: C
options.text: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum and spin quantum number

options.label: D
options.text: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum and Magnetic orbital quantum number.

correct_option_texts: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum and Magnetic orbital quantum number.
questions.explanation:
Quantum numbers designate specific shells, subshells, orbitals, and spins of electrons. This means that they describe completely the characteristics of an electron in an atom, i.e., they describe each unique solution to the Schrödinger equation, or the wave function, of electrons in an atom.
There are a total of four quantum numbers: the principal quantum number (n), the orbital angular momentum quantum number (l), the magnetic quantum number (m_l), and the electron spin quantum number (m_s). The principal quantum number, n, describes the energy of an electron and the most probable distance of the electron from the nucleus. In other words, it refers to the size of the orbital and the energy level an electron is placed in. The number of subshells, or ll, describes the shape of the orbital. It can also be used to determine the number of angular nodes.
The magnetic quantum number, m_l, describes the energy levels in a subshell, and m_s refers to the spin on the electron, which can either be up or down.

questions.question: According to the quantum theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and ml. The values these can take are:
questions.question_raw: According to the quantum theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and ml. The values these can take are:
questions.explanation:
n (Principal quantum number) value tell about the shell to which the electro belong, Ex, If n=1 electron belongs to the first shell (K) around the nucleus
l (Azimuthal quantum number) tell about angular momentum, and shape of the orbitals, and it designates the subshells to which the electron belongs to. For a given value of 'n', 'l' can have a value ranging from 0 to n-1. Ex, If n=2 then, the value of 'l' will be 0 and 1 (0 to n-1).
m_l ( Magnetic orbital quantum number) determines the number of preferred orientations of the orbitals in the subshell, which are defined by given 'l' value.
Ex if 'l'= 2 then m_l = 2l+1 =2*2 +1= 5 m value ie., m=+2,+1,0,-2,-1.

questions.question: Spin quantum number with two spin states of the electron represented by two arrows, $\uparrow $ (spin-up) and ↓ (spin down) was introduced to account for:
questions.question_raw: Spin quantum number with two spin states of the electron represented by two arrows, {tex}\uparrow {/tex} (spin-up) and ↓ (spin down) was introduced to account for:

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.text: the splitting of emission lines in an electric field

options.label: C
options.text: the splitting of emission lines in vacuum

options.label: D
options.text: to account for uncertainty in electron states

correct_option_texts: the splitting of emission lines in a Magnetic field
questions.explanation: In 1920, Otto Stern and Walter Gerlach designed an experiment, which unintentionally led to the discovery that electrons have their own individual, continuous spin even as they move along their orbital of an atom. The experiment mentioned above by Otto Stern and Walter Gerlach was done with silver which was put in an oven and vaporized. The result was that silver atoms formed a beam that passed through a magnetic field in which it split in two.

questions.explanation:
Probability density of finding an electron at a point within an atom, it is possible to predict the region around the nucleus where the electron can most probably be found.
${{\psi }^{2}}$ has no physical significance while $ψ^2$ represents the probability density of finding an electron.

questions.question: Due to the presence of electrons in the inner shells, the electron in the outer shell will not experience the full positive charge of the nucleus (Z_e). This is known as:

options.label: B

options.label: D

questions.explanation: Shielding effect can be defined as a reduction in the effective nuclear charge on the electron cloud, due to a difference in the attraction forces of the electrons on the nucleus. It is also referred to as the screening effect (or) atomic shielding.

questions.question: The Aufbau principle states that in the ground state of the atoms, the orbitals are filled in order of:
questions.question_raw: The Aufbau principle states that in the ground state of the atoms, the orbitals are filled in order of:
questions.explanation: The Aufbau Principle states that in the ground state of an atom, an electron enters the orbital with the lowest energy first and subsequent electrons are fed in the order of increasing energies. The word 'aufbau' in German means 'building up'. Here, it refers to the filling up of orbitals with electrons.

options.label: B

correct_option_texts: No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers
questions.explanation: The Pauli Exclusion Principle states that, in an atom or molecule, no two electrons can have the same four electronic quantum numbers. As an orbital can contain a maximum of only two electrons, the two electrons must have opposing spins.

questions.explanation: The oil drop experiment was performed by Robert A. Millikan and Harvey Fletcher in 1909 to measure the elementary electric charge (the charge of the electron). The experiment entailed observing tiny electrically charged droplets of oil located between two parallel metal surfaces, forming the plates of a capacitor.

 A high potential difference (>1200 V) is applied across the two aluminium electrodes.

questions.question: Experiments on electrical discharge through gases ultimately lead to the discovery of ________.
questions.question_raw: Experiments on electrical discharge through gases ultimately lead to the discovery of ________.
questions.explanation:
Towards the end of the 19th century, Joseph J.Thomson (1856-1940) was studying electric discharges. It was known that the discharge and the glow in the gas were due to something coming from the cathode, the negative pole of the applied high voltage. Thomson made a series of experiments to study the properties of the rays coming from the cathode. He observed that the cathode rays were deflected by both electric and magnetic fields - they were obviously electrically charged.
Thomson's result was $\frac{e}{m}=1.8\times {{10}^{-11}}$ coulombs kg
The particle that J.J.Thomson discovered in 1897, the electron, is a constituent of all the matter we are surrounded by. All atoms are made of a nucleus and electrons. He received the Nobel Prize in 1906 for the discovery of the electron, the first elementary particle.

questions.question: Television pictures result due to
questions.question_raw: Television pictures result due to

options.label: A
options.text: fluorescence of protons on the television screen

options.label: B
options.text: fluorescence of canal rays on the television screen

options.label: C
options.text: phosphorescence of protons on the television screen

options.label: D

questions.explanation:
Cathode rays (also called an electron beam or e-beam) are streams of electrons observed in vacuum tubes. If an evacuated glass tube is equipped with two electrodes and a voltage is applied, the glass behind of the positive electrode is observed to glow, due to electrons emitted from and traveling away from the cathode (the electrode connected to the negative terminal of the voltage supply).
Cathode ray tubes (CRTs) use a focused beam of electrons deflected by electric or magnetic fields to create the image in a television set.

questions.question: The mass of an electron is
questions.question_raw: The mass of an electron is
questions.explanation:
The electron is a subatomic particle, symbol e^− or $\beta^-$ , with a negative elementary electric charge.
The electron has a mass that is approximately $1\over 1836$ that of the proton.
So mass of electron = ${1\over 1836}\times 1.6726219 × 10^{-27}$ = 9.1094 $\times$ 10^-31 kg

questions.explanation:
Max Planck theorized that energy was transferred in chunks known as quanta, equal to $h
u$. The variable h is a constant equal to 6.63 $	imes$ 10^-34 J·s and the variable $
u $represents the frequency in 1/s.
This equation allows us to calculate the energy of photons, given their frequency.
If the wavelength is given, the energy can be determined by first using the wave equation$ (c = \lambda 	imes 
u) $to find the frequency, then using Planck’s equation to calculate energy.

questions.question: Wavenumber of yellow radiations having a wavelength of 5800 $\overset{\circ }{\mathop{A}}\,$ is:

Wavenumber is defined as the reciprocal of a wavelength.

where, $\lambda $ = wavelength = 5800 Å = 5.8 $\times$ 10^-7 m
So, $\bar \nu ={1\over 5.8\times 10^{-7}}= $ 1.72 $\times$ 10^6 m^-1.

questions.question: Around 10^15 Hz corresponds to the region of the electromagnetic spectrum
questions.question_raw: Around 1015 Hz corresponds to the region of the electromagnetic spectrum
questions.explanation: A frequency of $10^{15} {~Hz}$ primarily corresponds to the ultraviolet (UV) region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The UV region spans frequencies between $10^{14} {~Hz}$ and $10^{17} {~Hz}$. While visible light falls within $4 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}$ to $7.5 \times$ $10^{14} {~Hz}$, a frequency around $10^{15} {~Hz}$ is near the upper limit of visible light and is generally considered to be part of the UV spectrum. Thus, $10^{15} {~Hz}$ most accurately belongs to the ultraviolet region.

questions.question: Radio frequency region of the electromagnetic spectrum is used for broadcasting. It is
questions.question_raw: Radio frequency region of the electromagnetic spectrum is used for broadcasting. It is
questions.explanation: Radiofrequency (RF) is any of the electromagnetic wave frequencies that lie in the range extending from around 3 kHz to 300 GHz, which include those frequencies used in radio communication or radar. RF usually refers to electrical rather than mechanical oscillations.

questions.question_raw: One of the orbitals is non-directional
questions.explanation: Orbitals with subshell quantum number l = 0 are called s orbitals. All s orbitals are spherical in shape and have spherical symmetry. This means that the wave function will depend only on the distance from the nucleus and not on the direction.

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 $\times$ 10^15 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^-34 Js)?
questions.question_raw: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js)?
questions.explanation:
We know Planck's equation is
E = hv
where E is energy, h is Planck’s constant and v is frequency.
E = 6.626 $\times$ 10^-34 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^15 = 1.988 $\times$ 10^-18 J

questions.question:
Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 $\mathop A\limits^ \circ $?
(Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^-34 J)
questions.question_raw:
Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 {tex}\mathop A\limits^ \circ {/tex}?
(Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J)
questions.explanation:
Energy (E) of a photon having wavelength ($\lambda$) is given by the expression, $E={hc\over \lambda}$
where, h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^-34
c = velocity of light in vacuum = 3 $\times$10^8 m/s 
Wavelength $\lambda = 0.50 A^° = 0.50\times 10^{-10} m$ $\lambda$ = 0.50 Å = 0.50$\times$ 10^-10 m
Substituting the values in the given expression of E:
$6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^8\over 0.50\times 10^{-10}$ = 3.98 $\times$ 10^-15 J

questions.explanation: The Lyman series is a hydrogen spectral series of transitions and resulting ultraviolet emission lines of the hydrogen atom as an electron goes from n $\ge $ 2 to n = 1 (where n is the principal quantum number), the lowest energy level of the electron.

questions.question: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: _6^12C, _6^13C, _6^14C . They contain:
questions.question_raw: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: 612C, 613C, 614C . They contain:
questions.explanation:
Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but that have a different number of neutrons.
Atomic number of carbon = 6
So number of protons in each isotope = 6
Number of neutrons in $^{12}_6C$ = 12 - 6 = 6
Number of neutrons in $^{13}_6C$ = 13 - 6 = 7
Number of neutrons in $^{14}_6C$ = 14 - 6 = 8

questions.explanation:
Atomic number of iron (Fe) = 26
So, number of protons = 26
Number of protons = number of electrons
But To gain 3+ charge it should loose 3 electron. So number of electrons present = 26-3 = 23
Now atomic mass = 56 (Given).
Number of neutrons = atomic mass - number of protons = 56 - 26 = 30

atomic number O has atomic number = 8 so number of electrons in $O_2$ = 16

questions.question: In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of 600m/s with an accuracy of 0.005%. Certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is (h = 6.6 $\times$10^-34 Js)?
questions.question_raw: In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of 600m/s with an accuracy of 0.005%. Certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is (h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 Js)?
questions.explanation:
Velocity = 600m/s
Accuracy = 0.005%
so,uncertainty in the velocity = ${600 \times 5 \over 1000 }={3000\over 1000 }$= 3 m/s
$∆x $ = ?
Using the Heisenberg uncertainty equation, we have
$Δx \times Δp ={ h\over h\pi }$
$\Rightarrow \Delta x = {h\over 4\pi m \Delta v}$= $6.634 \times 10^{-34}\over 4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31 }\times 3 $ = 1.92 $\times$10^-3m

questions.question: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 is _____.
questions.question_raw: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 is _____.
questions.explanation:
Here l = 3 means the subshell is f. 
n = 4 means it is present in the 4th orbit. Finally, it is a 4f subshell.
The number of orbital in f sub shell = 2l + 1 = 2 $\times$ 3 + 1 = 7
Each orbital can accommodate 2 electrons = 7 $\times$ 2 = 14 electrons.
Therefore, the maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 (4f) is 14 electrons.

questions.question: Calculate the energy required for the process He^+(g) $\to$ He^2+(g) + e^- (Hint: The ionization energy of the lowest state, also called as the ground state is 2.18 $\times$ 10^-18 J atom^-1).
questions.question_raw: Calculate the energy required for the process He+(g) {tex}\to{/tex} He2+(g) + e- (Hint: The ionization energy of the lowest state, also called as the ground state is 2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J atom-1).
questions.explanation:
He^+ ion is a single electron species which resembles like hydrogen. Therefore, the energies of the stationary states of hydrogen-like ions are given by the expression
$E_n ={ 2.18 \times 10^{-18} }\times { Z^2 \over n^2 }$
Here n = 1 and Z = 2
$E_n = {2.18 \times 10^{-18}}\times {2^2\over 1^2}$ = 8.72 $\times$ 10^- 18J atom^-1

questions.question: The electrons are ejected from the metal surface when the light of certain frequencies strikes the surface is called ________.

questions.explanation: Electrons are ejected from the metal when the light of a certain frequency strikes the surface of a metal, This phenomenon is known as the photoelectric effect and the ejected electrons are called photoelectrons.

questions.question: Which transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer type transition from n = 4 to n = 2 of He^+ spectrum
questions.question_raw: Which transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer type transition from n = 4 to n = 2 of He+ spectrum
questions.explanation:
For He^+ ion,
We have
${1\over λ }= Z^2R­_h{[{1\over n_1^2 }-{1\over n_2^2}] } $$= 2^2R_h{ [{1\over 2^2} – {1\over 4^2}]}$$=\frac{3}{4}{{R}_{h}}$ ….(i)
Now for the hydrogen atom
${1\over λ }= R_h{ [{1\over n_1^2 }-{1\over n_2^2}] }$….(ii)
Equating equation (i) and (ii), we get
${1\over n_1^2} -{1\over n_2^2 }= {3\over 4}$
Obviously, n_1 = 1 and n_2 = 2
Hence, the transition n = 2 to n = 1 in hydrogen atom will have the same wavelength as the transition, n = 4 to n = 2 in He^+ species.

questions.question: 2 $\times$ 10^8 atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Find the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
questions.question_raw: 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Find the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
questions.explanation:
Total Length = 2.4 cm
Total number of atoms along the length = 2 $\times$ 10^8
Therefore diameter of each atom = $2.4 \over 2 × 10^8$ = 1.2 $\times$ 10^-8 cm
And radius of the atom = $ 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\over 2$ = 0.60 $\times$ 10^-8 cm 
= 6.0 $\times$ 10^-11m

questions.question: The product of the uncertainties of the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. The product of their uncertainties is always

options.label: A
options.text: equal to or greater than h/3$\pi$

options.label: B
options.text: equal to or greater than h/2$\pi$

options.label: C
options.text: equal to or greater than h/$\pi$

options.label: D
options.text: equal to or greater than h/4$\pi$.

correct_option_texts: equal to or greater than h/4$\pi$.
questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2$\pi$.
$Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}$

options.label: D
options.text: greater than or equal to h/2$\pi$

correct_option_texts: greater than or equal to h/4$\pi$.
questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values (the errors are represented by the triangle symbol in front of each property, the Greek letter "delta") has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2$\pi$.
$Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}$

questions.question: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7 % more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
questions.question_raw: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7 % more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
questions.explanation:
P + N = 81
N = P + (0.317)P
Solving these two equations we get P = 35
The atomic number of Bromine is 35.
So the symbol is ${}_{35}^{81}{\text{Br}}$

questions.question: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.18 $\times$ 10^-18 J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.question_raw: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.explanation:
The energy of first (Bohr) orbit in hydrogen atom = -2.17 $\times$ 10^-18Jatom^-1
The energy of the fifth orbit will be given by $E_n= E_1\times {Z^2\over N^2}$
 $E_5={-2.17\times 10^{-18}\over 5^2}=-8.68\times 10^{-20}Jatom^{-1}$.
The energy of the fifth orbital = $=-8.68\times 10^{-20}Jatom^{-1}$

questions.question: Give the name and an atomic number of the inert gas atom in which the total number of d-electrons is equal to the difference between the numbers of total p and total s electrons.

questions.explanation:
Kr has atomic no. 36. And in Kr total number of d-electrons is equal to the difference between the numbers of total p and total s electrons.
1s^2,2s^2,2p^6,3s^2,3p^6,3d^10,4s^2,4p^6
Total s-electrons =8
Total p-electrons =18
Difference =18−8=10 
Total d-electrons =10

Given : mass = 0.1 kg; veloctiy = 10 m/s.

$λ={h\over mv}$ where h is Planck's constant.
$\lambda = {6.626 \times 10^{-34}\over 10\times 0.1}=6.626 \times 10^{-34}$m

questions.question: Mendeleev 'Periodic Law' states that:
questions.question_raw: Mendeleev 'Periodic Law' states that:

options.label: C

questions.explanation: If the elements are arranged in the order of their increasing atomic weights, after a regular interval similar properties of elements are repeated.

questions.question: Mendeleev arranged elements in a periodic table in order of their increasing atomic weights in such a way that the elements with similar properties occupies:
questions.question_raw: Mendeleev arranged elements in a periodic table in order of their increasing atomic weights in such a way that the elements with similar properties occupies:

questions.explanation: Mendeleev arranged elements in horizontal rows and vertical columns of a table in order of their increasing atomic weights in such a way that the elements with similar properties occupied the same vertical column or group. This is because the properties of elements are repeating after a regular interval.

questions.question:
Correct option to complete the following statement is:
Mendeleev's classification of the element is based upon similarities in properties of different elements with increasing order of their atomic weights.
In cases where dissimilarities in properties of an element appeared he placed the elements with similar ______ together.
questions.question_raw:
Correct option to complete the following statement is:
Mendeleev's classification of the element is based upon similarities in properties of different elements with increasing order of their atomic weights.
In cases where dissimilarities in properties of an element appeared he placed the elements with similar ______ together.
questions.explanation:
Mendeleev believed that the properties of elements are the function of their atomic weights. He arranged elements in increasing order of their atomic weight but gave priority to the properties overweight. Wherever a dissimilarity in properties of an element occurred during arranging them in ascending order of their atomic weights, he ignored the atomic weight of the element but kept the elements with similar properties together.
Thus, he laid more stress over the similarities in the properties of different elements rather than their atomic weights.

questions.question: Mendeleev proposed that some of the elements were still undiscovered and, therefore, left several gaps in the table. He left the gap under aluminium and a gap under silicon and called these elements Eka-Aluminium and Eka-Silicon. These were later discovered and named.
questions.question_raw: Mendeleev proposed that some of the elements were still undiscovered and, therefore, left several gaps in the table. He left the gap under aluminium and a gap under silicon and called these elements Eka-Aluminium and Eka-Silicon. These were later discovered and named.
questions.explanation: Mendeleev arranged the elements according to their atomic weights. He corrected the atomic weights of a few existing elements to explain their chemical and physical behaviour. Moreover, he felt that few elements are still to be discovered, so he left empty spaces for them in his periodic table. Two such elements whose properties he predicted; and were later discovered are Gallium and Germanium respectively.

questions.question:
Complete the statement with suitable word /(s)
Modern Periodic Law is stated as:
The physical and chemical properties of different elements are periodic functions of their ______.
questions.question_raw:
Complete the statement with suitable  word /(s)
Modern Periodic Law is stated as:
The physical and chemical properties of different elements are periodic functions of their ______.
questions.explanation:
The Modern periodic law is stated as:
The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers. That is the properties of elements will be repeated after regular intervals.

questions.question: It is now recognized that the ‘Modern Periodic Law’ is essentially the consequence of:
questions.question_raw: It is now recognized that the ‘Modern Periodic Law’ is essentially the consequence of:

options.label: D
options.text: periodic variations in the element lattice.

questions.explanation: Modern Periodic Law of the element may be defined as the Physical and chemical properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers. It means, when the elements are arranged in the order of their increasing atomic numbers, it is observed that the elements of similar properties recur at regular intervals or periodically. As a result of this, the elements fall in certain groups and lead to an arrangement called the modern periodic table of elements. It must be noted that elements are arranged in the periodic table in order of atomic numbers because the atomic number is the most fundamental property of an element. The systematic arrangement of elements in the modern periodic table according to their atomic number helps in justification of isotopes of elements in one place.

questions.question: In the modern periodic table:

questions.explanation: In the modern periodic table, the elements are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic number. It contains 7 horizontal rows called periods and 18 vertical columns called groups.

questions.question: All the orbitals in the valence shell of the noble gases:

options.label: C
options.text: not completely filled by electrons and it is very easy to alter this stable arrangement by the addition or removal of electrons.

questions.explanation: These elements are called noble elements because they contain ns^2, np^6 valence shell electronic configuration. A complete octet in the outermost shell makes these elements chemically stable, and therefore they do not easily react with other elements to form compounds.
