options.label: B
options.text: The first two groups of the periodic table.

questions.explanation: The two rows of elements at the bottom of the Periodic Table, called the Lanthanoids, Ce(Z = 58) – Lu(Z = 71) and Actinoids, Th(Z = 90) – Lr (Z = 103) are characterised by the outer electronic configuration (n-2)f^1-14 (n-1)d^0-1 ns^2. The last electron added to each element is filled in f- orbital. These two series of elements are hence called the Inner- Transition Elements (f-Block Elements).

questions.question: Metalloids show the properties of:
questions.question_raw: Metalloids show the properties of:

options.label: A
options.text: properties that are characteristic of both metals and plastics.

options.label: B
options.text: properties that are characteristic of both metals and gases.

options.label: C
options.text: properties that are characteristic of both metals and nonmetals.

options.label: D
options.text: properties that are characteristic of both metals and viscous liquids.

correct_option_texts: properties that are characteristic of both metals and nonmetals.
questions.explanation: A metalloid is any chemical element which has properties in between those of metals and nonmetals, or that has a mixture of them. Typical metalloids have a metallic appearance, but they are brittle and only fair conductors of electricity. Chemically, they behave mostly as nonmetals. They can form alloys with metals. Most of their other physical and chemical properties are intermediate in nature. The six commonly recognized metalloids are boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic, antimony, and tellurium.

questions.question: The first ionisation enthalpies of Na, Mg, Al and Si are in the order:
questions.question_raw: The first ionisation enthalpies of Na, Mg, Al and Si are in the order:
questions.explanation: $\mathrm { Na } < \mathrm {Al } < \mathrm { Si } < \mathrm { Mg }$

questions.question: Ionization enthalpy increases across a period because:
questions.question_raw: Ionization enthalpy increases across a period because:

options.label: A
options.text: outermost electrons are held more and more tightly.

options.label: D
options.text: outermost electrons are not held tightly.

correct_option_texts: outermost electrons are held more and more tightly.
questions.explanation: Consider moving from lithium to fluorine across the second period, where successive electrons are added to orbitals in the same principal quantum level. The shielding of the nuclear charge by the inner core of electrons does not increase very much to compensate for the increased attraction of the electron to the nucleus. Thus, across a period, increasing nuclear charge outweighs the shielding. Consequently, the outermost electrons are held more and more tightly and the ionization enthalpy increases across a period.

questions.question: In the periodic table, Electronegativity generally:
questions.question_raw: In the periodic table, Electronegativity generally:

options.label: A
options.text: increases across a period from left to right and decreases down a group.

options.label: B
options.text: decreases across a period from left to right and decreases down a group.

options.label: C
options.text: decreases across a period from left to right and increases down a group.

options.label: D
options.text: increases across a period from left to right and increases down a group.

correct_option_texts: increases across a period from left to right and decreases down a group.
questions.explanation: The electronegativity value increases as the effective nuclear charge on the atomic nucleus increases. In a period moving from left to right, the electronegativity increases due to a decrease in atomic radii resulting in an increase in effective nuclear charge. Whereas, in a group moving from top to bottom, the electronegativity decreases because of the atomic radius increases due to the subsequent addition of shells.

questions.question: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.question_raw: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.explanation:
Neutral Cl ( Z=17) has 17 electrons and with the addition of one more electron, it becomes Cl^- anion which has 18 electrons.
Neutral Ca (Z=20) has 20 electrons and with the removal of 2 electrons, it becomes Ca^2+ cation which has 18 electrons.
Since Cl^- and Ca^2+ both has 18 electrons, so they are isoelectronic.

questions.question: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.question_raw: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.explanation: The high ionization energy of B, N, and Mg is due to the presence of stable electronic configuration in these atoms as compared to the next element in the period. For example, Be (Is^2 2s^2) has completely filled orbitals as compared to B (1s^2 2s^2 2p^1) where there is one electron in p-orbital that can easily be lost. Therefore, B has lower ionization energy than Be.

Although the effective nuclear charge increases but its effect is negligible in comparison to the effect of the increasing number of shells. Therefore the correct trend is F< Cl < Br < I.

The last electron added to each element is filled in f- orbital. These two series of elements are hence called the Inner-Transition Elements (f-Block Elements).

questions.question: One of the following options is not used for explaining atomic radius:
questions.question_raw: One of the following options is not used for explaining atomic radius:
questions.explanation: The distance from the centre of the nucleus to the outermost shell containing the electrons is called the atomic radius. There are four types that are widely used: covalent radii, metallic radii, van der Waal's radii, and ionic radii.

questions.question: The correct increasing order of atomic radii for the same element is ____ ?
questions.question_raw: The correct increasing order of atomic radii for the same element is ____ ?

options.label: A
options.text: covalent radius < Metallic radius < van der Waal's radius

options.label: B
options.text: covalent radius < crystal radius < Metallic radius

options.label: C
options.text: Metallic radius < covalent radius < van der Waal's radius

options.label: D
options.text: van der Waal's radius < Metallic radius < covalent radius

correct_option_texts: covalent radius < Metallic radius < van der Waal's radius
questions.explanation:
For the same element ,
 Metallic radius of atoms are always greater than corresponding covalent radius.
 Metallic radius is always lesser than van der Waal's radius
 Hence , the correct increasing order of atomic radii of an element is covalent < Metallic < van der Waal's radius.

questions.question: Which one of the following molecules represents the increasing order of bond length?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following molecules represents the increasing order of bond length?
questions.explanation:
The bond lengths depend upon the number and type of bonds present in a molecule. The greater the number of bonds shorter would be the bond length.
Thus, the given molecules are all diatomic molecules where,
 N_2 has the smallest bond length as it is triple bonded.
 O_2 is double-bonded so its bond length is greater than N_2.
 F_2 and Cl_2 both, though single-bonded, the bond length of Cl_2 is greater than that of F_2 due to its larger atomic size.
Hence, the correct increasing order of given molecules is N_2 < O_2 < F_2 < Cl_2.

questions.question: Choose one of the following in the increasing order of electron gain enthalpy.
questions.question_raw: Choose one of the following in the increasing order of electron gain enthalpy.
questions.explanation: In general, as we move down the group electron gain enthalpy decreases; so the expected trend should be I < Br < Cl < F but in actual the order is I < Br < F < Cl. This is due to the small size of the F atom. The valence electrons of F atom provide strong repulsion to incoming electron, hence F has unexpectedly low electron affinity.

questions.question: The increasing order of ionic radii for given species is:
questions.question_raw: The increasing order of ionic radii for given species is:
questions.explanation:
In the case of cations,
 there is a decrease in ionic radii of the resulting cation as compared to its neutral parent atom, due to loss of electron /(s)
 greater the positive charge over the cation more would be a decrease in ionic size or radius.
 In the case of anion,
 the addition of each new electron (or negative charge) results in an increase in anionic radius.
 With an increase in negative charge over anion the anionic radius also becomes larger.
 Thus, considering as above the correct answer is-
 $Al^{3+} \ {<} \ Mg^{2+} \ {<} \ Na^+ \ {<} \ F^- {<} O^{2-} \ {<} \ N^{3-}$

questions.question:
Al^(3+) < Mg^(2+) < Na^+ < F^- < O^(2-) < N^(3-)
The above can be aptly described as:
questions.question_raw:
Al(3+) < Mg(2+) < Na+ < F- < O(2-) < N(3-)
The above can be aptly described as:

options.label: A
options.text: the same number of electrons (7 electrons).

options.label: B
options.text: the same number of electrons (8 electrons).

options.label: D
options.text: the same number of electrons (9 electrons).

questions.explanation: Isoelectronic species are elements or ions that have the same or an equal number of electrons. Although isoelectronic species have the same number of electrons, they are different in their physical and chemical properties. This Al^(3+) < Mg^(2+) < Na^+ < F^- < O^(2-) < N^(3-) trend is isoelectronic species.

questions.question: The correct increasing order of radii of following species is _______?
questions.question_raw: The correct increasing order of radii of following species is  _______?
questions.explanation:
It is because 
(i) radius of anion ( anionic radius ) is greater , and
(ii) radius of cation ( cationic radius ) is lesser 
than that of its parent neutral atom.

questions.question: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.question_raw: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.explanation:
As bond order increases bond dissociation energy also increases.
Thus N_2
 The triple bonded N_2 with bond order = 3 has the highest bond dissociation energy.
 The doubly bonded O_2 with bond order = 2 has lesser bond dissociation energy than N_2
 F_2 and Cl_2 both are single bonded having the same bond order = 1 
But, the electron-electron repulsive force (e-e repulsion) is maximum in the F_2 molecule due to its small size. This accounts for the lower bond dissociation energy of F_2 as compared to Cl_2.

questions.question: The typical range of molar enthalpies for the strongest intermolecular (Hydrogen) bonds is:
questions.question_raw: The typical range of molar enthalpies for the strongest intermolecular (Hydrogen) bonds is:
questions.explanation: A hydrogen bond is an electrostatic attraction between two polar groups that occurs when a hydrogen (H) atom linked to a small-sized highly electronegative atom such as nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), or fluorine (F). Therefore unlike a covalent bond, it has very low dissociation energy with a range of molar enthalpy = (4 - 25) kJ.

tests.testPaperName: Classification of Elements and Periodicity CBSE Online Test 04
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

questions.question: In terms of period and group, where would you locate the element with Z = 114 in the modern periodic table?
questions.question_raw: In terms of period and group, where would you locate the element with Z = 114 in the modern periodic table?
questions.explanation:
Elements with atomic numbers Z = (87 - 114) are placed in the 7th period.
Thus, the element with Z= 114 (Flerovium) is placed in the 7th period and 14th group of the modern periodic table.

questions.question: For an ionic compound with the tetrahedral structure the ratio $\frac{{r^+}}{{r^{ - }}}$ lies in the range of_____.
questions.question_raw: For an ionic compound with the tetrahedral structure the ratio {tex}\frac{{r^+}}{{r^{ - }}}{/tex} lies in the range of_____.
questions.explanation: For the tetrahedral structure of an ionic solid, $\frac{{{r^+ }}}{{{r- }}}$ lies in the range of 0.225 to 0.414.

questions.question: The relative tendency of an atom in a molecule to attract the shared pair of electrons towards itself is termed as its:
questions.question_raw: The relative tendency of an atom in a molecule to attract the shared pair of electrons towards itself is termed as its:
questions.explanation: A qualitative measure of the ability of an atom in a chemical compound to attract shared electrons to itself is called electronegativity. Unlike ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy, it is not a measurable quantity. However, a number of numerical scales of electronegativity of elements viz., Pauling scale, Mulliken-Jaffe scale, Allred-Rochow scale have been developed. The electronegativity of any given element is not constant; it varies depending on the element to which it is bound.

questions.question: The order of screening effect of electrons of s, p, d and f orbitals of a given shell of an atom on its outer shell electrons is:
questions.question_raw: The order of screening effect of electrons of s, p, d and f orbitals of a given shell of an atom on its outer shell electrons is:
questions.explanation: Screening effect order follows s>p>d>f, this due to the nature of the orbitals involved. s-orbitals are least diffused and the two electrons in s-orbitals provide greatest shielding of the nuclear charge to the outermost electron. The diffused character increases in the order p>d>f. f-orbitals are so diffused that fourteen electrons inside them provide virtually no screening of the nuclear charge.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an actinoid?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not an actinoid?
questions.explanation: Terbium (Z=65) has an electronic configuration of [Xe] 4f^9 6s^2. It is a member of 4f-inner transition series which is called lanthanoid series. Rest option are all the members of 5f-inner transition series, also known as the actinoid series i.e. Curium ([Rn] 5f^7 6d^1 7s^2) ; Californium ([Rn] 5f^10 7s_2); and Uranium ([Rn] 5f^3 6d^1 7s^2).

questions.question: The electronic configuration of gadolinium (Atomic number 64) is
questions.question_raw: The electronic configuration of gadolinium (Atomic number 64) is
questions.explanation: $[ \mathrm { Xe } ] 4 \mathrm { f } ^ { 7 } 5 \mathrm { d } ^ { 1 } 6 \mathrm { s } ^ { 2 }$

questions.question: Among the alkali metals, Cesium is the most reactive because:
questions.question_raw: Among the alkali metals, Cesium is the most reactive because:

options.label: A
options.text: It is the heaviest alkali metal.

options.label: B
options.text: The outermost electron is very loosely bound than the outermost electron of the other alkali metals.

options.label: C
options.text: Its incomplete shell is nearest to the nucleus.

options.label: D
options.text: It has a single electron in the valence shell.

correct_option_texts: The outermost electron is very loosely bound than the outermost electron of the other alkali metals.
questions.explanation: Cesium has the lowest ionization energy in the alkali metal group because it has the biggest atomic size in the alkali metal family. The valence electron is most loosely held due to its bigger size, and it can easily be lost.

questions.question: The effective distance between the centre of the nucleus of an ion and the point up to which the nucleus has an influence on its electron cloud is called_____.
questions.question_raw: The effective distance between the centre of the nucleus of an ion and the point up to which the nucleus has an influence on its electron cloud is called_____.
questions.explanation:
Since 'Ionic radius' is defined as the effective distance from the centre of the nucleus of an ion up to which it has an influence on its electron cloud. The correct answer is " ionic radius ".
The term Ionic radius is a general term used for both the cations as well as anions.

questions.question: For alkali metals, which one of the following trends is INCORRECT?
questions.question_raw: For alkali metals, which one of the following trends is INCORRECT?

options.label: A
options.text: Ionization energy: Li > Na > K > Rb

options.label: C
options.text: Hydration energy: Li > Na > K > Rb

options.label: D
options.text: Atomic size: Li < Na < K < Rb

questions.explanation: The expected trend for density is Li < Na < K < Rb but the actual trend is Li < K < Na < Rb. The size of the alkali metal increase down the group but when we move from Na to K, the increase in atomic size is more than expected.

questions.question: For the four successive transition elements (Cr, Mn, Fe, and Co), the stability of + 2 oxidation state will be there in which of the following order? (At. nos. Cr = 24, Mn = 25, Fe = 26, Co = 27).
questions.question_raw: For the four successive transition elements (Cr, Mn, Fe, and Co), the stability of + 2 oxidation state will be there in which of the following order? (At. nos. Cr = 24, Mn = 25, Fe = 26, Co = 27).
questions.explanation: This is attributed to the extra stability of half-filled orbitals that is seen only in Mn^2+ (i.e. d^5). The other members have Fe (d^6); Cr (d^4) and Co (d^7) respectively.

questions.question: Generally, the first ionization energy increases throughout the period. But there are some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.question_raw: Generally, the first ionization energy increases throughout the period. But there are some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.explanation: The exception in ionization energy is due to the extra stability of fully filled and half-filled valence orbitals. It appears in the case of N and O where N is half-filled; Be and B where B has fully filled; Mg and Al where Mg has fully filled valence orbitals. No such exception appears when we move from Na to Mg.

questions.question: Where is nitrogen found in the periodic table?
questions.question_raw: Where is nitrogen found in the periodic table?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen is the seventh element on the periodic table. It is located in period 2 and group 15. It is at the top of the periodic table, between carbon and oxygen.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Metalloid?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is a Metalloid?
questions.explanation: A metalloid is any chemical element which has properties in between those of metals and nonmetals, or that has a mixture of them. The six commonly recognized metalloids are boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic, antimony, and tellurium. Antimony is capable of displacing hydrogen from water (like metal), but still, most of its chemistry is characteristic of a nonmetal.

questions.question: Metals usually used as catalysts in different reactions belong to:
questions.question_raw: Metals usually used as catalysts in different reactions belong to:
questions.explanation: Transition metals and their compounds function as catalysts either because of their ability to change oxidation state or, in the case of the metals, to adsorb other substances on to their surface and activate them in the process.

questions.question: Mg^2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.question_raw: Mg2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.explanation: Total electrons in Mg^2+ and Na^+ are same i.e. 10.

questions.question: Select the species with diamagnetic nature:
questions.question_raw: Select the species with diamagnetic nature:
questions.explanation:
${[Ni(CN]_4]}^{2-}$ is a diamagnetic species because:
 The $Ni^{2+}$ ion has the valence shell electronic configuration: $3d^8 , 4s^0$ .
 The presence of a strong field $CN^-$ ions, as a legand forces all the electrons to get paired up in 3d orbitals providing 4 pairs of paired electrons and one empty d shell. 
 The empty 3d, 3s, and two 4p orbitals undergo dsp^2 hybridization to get bonded up with [CN]^- ion. Thus, [Ni(CN)_4]^2- shows a diamagnetic nature.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristic is not correct regarding alkali metals?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following characteristic is not correct regarding alkali metals?

options.label: C
options.text: Their ions are isoelectronic with noble gas

questions.explanation: Alkali metals have the lowest ionization energy value in the respective period.

questions.question: The correct statement with regard to $H_2^ + $ and $H_2^ - $ is:
questions.question_raw: The correct statement with regard to {tex}H_2^ + {/tex} and {tex}H_2^ - {/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Bond order of both species is $\frac {1}{2}$ but $H_2^ -$​ contains one electron in the antibonding orbital which results in repulsion and decreases the stability. On the other hand, $H_2^ + $ does not contain any electron in the antibonding molecular orbital.
Hence $H_2^ + $​ is more stable.

questions.question: The magnetic moment of a transition metal ion is $\sqrt {15} $ B.M. Therefore the number of unpaired electrons present in it is______.
questions.question_raw: The magnetic moment of a transition metal ion is {tex}\sqrt {15} {/tex} B.M. Therefore the number of unpaired electrons present in it is______.
questions.explanation: The spin magnetic moment is $= \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ BM; where n is a number of unpaired electrons. When n =3; then magnetic moment with be $\sqrt{15}$ BM.

questions.question: An element with atomic number 21 is a:
questions.question_raw: An element with atomic number 21 is a:
questions.explanation:
The element is Scandium (Sc) with atomic number (Z) = 21
The electronic configuration of _21Sc s [Ar] 3d^1, 4s^2. Hence, it is a transition element.

questions.question: The electronic configuration of an element in Group 15 and period 3 of the periodic table is ______.
questions.question_raw: The electronic configuration of an element in Group 15 and period 3 of the periodic table is ______.
questions.explanation:
Since, according to the classification of elements in the modern periodic table,
The period number gives an idea of Valence shell number (n) while the group number gives an idea of Valence electrons i.e. no. of electrons short to nearest octet or duplet. 
$\therefore$ The electronic configuration of the element in the 3rd period and 15th Group is $( 1s^2 , 2s^2 2p^6 , 3s^2 3p^3 )$.

questions.question: The formula for Xenon compounds containing $\sigma \ bonds = 5 \ and \ \pi \ bonds = 3$ is
questions.question_raw: The formula for Xenon  compounds  containing  {tex}\sigma \ bonds = 5 \ and \ \pi \ bonds = 3{/tex}  is
questions.explanation: The presence of $five \ \sigma \ bonds \ and \ three \ \pi \ bonds $ in compound ${\text{Xe}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$ has been confirmed by its IR spectra.

questions.question: Dmitri Mendeleev (1834-1907) and the German chemist, Lothar Meyer (1830-1895) proposed arranging elements in:
questions.question_raw: Dmitri Mendeleev (1834-1907) and the German chemist, Lothar Meyer (1830-1895) proposed arranging elements in:

options.label: A
options.text: Decreasing order of their atomic numbers

options.label: B
options.text: Increasing order of their atomic numbers

options.label: C
options.text: Increasing order of their atomic weights

options.label: D
options.text: Decreasing order of their atomic weights

correct_option_texts: Increasing order of their atomic weights
questions.explanation: When they arranged elements in the increasing order of their atomic weights, both of them observed that the chemical and physical properties of the elements repeated after a regular interval.

questions.question: A transition metal ion exists in its highest oxidation state. It is expected to behave as:
questions.question_raw: A transition metal ion exists in its highest oxidation state. It is expected to behave as:

options.label: D
options.text: A central metal in a coordination compound

questions.explanation: The transition metal in its highest oxidation state will have no desire to further loose electrons. It will act as an oxidizing agent where it will accept electrons and is reduced in a chemical reaction.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?
 Alkali metals form covalent bonds with oxygen.
 Alkali metals form oxides that act as basic anhydrides.
 Alkali metals usually have a +1 oxidation state.
 Alkali metals have relatively low first ionization energies.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is false?

  1. Alkali metals form covalent bonds with oxygen.
  2. Alkali metals form oxides that act as basic anhydrides.
  3. Alkali metals usually have a +1 oxidation state.
  4. Alkali metals have relatively low first ionization energies.
questions.explanation: Alkali metals have lowest ionization energies in their respective periods. They prefer to loose an electron to form a cation and thereby form an ionic compound.

questions.question: Which one of the following species is smallest in its size Al^3+, Mg^2+, Na^+, Ne?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following species is smallest in its size Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, Ne?
questions.explanation:
The given species are all isoelectronic species.
 Ne though isoelectronic with other species is a neutral and inert element, so in the absence of any charge over its atom would have the largest size. 
 In the case of other cationic species;
 Greater the number of positive charges over a cation more is the effective nuclear attraction resulting in a greater reduction in the size of the cation. Therefore, out of $Al^3+ \ , \ Mg^{2+} \ , \ Na^+ \ , Ne$ 
 $Al^{3+}$ is the smallest in size.

questions.question: Which of the following will have the most negative electron gain enthalpy and which one the least negative? P, S, Cl, F.
questions.question_raw: Which of the following will have the most negative electron gain enthalpy and which one the least negative? P, S, Cl, F.
questions.explanation: Chlorine has the electronic configuration of 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5, it need one more electrons to get fully filled electronic configuration. It will accept an electron easily to acquire stability, hence it has most negative electron gain enthalpy. Whereas phosphorus has 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^3; here p-orbitals are half filled. This half filled configuration gives extra stability to phosphorus, hence it will be resistent to accept an electron. It has a positive electron affinity or in simple words energy is added to make P accept an electron.

questions.question: S-block elements comprise:
questions.question_raw: S-block elements comprise:

options.label: A
options.text: Group 4 (alkali metals) and Group 7 (alkaline earth metals)

options.label: C
options.text: Group-1 (alkali metals) and Group-2 (alkaline earth metals)

correct_option_texts: Group-1 (alkali metals) and Group-2 (alkaline earth metals)
questions.explanation: S-block elements comprise of Group 1 (alkali metals) and Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) with the general outermost electronic configuration as ns^1-2.

questions.question: It is now recognized that the ‘Modern Periodic Law’ is essentially the consequence of the:
questions.question_raw: It is now recognized that the ‘Modern Periodic Law’ is essentially the consequence of the:

options.label: B
options.text: periodic variations in the element lattice.

questions.explanation: Modern Periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are the periodic functions of their atomic numbers. It implies that the chemical and physical properties of the elements and any variations in these properties can easily be predicted via the outermost electronic configuration.

questions.question: The correct decreasing order of chemical reactivities in terms of their oxidizing properties for F, Cl, O and N is:
questions.question_raw: The correct decreasing order of chemical reactivities in terms of their oxidizing properties for F, Cl, O and N is:
questions.explanation:
All these elements are p-block elements.
The oxidizing character of elements increases from left to right across a period because of the presence of vacant d - orbitals in their valence shells. Thus we get the decreasing order of oxidizing properties as F > O > N
Again, the oxidizing character of elements decreases down the group. Thus, we get F > Cl. However, the oxidizing character of O is more than that of Cl ie. O > Cl
Hence, the correct order of chemical reactivities of F, Cl, O, N in terms of their oxidizing properties is F > O > Cl > N.

questions.question: The hybridizations of atomic orbitals of nitrogen in NO_2^+, NO_3^- and NH_4^+ respectively are:
questions.question_raw: The hybridizations of atomic orbitals of nitrogen in NO2+, NO3- and NH4+ respectively are:
questions.explanation: The types of hybrid orbitals of nitrogen in NO_2^+, NO_3^–, and NH_4^+ respectively are expected to be sp, sp^2, and sp^3.

questions.question: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:
questions.question_raw: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:

options.label: A
options.text: Atomic mass is low so they are stable.

options.label: B
options.text: Total number of electrons in the outermost shell is duplets.

options.label: C
options.text: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.

options.label: D
options.text: Noble gases form ions to complete their outermost orbitals.

correct_option_texts: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.
questions.explanation: The six noble gases (Helium, Neon, Argon, Krypton, Xenon, Radon) are found in group 18 of the periodic table. These elements were considered to be inert gases until the 1960s because their oxidation number of 0 prevents the noble gases from forming compounds readily. All noble gases have the maximum number of electrons possible in their outer shell (2 for Helium, 8 for all others), making them stable.

questions.question: Stable outer octet of electrons is achieved in chlorine atom during the formation of NaCl by:
questions.question_raw: Stable outer octet of electrons is achieved in chlorine atom during the formation of NaCl by:

options.label: A
options.text: The formation of a hybrid orbital

options.label: C
options.text: The formation of a coordinate bond

questions.explanation: Na loses its one electron forming Na^+ while Cl gains the electron forming Cl^-.

questions.question: The bond formed, as a result of the electrostatic attraction between the positive and negative ions was termed as:
questions.question_raw: The bond formed, as a result of the electrostatic attraction between the positive and negative ions was termed as:
questions.explanation: The chemical bond formed between two atoms due to the transfer of electron(s) from one atom to the other. atom is called an "ionic bond" or "electrovalent bond". In an electrovalent bond one atom loses an electron to form a positive ion and the other atom gains the electron to form a negative ion. The resulting ions are held together by electrostatic attraction.

questions.question: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:
questions.question_raw: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:

options.label: B
options.text: the number of valence electrons of the element

correct_option_texts: the number of valence electrons of the element
questions.explanation:
Gilbert N. Lewis is widely known for his use of simple symbolic representations of elements that show valence electrons as dots.
The Lewis electron-dot symbols focus on the electrons in the highest principal energy level in the atom, the valence electrons.
After all, these are the electrons that participate in chemical reactions. Lewis electron-dot symbols work well for the representative elements.

questions.question: The formation of the Cl_2 molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.question_raw: The formation of the Cl2 molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.explanation: Two chlorine atoms will each share one electron to get a full outer shell and form a stable Cl_2 molecule. By sharing the two electrons where the shells touch each chlorine atom can count 8 electrons in its outer shell. These full outer shells with their shared electrons are now stable and the Cl_2 molecule will not react further with other chlorine atoms. One pair of shared electrons form a single covalent bond.

questions.question:
The electronic configurations of three elements, A, B and C are given below.
A 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6
B 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^3
C 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5 
The molecular formula of the compound formed from B and C will be
questions.question_raw:
The electronic configurations of three elements, A, B and C are given below.
A 1s2 2s2 2p6
B 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p3
C 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p5 
The molecular formula of the compound formed from B and C will be
questions.explanation: B represents phosphorus (P) and C represents Chlorine (Cl). Thecompound formed is PCl_3 i.e., BC_3.

questions.question: 788 kJ of energy is required to separate one mole of solid NaCl into one mole of Na^+ (g) and one mole of Cl^- (g) to an infinite distance. This process explains:
questions.question_raw: 788 kJ of energy is required to separate one mole of solid NaCl into one mole of Na+ (g) and one mole of Cl- (g) to an infinite distance. This process explains:
questions.explanation: Lattice enthalpy is simply the change in Enthalpy associated with the formation of one mole of an ionic compound from its oppositely charged ions in their standard states under standard conditions.

questions.question: A qualitative measure of the stability of an ionic compound is provided by:
questions.question_raw: A qualitative measure of the stability of an ionic compound is provided by:
questions.explanation: Lattice energy is an estimate of the bond strength in ionic compounds. It is defined as the heat of formation for ions of opposite charge in the gas phase to combine into an ionic solid. The stability of the ionic bond is directly proportional to lattice energy.

questions.question: Bond lengths are lower in elements having:
questions.question_raw: Bond lengths are lower in elements having:
questions.explanation:
The bond length depends on the strength of the bond. The stronger the bond is, the shorter it will be. The triple bonds are the strongest and hence the shortest. Then comes double bonds which are of intermediate strength between the triple and single bonds. And finally, the single bonds are weaker than the other two. This way, Triple bonds are the shortest. Then comes double bonds. Finally, single bonds are the longest among the three.
The order of bond lengths is given as Triple bond < Double bond < Single bond.

questions.question: Bond angle helps us in:
questions.question_raw: Bond angle helps us in:

options.label: B
options.text: determining the reactivity of the element

options.label: C
options.text: determining the stability of the element

questions.explanation: Bond angles also contribute to the shape of a molecule. Bond angles are the angles between adjacent lines representing bonds. The bond angle can help differentiate between linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal-bipyramidal, and octahedral. The ideal bond angles are the angles that demonstrate the maximum angle where it would minimize repulsion, thus verifying the VSEPR theory.

questions.question: The amount of energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type between two atoms in a gaseous state is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The amount of energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type between two atoms in a gaseous state is called ________.
questions.explanation: Bond enthalpy also known as bond energy is the energy that is needed to break a particular bond in a gaseous compound. The unit that expresses bond enthalpy is kilojoules per mole or kJ/mol.

questions.question: H.O.H bond angle in water is:
questions.question_raw: H.O.H bond angle in water is:
questions.explanation: Due to the presence of two lone pairs on O in the H_2O bond angle reduce to 104.5^0 from 109^0.

questions.question: For N_2, bond order is:
questions.question_raw: For N2, bond order is:
questions.explanation: Bond order is the number of chemical bonds between a pair of atoms. in diatomic nitrogen N≡N, Triple covalent bond is present in nitrogen so the bond order is 3.

questions.question: N_2, CO, and NO^+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is:
questions.question_raw: N2, CO, and NO+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is:
questions.explanation:
Total number of electrons in N_2 molecule is 7+ 7= 14.
As per the formula Bonded pair of electrons N_b: $σ1s^2\ σ2s^2\ {tex}\pi$2p_y^2\ $\pi$2p_z^2\ σ 2p_x^2{/tex}
Total 10 electrons.
Anti bond pairs of electrons N_a: $σ 1s*^2\ σ 2s*^2$ Total 4 electrons.
Bond Order (B.O.) = ${N_b-N_a\over 2} ={ 10-4\over 2} = 3 $
Similarly bond order of CO And NO^+ is 3

questions.explanation: A dipole moment ​is a measurement of the separation of two opposite electrical charges. Dipole moments are a vector quantity. The magnitude is equal to the charge multiplied by the distance between the charges and the direction is from negative charge to positive charge: $\mu$ = q $\times$ r

questions.question: In NO^-_3 ion, the number of bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atom are
questions.question_raw: In NO-3 ion, the number of bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atom are
questions.explanation:
The nitrate ion is formed by the loss of the hydrogen ion, and so its structure is:
Around the central nitrogen there are 4 pairs of shared electrons, and no remaining lone pair. The original lone pair has now become a bonding pair. Two of those pairs make up a double bond. The double bond unit and the two single bonds arrange themselves as far apart as possible in a trigonal planar arrangement - exactly the same as the carbonate ion.

questions.question: The criterion of overlap of atomic orbitals is the main factor for the formation of:
questions.question_raw: The criterion of overlap of atomic orbitals is the main factor for the formation of:
questions.explanation:
The valence bond theory is introduced to describe the bonding in organic molecules. In this model, bonds are considered to form from the overlapping of two atomic orbitals on different atoms, each orbital containing a single electron.
In general, greater the overlap, stronger is the bond formed between the two atoms. Thus, according to the orbital overlap concept, atoms combine together by overlapping their orbital and thus forming a lower energy state where their valence electrons with opposite spin, pair up to form the covalent bond.

questions.question: The octet of electrons represents a particularly stable electronic arrangement. Atoms achieve a stable octet when they are linked by chemical bonds. This rule is associated with which one of the following theories?
questions.question_raw: The octet of electrons represents a particularly stable electronic arrangement. Atoms achieve a stable octet when they are linked by chemical bonds. This rule is associated with which one of the following theories?

options.label: B
options.text: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion VSEPR Theory

questions.explanation:
G.N.Lewis, proposed the octet rule to explain the valence electron sharing between atoms that resulted in a bonding type with the atoms attaining noble gas electronic configuration.
The statement is: "a bond is formed between two atoms by mutual sharing of pairs of electrons to attain a stable outer-octet of electrons for each atom involved in bonding".

questions.question: During the formation of a chemical bond:
questions.question_raw: During the formation of a chemical bond:

options.label: A
options.text: energy of the system does not change

options.label: B
options.text: electron-electron repulsion becomes more than the nucleus-electron attraction

questions.explanation: A chemical bond is a lasting attraction between atoms that enables the formation of chemical compounds. The bond may result from the electrostatic force of attraction between atoms with opposite charges, or through the sharing of electrons as in the covalent bonds. When a bond forms, electrons are attracted to the space between nuclei where the electrostatic force of attraction is greater. As the electrons fall to a position of lower potential energy, the total mechanical energy of the molecular system decreases. Part of the mechanical energy of the unbound atoms is lost when they form the molecular system with a lower total mechanical energy.

questions.question: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.explanation:
In XeF_​​​​2: Xe has 2 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, since Xe has 8 valence electrons, the no.of lone pairs = ${no. \ of \ non-bonding \ electrons\over 2}={6\over 2}=3$
 In XeO_3: Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with O (bcoz of double bond). So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.
 In XeF_4: Xe has 4 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 4 non bonding electrons and therefore 2 lone pairs.
 In XeF_6: Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.
So XeF_2 has the maximum no. of lone pairs associated with Xe.

questions.question: Among the following, the maximum covalent character is shown by the compound is:
questions.question_raw: Among the following, the maximum covalent character is shown by the compound is:
questions.explanation:
Few ionic bonds have partial covalent characteristics which were first discussed by a scientist, Kazimierz Fajans in 1923. He gave the rule to predict whether a chemical bond will be covalent or ionic which is now commonly known as Fajan’s rule. The rule can be stated as:
 Size of the ion: Smaller the size of cation, larger the size of anion, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
 Charge of Cation: Greater the charge of cation, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
 Electronic configuration- For cations with the same charge and size, the one, with (n-1)d^nns^​​​0 which is found in transition elements, has greater covalent character than the cation with ns^2np^6 electronic configuration, which is commonly found in alkali or alkaline earth metals.
Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that AlCl_​​​3 has a high covalent character.

questions.question: Which one of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.explanation: The number of electrons in Nitrogen is 7 and in oxygen is 8. Hence the number of electrons that would be present in the molecular orbitals in NO is 7 + 8 = 15. As the number of electrons is odd, all the electrons in the NO molecule cannot be paired. Hence, a single electron would be present in a $\pi$*2p orbital. Therefore NO is an odd electron species and the gas is hence paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electron.

questions.question: Hydrogen bonds are formed in many compounds e.g., H_2O, HF, NH_3. The boiling point of such compounds depends to a large extent on the strength of the hydrogen bond and the number of hydrogen bonds. The correct decreasing order of the boiling points of the above compounds is:
questions.question_raw: Hydrogen bonds are formed in many compounds e.g., H2O, HF, NH3. The boiling point of such compounds depends to a large extent on the strength of the hydrogen bond and the number of hydrogen bonds. The correct decreasing order of the boiling points of the above compounds is:
questions.explanation:
The smaller the size of the atom, the greater is the electronegativity, and hence stronger is the H−bonding. Thus, the order of strength of H-bonding is HF > H_2O > NH_3. 
But each HF molecule is linked only to two other HF molecules while each H2​O molecule is linked to four other H2​O molecules through H−bonding.
Hence, the decreasing order of boiling points is H_2​O > HF > NH_3.

questions.question: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C $ \equiv $ O in order of decreasing bond length.
questions.question_raw: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O in order of decreasing bond length.
questions.explanation: The length of the bond is determined by the number of bonded electrons (the bond order). The higher the bond order, the stronger the pull between the two atoms and the shorter the bond length. Generally, the length of the bond between two atoms is approximately the sum of the covalent radii of the two atoms. CO has a triple bond so has minimum bond length.

questions.question: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C$ \equiv $O in order of decreasing bond strength.
questions.question_raw: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C{tex} \equiv {/tex}O in order of decreasing bond strength.
questions.explanation: The more bonds between atoms, the shorter the bond is. For example, a triple bond will be shorter than a double bond. The shorter the bond is the stronger it is (higher dissociation energy). Bond order is basically the number of bonds between atoms, so a larger number bond order=more bonds=shorter bonds=stronger bonds. i.e., C$ \equiv $O > C=O > C-O.

questions.question: N_2, CO and NO^+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is :
questions.question_raw: N2, CO and NO+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is :
questions.explanation:
Total number of electrons in the N_2 molecule is 7+ 7= 14.
As per the formula Bonded pair of electrons N_b: $σ1s^2\ σ2s^2\ {tex}\pi$2p_y^2\ $\pi$2p_z^2\ σ 2p_x^2{/tex}
Total 10 electrons.
Anti bond pairs of electrons N_a: $σ 1s*^2\ σ 2s*^2$ Total 4 electrons.
Bond Order (B.O.) = ${N_b-N_a\over 2} ={ 10-4\over 2} = 3 $
Similarly, the bond order of CO and NO^+ is 3.

questions.question: Bond order of ${\text{O}}_2^ + $ and ${\text{O}}_2^ - $ are:
questions.question_raw: Bond order of {tex}{\text{O}}_2^ + {/tex} and {tex}{\text{O}}_2^ - {/tex} are:
questions.explanation:
The electronic configuration of the O_2^+ ion containing 15 electrons can be written as: $\sigma $1s^2< $\sigma $*1s^2< $\sigma $2s^2< $\sigma $*2s^2< $\sigma $2p_z^2 < ($\pi $2p_x^2= $\pi $2p_y^2) < ($\pi $*2p_x^1= $\pi $*2p_y) < $\sigma $*2p_z
The bond order can be found as:
B.O = $N_b-N_a\over 2$
N_b= Number of electrons in the bonding orbitals = 10
N_a = Number of electron in the anti-bonding orbitals = 5
B.O = ^${10-5\over 2 }= {5\over 2 }$= 2.5
The electronic configuration of the O_2^- ion containing 17 electrons can be written as: $\sigma $1s^2< $\sigma $*1s^2< $\sigma $2s^2< $\sigma $*2s^2< $\sigma $2p_z^2 < ($\pi $2p_x^2 = $\pi $2p_y^2) < ($\pi $*2p_x^2= $\pi $*2p_y^1) < σ*2p_z
The bond order can be calculated as: B.O = ${10-7\over 2} ={ 3\over 2}$= 1.5

questions.question: NaCl does not conduct electricity in the ____________ but do conduct it when melted.
questions.question_raw: NaCl does not conduct electricity in the ____________ but do conduct it when melted.
questions.explanation: The essential requirement for conducting electricity is the presence of free electrons or mobile/free ions. Sodium chloride in the solid-state is closely packed by the strong electrostatic force of attraction and the ions are immobile. Therefore as there is no mobile ions conduction of electricity is not possible. But ionic compounds in molten state ions have enough energy to break the electrostatic forces of attraction and become mobile therefore conduct electricity.

chapters.name: States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)
chapters.full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)

tests.testPaperName: States of Matter CBSE Online Test 01
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)

questions.question: Which are three important states of matter?
questions.question_raw: Which are three important states of matter?
questions.explanation:
Three phases of matter are:
 Solid - firm and stable in shape; not liquid or fluid, strongest intermolecular forces.
 Liquid - A liquid is a nearly incompressible fluid that conforms to the shape of its container but retains a (nearly) constant volume independent of pressure. Intermediate molecular forces. 
 Gas -Takes the volume of the container, weakest intermolecular forces.

questions.question: Physical behavior of the states differs greatly even though chemical behavior of the three states is identical because chemical properties depends upon?
questions.question_raw: Physical behavior of the states differs greatly even though chemical behavior of the three states is identical because chemical properties depends upon?

options.label: A
options.text: intermolecular non bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular bonding forces.

options.label: B
options.text: non bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular bonding forces.

options.label: C
options.text: intramolecular bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular Non-bonding forces.

options.label: D
options.text: covalent bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on energy supplied.

correct_option_texts: intramolecular bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular Non-bonding forces.
questions.explanation: The question is based on the understanding of how the forces operate in a molecule. It is known that chemical properties depend on intramolecular bonding forces (holding the atoms in a molecule together), whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular non-bonding force (such as different kinds of polar interactions holding the molecules together).

questions.question: The Dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have:
questions.question_raw: The Dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have:

options.label: D
options.text: an electron cloud that causes temporary dipoles.

questions.explanation: The dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have a permanent electrical dipole moment, the partial charge of one molecule get attracted to the opposite charge of another molecule.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Hydrogen bond incorrect?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements about Hydrogen bond incorrect?

options.label: A
options.text: In hydrogen bonding, the H atom becomes partially positive and is attracted to the more negative N atom.

options.label: B
options.text: In hydrogen bonding, the H atom becomes partially negative and is attracted to the more positive N atom.

options.label: C
options.text: In hydrogen bonding, the H atom becomes partially positive and is attracted to the more negative O atom.

options.label: D
options.text: In hydrogen bonding, the H atom becomes partially positive and is attracted to the more negative F atom.

correct_option_texts: In hydrogen bonding, the H atom becomes partially negative and is attracted to the more positive N atom.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen atom covalently bonded to highly electronegative atom such as N, O experience the electrostatic field of another highly electronegative atom due to which a partial positive charge is developed on H atom.

questions.question: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the:
questions.question_raw: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the:
questions.explanation: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the dispersion force (or London force).

questions.question: The average kinetic energy and thermal energy are proportional to:
questions.question_raw: The average kinetic energy and thermal energy are proportional to:
questions.explanation:
The average KE and Thermal energy are proportional to absolute temperature.
The kinetic energy is the amount of motion in the atoms and molecules of a substance. The molecules of the gas gain energy as the temperature increases as there is more motion of the molecules. As the temperature decreases the motion lessens until at absolute zero all motion (kinetic energy) stops.

questions.question: Three states of matter are the result of?
questions.question_raw: Three states of matter are the result of?

options.label: A
options.text: The balance between intermolecular forces and the intramolecular binding energy of the molecules.

options.label: B
options.text: The balance between Van Der Waals forces and the London forces.

options.label: C
options.text: The balance between intermolecular forces and the thermal energy of the molecules.

options.label: D
options.text: The balance between Hydrogen bonding forces and the London forces.

correct_option_texts: The balance between intermolecular forces and the thermal energy of the molecules.
questions.explanation: Three states of matter are the result of a balance between intermolecular forces and the thermal energy of the molecules. These two factors differentiate them from each other. For understanding, the strongest intermolecular forces are in solid than in liquid and weakest in gases.

questions.question: Gases have a much lower density than the solids and liquids because:
questions.question_raw: Gases have a much lower density than the solids and liquids because:

options.label: A
options.text: Gases do not have intermolecular forces that attract each molecule to another.

options.label: B
options.text: Gases contain lower specific enthalpy than solids.

options.label: C
options.text: Thermal energy overcomes on the attractive intermolecular forces.

options.label: D
options.text: Distance between the molecules is small compared to solids.

correct_option_texts: Thermal energy overcomes on the attractive intermolecular forces.
questions.explanation: In gases, molecules are far apart from each other, thermal energy which is a measure of freeness of motion is thus very high resulting in a very large volume for the same mass (compared to solid and liquid). Hence there density is the lowest.

questions.question: At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed amount (i.e., number of moles n) of gas is:
questions.question_raw: At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed amount (i.e., number of moles n) of gas is:

options.label: B
options.text: Varies as square of its volume.

questions.explanation:
From ideal gas equation PV = nRT,
keeping n and T fixed,
P $\alpha$ $\frac{1}{V}$. (Boyles Law)
Here, T = Temperature
P = Pressure
$\alpha$ = sign of Proportionality
V = Volume
n = number of moles of gas
R = Universal gas constant

questions.question: Charles’ law states that pressure remaining constant, the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is?
questions.question_raw: Charles’ law states that pressure remaining constant, the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is?

options.label: A
options.text: Constant irrespective of its absolute temperature.

options.label: B
options.text: Directly proportional to square of absolute temperature.

options.label: C
options.text: Directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

options.label: D
options.text: Inversely proportional to its absolute temperature.

correct_option_texts: Directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

questions.question: Gay Lussac’s law states that at constant volume, the pressure of a fixed amount of gas:
questions.question_raw: Gay Lussac’s law states that at constant volume, the pressure of a fixed amount of gas:

options.label: A
options.text: Directly proportional to the square of absolute temperature.

options.label: B
options.text: Constant irrespective of its absolute temperature.

questions.explanation:
The question is based on the statement of Gay Lussac’s law- “At constant temperature, the pressure of a given mass of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.”
Mathematically; 
$P\alpha T$ (at constant temperature)
or $\frac{P}{T}$ = constant

questions.question: According to __________ equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules.
questions.question_raw: According to __________ equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules.
questions.explanation:
This question is based on the statement of Avogadro's law that states 'equal volumes of all gases under the same conditions of temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules'.
V $\propto$ n (at constant T and P)

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct ideal gas law?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct ideal gas law?
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT is the correct Ideal Gas equation.
Here, P: Pressure
V: Volume
n: Number of Moles
R: Universal Gas Constant
T: Temperature

questions.question: CO_2 can be easily liquified and even solidified because
questions.question_raw: CO2 can be easily liquified and even solidified because

options.label: A
options.text: It has comparatively more force of attraction than other gases

options.label: C
options.text: It has weak forces of attraction

correct_option_texts: It has comparatively more force of attraction than other gases
questions.explanation: It has comparatively more force of attraction than other gases

questions.question: Dalton’s Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure exerted by the mixture of non-reactive gases is equal to the:
questions.question_raw: Dalton’s Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure exerted by the mixture of non-reactive gases is equal to the:

options.label: A
options.text: the difference in the partial pressures of individual gases.

options.label: B
options.text: sum of the partial pressures of individual gases.

options.label: C
options.text: maximum of the partial pressures of individual gases.

options.label: D
options.text: average of the partial pressures of individual gases.

correct_option_texts: sum of the partial pressures of individual gases.
questions.explanation: The Dalton Law of Partial pressures for Gases states that total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressure of individual gases.

tests.testPaperName: States of Matter CBSE Online Test 02
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)

questions.question: In the kinetic molecular theory attraction between molecules of the gas is:
questions.question_raw: In the kinetic molecular theory attraction between molecules of the gas is:

options.label: C
options.text: depends on the speed of the molecule

questions.explanation: The Kinetic Theory of Gases assumes that no intermolecular forces exert between the gas molecules.

questions.question: Real gases show deviations from ideal gas law because
questions.question_raw: Real gases show deviations from ideal gas law because

options.label: C
options.text: size of molecules cannot be measured.

questions.explanation: Real gases have intermolecular forces of interaction due to which there is a difference in the actual pressure values when compared to the ideal gases, the pressure is found to be less than the pressure of ideal gases under similar conditions, similar deviation occurs when the size of molecules is taken into account due to which volume correction is required.

questions.question: Which is the correct form of van der Waals equation?
questions.question_raw: Which is the correct form of van der Waals equation?
questions.explanation: $\left( {p + \frac{{a{n^2}}}{{{V^2}}}} \right)\left( {V - nb} \right) = nRT$ is van der Waals gas equation of real gases for 'n' moles of gas.

questions.question: The deviation from ideal behavior can be measured in terms of compressibility factor Z, which is
questions.question_raw: The deviation from ideal behavior can be measured in terms of compressibility factor Z, which is
questions.explanation: Z = $ \frac{{pV}}{{nRT}}$ is a correction factor which describes the deviation of real gas from ideal gas behaviour. It is defined as the ratio of the molar volume of a gas to the molar volume of an ideal gas at the same temperature and pressure.

questions.question: The behaviour of the gas becomes more ideal when:
questions.question_raw: The behaviour of the gas becomes more ideal when:

options.label: B
options.text: Number of moles is very low

questions.explanation: This question is based on understanding the concept of molecular interactions between a molecule of real gases which is least at low P and High T conditions. Thus under these conditions real gases behave as ideal.

questions.question: Critical temperature (T_C) of carbon dioxide is the highest temperature at which liquid carbon dioxide is observed. Above this temperature it is ________.
questions.question_raw: Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide is the highest temperature at which liquid carbon dioxide is observed. Above this temperature it is ________.
questions.explanation: T_c is the highest temperature where gas can exist in a liquid state. Above this, no liquid state exists. It is impossible to compress the gas into liquid above the critical temperature, no matter whatever amount of pressure is applied.

questions.question: Above the Critical temperature (T_C) of carbon dioxide, what happens?
questions.question_raw: Above the Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide, what happens?

options.label: B
options.text: Liquefaction of the gas is not possible.

options.label: C
options.text: Liquefaction of the gas is possible.

options.label: D
options.text: Solidification of the gas is possible.

correct_option_texts: Liquefaction of the gas is not possible.
questions.explanation: T_c is the highest temperature where gas can exist in a liquid state. Above this, no liquid state exists hence it is not possible to liquefy the gas no matter what pressure is applied.

questions.question: The three states of matter of H_2O are in equilibrium:
questions.question_raw: The three states of matter of H2O are in equilibrium:
questions.explanation:
The triple point of a substance is the temperature and pressure at which three phases (i.e. gas, liquid and solid) of that substance coexist at thermal equilibrium.
The triple point of pure water is at 0.01°C (273.16K, 32.01°F) and 4.58 mm (611.2Pa) of mercury, where all the three (i.e., solid, liquid and gas) states coexist in equilibrium.

questions.question: Surface tension decreases as the temperature is raised because increase in temperature
questions.question_raw: Surface tension decreases as the temperature is raised because increase in temperature

options.label: A
options.text: decreases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction is not changed.

options.label: B
options.text: decreases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction decreases.

options.label: C
options.text: increases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction increases.

options.label: D
options.text: increases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction decreases.

correct_option_texts: increases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction decreases.
questions.explanation: Surface tension is inversely proportional to the temperature. So surface tension decreases with rising temperature. With the rise in temperature liquids expands, the interparticle distance increases, and the kinetic energy of molecules and hence disorder decreases, so intermolecular forces decrease results in a decrease in surface tension.

questions.question: Viscous force is directly proportional to
questions.question_raw: Viscous force is directly proportional to

options.label: A
options.text: velocity gradient and inversely proportional to area.

options.label: C
options.text: area and inversely proportional to velocity gradient.

options.label: D
options.text: velocity gradient squared and inversely proportional to area.

questions.explanation:
Viscous force is directly proportional to area and velocity gradient.
We have,
${F_\upsilon } = \eta A(dV/dH)$
where A = Area
We have, dV/dH = velocity gradient
$\eta = Coefficient\;of\;viscocity$

questions.question: Water has high surface tension and high capillarity because of _______.
questions.question_raw: Water has high surface tension and high capillarity because of _______.
questions.explanation: In water molecules, due to high electronegativity difference between H and O atoms and lone pairs of electrons on oxygen atom, a greater number of hydrogen bonds formed.

questions.question: What will be the minimum pressure required to compress 500 dm^3 of air at 1 bar to 200 dm^3 at 30°C?
questions.question_raw: What will be the minimum pressure required to compress 500 dm3 of air at 1 bar to 200 dm3 at 30°C?
questions.explanation:
Using the ideal gas equation, PV = nRT,
Since T is constant, we have
P_1V_1 = P_2V_2 i.e.
500 dm^3 $\times$ 1 bar = 200 dm^3 $\times$ P_2
Which gives P_2 = 2.5 bar

questions.question: A vessel of 120 mL capacity contains a certain amount of gas at 35°C and 1.2 bar pressure. The gas is transferred to another vessel of volume 180 mL at 35°C. What would be its pressure?
questions.question_raw: A vessel of 120 mL capacity contains a certain amount of gas at 35°C and 1.2 bar pressure. The gas is transferred to another vessel of volume 180 mL at 35°C. What would be its pressure?
questions.explanation:
Case I PV = nRT; n = $ \frac{{PV}}{{RT}}$
Here P = 1.2bar; V = 120ml and T = 308K.
Here n = $ \;\frac{{1.2 \times 120}}{{R \times (308)}} = \frac{{0.46}}{R}$
CaseII PV = nRT; P = $\frac{{nRT}}{V}$
here V = 180ml T = 308K and P = $ \frac{{0.46\; \times \;R\; \times \;308}}{{R \times \;180\;}}$ = 0.8bar

questions.question: At 0°C, the density of a certain oxide of a gas at 2 bar is the same as that of dinitrogen at 5 bar. What is the molecular mass of the oxide?
questions.question_raw: At 0°C, the density of a certain oxide of a gas at 2 bar is the same as that of dinitrogen at 5 bar. What is the molecular mass of the oxide?
questions.explanation:
Using relation; PV = ($\frac{m}{M}$)RT
we have
P_1M_1 = P_2M_2
P1 = $ \frac{{{P_2}{M_2}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{5 \times 28}}{2}$ = 70 g/mol

questions.question: Pressure of 1 g of an ideal gas A at 27°C is found to be 2 bar. When 2 g of another ideal gas B is introduced in the same flask at same temperature the pressure becomes 3 bar. Relationship between their molecular masses is:
questions.question_raw: Pressure of 1 g of an ideal gas A at 27°C is found to be 2 bar. When 2 g of another ideal gas B is introduced in the same flask at same temperature the pressure becomes 3 bar. Relationship between their molecular masses is:
questions.explanation:
using realtion, PV = ($\frac{m}{M}$)RT
P of 1g an ideal gas at 27 °C = 2 bar
P of 2g another ideal gas in same flask = 3 bar
pressure due to B P_total - P_A= 3 - 2 = 1bar. Now using relation,
$P = \frac{{mRT}}{{MV}}$
2 = $ \frac{{1RT}}{{{M_A}V}}$ .... (1)
1 = $ \frac{{2RT}}{{{M_B}V}}$ .... (2)
now, (1)/(2), we get
$\frac{2}{1} = \frac{{{M_B}}}{{2{M_A}}}$
M_B = 4M_A

tests.testPaperName: States of Matter CBSE Online Test 03
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)

questions.question: The drain cleaner, Drainex contains small bits of aluminum which react with caustic soda to produce dihydrogen. What volume of dihydrogen at 20°C and one bar will be released when 0.15g of aluminum reacts?
questions.question_raw: The drain cleaner, Drainex contains small bits of aluminum which react with caustic soda to produce dihydrogen. What volume of dihydrogen at 20°C and one bar will be released when 0.15g of aluminum reacts?
questions.explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2Al + 2NaOH + 2{H_2}O}&{ \to 2NaAl{O_2} + 3{H_2}} \\ \begin{gathered} 2 \times 27 \hfill \\ 0.15g \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} 3mol \hfill \\ ? \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}$
Moles of $Al = \frac{{0.15}}{{27}} = $ 0.0055 moles
2 mole of Al produces 3 moles of H_2
so 0.0055 moles of AL will produce ${\frac{3}{2}\;}$$\times$ 0.0055 = 0.00825 moles of H_2
Now PV = nRT where n = 0.00825, P = 1 bar and T = 293K.
$V_{H_2}\;=\frac{0.00825\;\times0.0821\;\times\;293\;}{1\times0.987}=$ 0.2025dm^3 = 202.5mL

questions.question: What will be the pressure exerted by a mixture of 3.2 g of methane and 4.4 g of carbon dioxide contained in a 9 dm^3 flask at 27°C?
questions.question_raw: What will be the pressure exerted by a mixture of 3.2 g of methane and 4.4 g of carbon dioxide contained in a 9 dm3 flask at 27°C?
questions.explanation:
Acc. to Daltons Law
P = P_1 + P_2
and by applying PV = nRT
P_1 $\times$ 9 = 0.2 $\times$ RT and P_2 $\times$ 9 = 0.1 $\times$ RT where T = 300K
now using P = P_1 + P_2
P = P1 + P2 = $\frac{{(0.2 + 0.1)}}{9} \times RT = \frac{{(0.2 + 0.1)}}{9} \times \frac{{0.0821 \times 300}}{1} $= 0.82 atm 
P = 0.82 atm = 0.82 $\times$ 101325 = 83086.5 pa = 8.3 $\times$ 10^4 Pa

questions.question: What will be the pressure of the gaseous mixture when 0.5 L of ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$ at 0.8 bar and 2.0 L of dioxygen at 0.7 bar are introduced in a 1L vessel at ${\text{27}}^\circ {\text{C}}$ ?
questions.question_raw: What will be the pressure of the gaseous mixture when 0.5 L of {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} at 0.8 bar and 2.0 L of dioxygen at 0.7 bar are introduced in a 1L vessel at {tex}{\text{27}}^\circ {\text{C}}{/tex} ?

questions.question: The density of a gas is found to be 5.46 g/dm^3 at 27°C at 2 bar pressure. What will be its density at STP?
questions.question_raw: The density of a gas is found to be 5.46 g/dm3 at 27°C at 2 bar pressure. What will be its density at STP?
questions.explanation:
For the same gas at different temperature and presuure; ratio of densities is given by relation $\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}=\frac{P_1T_2}{P_2T_1}$
${\rho _1} = ?,{\rho _2} = 5.46,$ We have, P_1 = 1 bar, P_2 = 2bar, T_2 = 300K, T_1 = 273K
Putting values,$\rho_1=\frac{P_1\;T_2\;\rho_2}{P_2\;T_1}=\;\frac{1\times300\times5.46}{2\times273}=$ 3g/dm^3
$\rho_1$ = 3g/dm^3

questions.question: 34.05 mL of phosphorus vapour weighs 0.0625 g at 546°C and 0.1 bar pressure. What is the molar mass of phosphorus? (in one decimal value)
questions.question_raw: 34.05 mL of phosphorus vapour weighs 0.0625 g at 546°C and 0.1 bar pressure. What is the molar mass of phosphorus? (in one decimal value)
questions.explanation:
PV = $ \frac{{mRT}}{M}$
M = $ \frac{{mRT}}{{PV}}$
M = $\frac{{0.0625g \times 0.083bar\;d{m^3}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 819K}}{{0.1bar \times 34.05 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}d{m^3}}}$
The molar mass of phosphorus = 124.75g/mol

questions.question: A student forgot to add the reaction mixture to the round-bottomed flask at 27°C, but instead, he/she placed the flask on the flame. After a lapse of time, he realized his mistake and using a pyrometer he found the temperature of the flask was 477 °C. What fraction of air would have been expelled out?
questions.question_raw: A student forgot to add the reaction mixture to the round-bottomed flask at 27°C, but instead, he/she placed the flask on the flame. After a lapse of time, he realized his mistake and using a pyrometer he found the temperature of the flask was 477 °C. What fraction of air would have been expelled out?
questions.explanation:
Using ideal gas equation PV = nRT, at constant pressure and volume(here volume of the container remains constant as it is open to atmosphere)
n_1T_1 = n_2T_2
$\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}} = \frac{{300}}{{750}} = 0.4$
This is the fraction of air remaining in a flask, so a fraction of air expelled out = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6

questions.question: Calculate the temperature of 4.0 mol of a gas occupying 5 dm^3 at 3.32 bar. (R = 0.083 bar dm^3K^-1mol^-1).
questions.question_raw: Calculate the temperature of 4.0 mol of a gas occupying 5 dm3 at 3.32 bar. (R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1).
questions.explanation:
We have PV = nRT
or ${T = \;\frac{{PV}}{{nR}} = \;\frac{{\;3.22bar\; \times 5d{m^3}}}{{4mol\; \times 0.083bar\;d{m^3}{K^{ - 1}}\;mo{l^{ - 1}}}}}$ = 50K

questions.question: Calculate the total number of electrons present in 1.4 g of dinitrogen gas.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the total number of electrons present in 1.4 g of dinitrogen gas.
questions.explanation:
moles of ${{N_2} = \frac{{1.4}}{{28}}\; = \;}$ 0.05 mol
And 1 mole of N_2 = 3.011 $\times$ 10^22 molecules of N_2
And 1 molecule of N_2 has 14 electrons
total number of electrons in 1.4 g of N_2 = 3.011 $\times$ 10^22 $\times$ 14 = 4.2154 $\times$ 10^23

questions.question: Bose-Einstein Condensate have
questions.question_raw: Bose-Einstein Condensate have
questions.explanation: Very low kinetic energy

questions.question: The colour of vapours formed on sublimation of iodine solid is
questions.question_raw: The colour of vapours formed on sublimation of iodine solid is
questions.explanation: Purple (violet)

questions.question: The payload is defined as the difference between the mass of displaced air and the mass of the balloon. Calculate the payload when a balloon of radius 10 m, mass 100 kg is filled with helium at 1.66 bar at 27°C. (Density of air = 1.2 kg m^-3 and R = 0.083 bar dm^3K^-1mol^-1)
questions.question_raw: The payload is defined as the difference between the mass of displaced air and the mass of the balloon. Calculate the payload when a balloon of radius 10 m, mass 100 kg is filled with helium at 1.66 bar at 27°C. (Density of air = 1.2 kg m-3 and R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Volume of balloon, V = $ \frac{4}{3} \times (3.14) \times {r^3} $ = 4187 m^3
Mass of displaced air, m = V $\times $ $\rho$ = 4187 $\times$ 1.2 = 5024.4kg
Temperature, T = 300 K
Mass of He filled in balloon, m_He = $ \frac{{PVM}}{{RT}}\; = \;\frac{{1.66 \times 4187\; \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.083 \times 300}} $ = 1116.4 kg
Total Mass of filled balloon, m' = 100 + 1116.4 = 1216.4 kg
Payload = mass of displaced air - mass of balloon = 5024.04 - 1216.44 = 3807.6 kg

questions.question: Calculate the volume occupied by 8.8 g of CO_2 at 31.1°C and 1 bar pressure. R = 0.083 bar dm^3K^-1mol^-1.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the volume occupied by 8.8 g of CO2 at 31.1°C and 1 bar pressure. R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1.

questions.question: 2.9 g of a gas at 95°C occupied the same volume as 0.184 g of dihydrogen at 17°C, at the same pressure. What is the molar mass of the gas?
questions.question_raw: 2.9 g of a gas at 95°C occupied the same volume as 0.184 g of dihydrogen at 17°C, at the same pressure. What is the molar mass of the gas?
questions.explanation:
We know that, ${PV = \frac{{mRT}}{M}}$
For unknown gas, P_1V_1 = ${\frac{{{m_1}R{T_1}}}{{{M_1}}}}$
For H_2 gas, P_2V_2${ = \frac{{{m_2}R{T_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}$
Here, P_1V_1 = P_2V_2
Hence, ${\frac{{{m_1}R{T_1}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{{m_2}R{T_2}}}{{{M_2}}}\;}$
${ \Rightarrow \frac{{2.9\;g \times R \times 368K}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{0.184g\; \times R \times 290K}}{{2\;g/mol}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ Molar mass of unknown gas, M_1 = 40 g/mol

questions.question: A mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen at one bar pressure contains 20% by weight of dihydrogen. Calculate the partial pressure of dihydrogen.
questions.question_raw: A mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen at one bar pressure contains 20% by weight of dihydrogen. Calculate the partial pressure of dihydrogen.
questions.explanation:
A mixture of H_2_ and O_2 contains 20% by weight of H_2 means H_2 = 20g and O_2 = 80g
moles of hydrogen, $n_{H_2}\;=\frac{20}2$ = 10 mol
moles of oxygen, $n_{O_2} = \frac{80}{32}$ = 2.5 mol
mole fraction of hydrogen,$\begin{array}{l}{x_H}_{{}_2\;}=\frac{n_{H_2}\;}{n_{H_2}+n_{O_2}}=\frac{10}{10+2.5}=\;0.8\\\end{array}$
partial pressure of H_2, $P_{H_2}=P_{Total}\times x_{H_2}=1bar\times0.8=0.8\;bar$

questions.question: Under which of the following two conditions, a gas deviates most from the ideal behaviour?
questions.question_raw: Under which of the following two conditions, a gas deviates most from the ideal behaviour?
questions.explanation:
A gas which obeys the ideal gas equation, pV = nRT under all conditions of temperature and pressure is called an ‘ideal gas’.
However, there is no gas that obeys the ideal gas equation under all conditions of temperature and pressure. Hence, the concept of an ideal gas is only theoretical or hypothetical. The gases are found to obey the gas laws fairly well when the pressure is low or the temperature is high.
Such gases are, therefore, known as ‘real gases’. All gases are real gases. Hence, at high pressure and low temperature, a real gas deviates most from ideal behaviour.

questions.question_raw: Which of the following changes decrease the vapour pressure of water kept in a sealed vessel?

options.text: Decreasing the volume of the vessel to one-half.

questions.explanation: Adding a non-volatile solute like salt decreases the vapour pressure of water. This is because some of the solute(salt) particles occupy the position of solvent molecules (water) on the liquid surface. So escaping the tendency of molecules (water) is reduced and hence vapour pressure decreases.
