In van der Waals equation of state of the gas law, the constant b is a measure of the volume occupied by the molecules.
It gives the effective size of the gas molecules. The greater value of b indicates a larger size of the molecules and smaller compressible volume.

questions.question: Acetylene (C_2H_2), an important fuel in welding, is produced in the laboratory when calcium carbide (CaC_2) reacts with water:${\text{Ca}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}0\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ Ca}}{\left( {{\text{OH}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right)$ For a sample of acetylene collected over water, total gas pressure (adjusted to barometric pressure) is 738 torr and the volume is 543 mL. At the temperature of the gas (23°C), the vapour pressure of water is 21 torr. How many grams of acetylene are collected?
questions.question_raw: Acetylene (C2H2), an important fuel in welding, is produced in the laboratory when calcium carbide (CaC2) reacts with water:{tex}{\text{Ca}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}0\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ Ca}}{\left( {{\text{OH}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){/tex} For a sample of acetylene collected over water, total gas pressure (adjusted to barometric pressure) is 738 torr and the volume is 543 mL. At the temperature of the gas (23°C), the vapour pressure of water is 21 torr. How many grams of acetylene are collected?
questions.explanation:
The total pressure is the sum of the partial pressure due to C_2H_2(g) and H_2O(g):
P_total = 748mm of Hg = pH_2O + pC_2H_2 = (21 mm of Hg) + PC_2H_2;PC_2H_2 = (738 - 21) = 717mm of Hg
As 760 mm of Hg corresponds to 1 atm,
$P{c_2}{H_2} = \frac{{717}}{{760}}atm $ = 0.943atm.
Using the ideal gas law, pV = nRT,
The number of moles of C_2H_2 formed is: $n = \frac{{pV}}{{RT}} = \frac{{(0.943atm) \times (0.543L)}}{{(0.08206\;Latm\;{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}})\left\{ {(23 + 273)K} \right\}}} $ = 0.0207mol
The molar mass of C_2H_2 is {2 $\times$ 12(C)} + {2 $\times$ 1(H)} = 26g mol^-1
This amount, therefore, corresponds to a mass of acetylene collected
mass = number of moles $ \times $ molar mass = 0.0207mol $\times$ 26g mol^-1 = 0.540g

questions.question: A scale model of a blimp rises when it is filled with helium to a volume of 55.0 dm^3. When 1.10 mol of He is added to the blimp, the volume is 26.2 dm^3. How many more grams of He must be added to make it rise? Assume constant T and P.
questions.question_raw: A scale model of a blimp rises when it is filled with helium to a volume of 55.0 dm3. When 1.10 mol of He is added to the blimp, the volume is 26.2 dm3. How many more grams of He must be added to make it rise? Assume constant T and P.
questions.explanation:
By, Avogadro's Law; $\frac{{{V_1}}}{{{V_2}}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}}$
$\frac{{55}}{{26.2}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{1.10}}$
So, ${n_1} = \frac{{55 \times 1.10}}{{26.2}}$ = 2.309mol
n_1 = 2.309 mol.
So, $\Delta n$ = 2.309 - 1.10 = 1.209 mol of He
Therefore mass of He that should be added = (2.309 - 1.10)mol $\times$ 4.003 g/mol = 4.84g.

questions.question: Hot H_2 can reduce copper(II) oxide, forming pure metal and H_2O. What volume of H_2 at 765°C torr and 225 is needed to reduce 35.5 g of copper(II) oxide?
questions.question_raw: Hot H2 can reduce copper(II) oxide, forming pure metal and H2O. What volume of H2 at 765°C torr and 225 is needed to reduce 35.5 g of copper(II) oxide?
questions.explanation:
CuO + H_2 $\to$ Cu + H_2O
1 mol of H_2 is required to reduce 1 mol of CuO. Mole of CuO = $\frac{35.5}{79.5}$ = 0.45 mol. So, moles of H_2 = 0.45mol.
PV = nRT
Here P = 765torr, n = 0.45mol, T = 498K
so V = 18.1L

questions.question: What mass of potassium chloride forms when 5.25 L of chlorine gas at 0.950 atm and 293 K reacts with 17.0 g of potassium?
questions.question_raw: What mass of potassium chloride forms when 5.25 L of chlorine gas at 0.950 atm and 293 K reacts with 17.0 g of potassium?
questions.explanation:
K + 0.5Cl_2 $\to$ KCl 
1 mol of K reacts with 0.5 mol of Cl_2 gas. Using relation; PV = nRT where P = 0.95 atm, V = 5.25 L and T = 293K
we have, n = 0.20733 mol
So, moles of Cl_2 = 0.20733 mol
mole of K = 0.43 mol
Cl_2 is limiting reagent. 0.5 mole of Cl_2 give 1 mole of KCl.
So, 0.20733 mole will give 0.414 mole of KCl = 30.9 g

questions.question: Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science concerned with
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science concerned with

options.label: A
options.text: heat and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.

options.label: B
options.text: mass and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.

options.label: C
options.text: potential and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.

options.label: D
options.text: kinetic energy and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.

correct_option_texts: heat and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.
questions.explanation: Thermodynamics deals with heat and its transformation from one form to another. The branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy (such as mechanical, electrical, or chemical energy) and by extension of the relationships between all forms of energy.

questions.question: In a closed system, which of the following take place?
questions.question_raw: In a closed system, which of the following take place?

options.label: A
options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter out of it but not vice versa.

options.label: B
options.text: The boundaries permit the flow of matter into it but not vice versa.

options.label: C
options.text: The boundaries permit the flow of matter into or out of it.

options.label: D
options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter into or out of it.

correct_option_texts: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter into or out of it.
questions.explanation: In a closed system, there is no flow of matter from system to surrounding or vice versa. For example, a certain quantity of fluid bounded within a closed cylinder constitutes a closed system.

questions.question: In an open system, which of the following take place?
questions.question_raw: In an open system, which of the following take place?

options.label: A
options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of energy into it.

options.label: B
options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter into or out of it.

options.label: C
options.text: The boundaries permit the flow of matter into or out of it.

options.label: D
options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter but not of energy.

correct_option_texts: The boundaries permit the flow of matter into or out of it.
questions.explanation: In an open system, there is a flow of matter and energy in and out of the system or vice versa. For example, an air compressor in which the air enters at low pressure and exit at high pressure and there is energy interaction with the surrounding.

questions.question: First law of thermodynamics is a restatement of:
questions.question_raw: First law of thermodynamics is a restatement of:
questions.explanation:
It is a restatement of the law of conservation of energy.
It states that Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy (such as mechanical, electrical, or chemical energy) and by extension, of the relationships between all forms of energy.

questions.question: According to the first law of thermodynamics $\Delta$U = q + w, here what is a sign of q and w?
questions.question_raw: According to the first law of thermodynamics {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w, here what is a sign of q and w?

options.label: A
options.text: q is negative if heat is transferred from the system and w is negative if work is done by the system.

options.label: B
options.text: q is positive if heat is transferred into the system and w is positive if work is done on the system.

options.label: C
options.text: q is negative if heat is transferred into the system and w is negative if work is done on the system.

options.label: D
options.text: q is positive if heat is transferred into the system and w is positive if work is done by the system.

correct_option_texts: q is positive if heat is transferred into the system and w is positive if work is done on the system.
questions.explanation: The first law is simply the conservation of energy equation. q is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed; w is positive if work is done on the system, and negative if work is done by the system.

questions.question: If V_f is the final volume and V_i is the initial volume and ${{\text{p}}_{{\text{ex}}}}$ external pressure then the work is done can be calculated by?
questions.question_raw: If Vf is the final volume and Vi is the initial volume and {tex}{{\text{p}}_{{\text{ex}}}}{/tex} external pressure then the work is done can be calculated by?
questions.explanation:
$ - \mathop \smallint \limits_{{V_i}}^{{V_f}} {p_{ex}}dV$ = w
It is equal to the integration of change in volume with respect to the initial and final volume.

questions.question: For the isothermal reversible free expansion of an ideal gas from V_i, the initial volume to V_f, the final volume of the work done on the gas is:
questions.question_raw: For the isothermal reversible free expansion of an ideal gas from Vi, the initial volume to Vf, the final volume of the work done on the gas is:
questions.explanation:
w = $- nRTln\left( {\frac{{{V_f}}}{{{V_i}}}} \right)$
This is work done by the system in an isothermal process where
w is the work done
n is the number of moles
R is the universal gas constant 
T is the temperature in Kelvin scale

questions.question: The correct equation for the change in enthalpy is:
questions.question_raw: The correct equation for the change in enthalpy is:
questions.explanation: Enthalpy is a thermodynamic quantity equivalent to the total heat content of a system. It is equal to the internal energy of the system plus the product of pressure and volume.

questions.question: The molar specific heat capacity of a substance is the quantity of heat needed to raise the temperature of one

questions.question: Which one among the following is the correct option for right relationship between C_p and C_v for one mole of ideal gas?

For a mole of an ideal gas, The difference between C_p and C_v is always constant i.e. equals to the gas constant.
Therefore, for one mole of an ideal gas, C_p - C_V = R.

questions.question: The enthalpy change of a chemical reaction ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}$ equals (a_i and b_i are stoichiometric coefficients)?
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy change of a chemical reaction {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}{/tex} equals (ai and bi are stoichiometric coefficients)?
questions.explanation: The enthalpy of reaction (${\Delta _r}H$) = (Sum of enthalpies of products)- (sum of enthalpies of reactants) = $\sum {{a_i}{H_{(product)}}} - \sum {{b_i}{H_{(reactant)}}} $

questions.question: Standard enthalpy of fusion or molar enthalpy of fusion is:

options.text: the enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one kg of a solid substance in the standard state.

options.label: C
options.text: the enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one mole of a solid substance in the standard state.

options.label: D
options.text: the enthalpy change that accompanies vaporization of one mole of a solid substance in the standard state.

correct_option_texts: the enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one mole of a solid substance in the standard state.
questions.explanation: Molar enthalpy of fusion (always increases i.e. $\Delta$H = positive) is the amount of energy needed to change completely one mole of a substance from the solid phase to the liquid phase in the standard state.

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.text: the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in at a pressure of 2 bar and 25^o C.

options.label: C
options.text: the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one kg of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.

options.label: D
options.text: the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.

correct_option_texts: the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.
questions.explanation: The standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation (reference states) is called standard molar enthalpy of formation.

questions.question: According to Hess’s Law, if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is:
questions.question_raw: According to Hess’s Law, if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is:

options.label: A
options.text: the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.

options.label: B
options.text: the difference of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.

options.label: C
options.text: the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at 2 bar.

options.label: D
options.text: the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at 35°C.

correct_option_texts: the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.
questions.explanation: Hess’s Law states that if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.

questions.question: Enthalpy of atomization is enthalpy change on breaking:
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of atomization is enthalpy change on breaking:

options.text: one kg of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the liquid phase.

options.label: C
options.text: one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the liquid phase.

options.label: D
options.text: one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase.

questions.explanation: Enthalpy of atomization is the enthalpy change that takes place on breaking one mole of bonds completely of a compound or an element, to obtain atoms in the gas phase.

questions.question: In the case of compounds such as CH_3CH_2Cl, CH_3NO_2, etc. which of the following term is used as bond enthalpy?
questions.question_raw: In the case of compounds such as CH3CH2Cl, CH3NO2, etc. which of the following term is used as bond enthalpy?
questions.explanation: For polyatomic molecules, the term means bond enthalpy is used for the enthalpy changes associated with chemical bonds.

questions.question: Spontaneity in the context of chemical thermodynamics means
questions.question_raw: Spontaneity in the context of chemical thermodynamics means

questions.question: Entropy is a state function and measures
questions.question_raw: Entropy is a state function and measures

options.label: A

options.text: the degree of randomness or disorder in the system.

questions.explanation: Entropy is a state function and it measures the degree of randomness or disorder of a system. It is denoted by 'S'.

questions.question: Change in entropy of a reversible process can be calculated by:
questions.question_raw: Change in entropy of a reversible process can be calculated by:
questions.explanation:
$\Delta {S_{sys}} = \frac{{{q_{sys,rev}}}}{T}$. The equation can be used to calculate the change in entropy of a system of a reversible process as entropy is a state property.
Where, q_sys, rev = Heat supplied to a system at temperature T.

questions.question: If change in Gibbs energy $\Delta {\text{G}}$ is negative (< 0) at constant pressure and temperature?
questions.question_raw: If change in Gibbs energy {tex}\Delta {\text{G}}{/tex} is negative (< 0) at constant pressure and temperature?

options.label: C
options.text: The process will never take place

options.label: D
options.text: The process will rarely take place

questions.explanation: $\Delta {\text{G}}$ gives criteria for the spontaneous and non-spontaneous process at constant temperature and pressure. $\Delta {\text{G}}$ < 0 (negative) means reaction is spontaneous and $\Delta {\text{G}}$ > 0 (positive) means reaction is non spontaneous.

questions.question: Standard Gibbs energy change ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}$ is related to the equilibrium constant of the reaction as follows:
questions.question_raw: Standard Gibbs energy change {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} is related to the equilibrium constant of the reaction as follows:
questions.explanation:
${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}$ = -RTln K 
where ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}$ is standard Gibbs energy change, K is the equilibrium constant, R is the universal gas constant and T is the temperature in kelvin scale.

questions.question: A thermodynamic state function is a physical quantity whose value:
questions.question_raw: A thermodynamic state function is a physical quantity whose value:
questions.explanation: State function is one which is only dependent on the initial and final state of the system and is independent of the path by which that change has occurred.

questions.question: For an adiabatic change:
questions.question_raw: For an adiabatic change:
questions.explanation: An adiabatic process would not allow the exchange of heat between the system and surroundings, hence q = 0.

questions.question: By convention, standard enthalpy for the formation of an element in reference state is:
questions.question_raw: By convention, standard enthalpy for the formation of an element in reference state is:
questions.explanation: By convention, enthalpies of formation of all elements in their most stable state of aggregation (reference state) at 25^oC and 1atm are taken as zero.

questions.question: $\Delta {{\text{U}}^\ominus} $of combustion of methane is -X kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$. The value of $\Delta {{\text{H}}^\ominus}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta {{\text{U}}^\ominus} {/tex}of combustion of methane is -X kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}. The value of {tex}\Delta {{\text{H}}^\ominus}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
CH_4 (g) + 2O_2 (g) → CO_2 (g) + 2H_2O (l)
 We know, $\Delta {H^\ominus} = {\text{ }}\Delta {U^\ominus} + {\text{ }}\Delta {n_g}$ RT
Δn_g is negative as product water in combustion reaction is in liquid state.
$\Delta {H^\ominus} = {\text{ }}-X-{\text{ }}\Delta {n_g}RT $
This implies $\Delta H \ominus$ will be more negative than $\Delta U \ominus$
Hence, $\Delta H \ominus$ < $\Delta U \ominus$

questions.question: The enthalpy of combustion of methane, graphite and dihydrogen at 298 K are, -890.3 kJ mol^-1, -393.5 kJ mol^-1 and -285.8 kJ mol^-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of CH_4 (g) will be
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of combustion of methane, graphite and dihydrogen at 298 K are, -890.3 kJ mol-1, -393.5 kJ mol-1 and -285.8 kJ mol-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of CH4 (g) will be
questions.explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-890.3 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ...(1)
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=-393.5 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ...(2)
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+0.5 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}=-285.8 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ...(3)
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}+2\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}\right)-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$
$\triangle$H = -393.5 +2(-285.8) - (-890.3)
= -74.8 kJ/mol

questions.question: A reaction, A + B $\to$ C + D + q is found to have a positive entropy change. The reaction will be:
questions.question_raw: A reaction, A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D + q  is found to have a positive entropy change. The reaction will be:
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$G = $\Delta$H -T $\Delta$S
$\Delta$S is positive and $\Delta$H is negative as heat is liberated in the reaction.
So $\Delta$G is negative hence reaction will be spontaneous at all temperatures.

questions.question: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
questions.question_raw: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta {\text{U}}$ = q + w (1st law of thermodynamics)
q = +701J (heat is absorbed by system)
w = -394 J (work is done by system)
$\Delta {\text{U}}$ = 701- 394
= 307J

questions.question:
The reaction of cyanamide, NH_2CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and $\Delta$U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol^-1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K.
NH_2CN(s) + $\frac{3}{2}$O_2(g) + N_2(g) +CO_2(g) + H_2O(l)?
questions.question_raw:
The reaction of cyanamide, NH2CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and {tex}\Delta{/tex}U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol-1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K.
NH2CN(s) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2(g) + N2(g) +CO2(g) + H2O(l)?
questions.explanation:
Here, $\Delta$n_g = 2 - ($\frac{3}{2}$) = $\frac{1}{2}$
$\Delta$H° = $\Delta$U° + $\Delta$n_gRT
$\Delta$H° = - 742.7 kJ/mol + (0.5 mol $\times$ 8.314 $\times$ 10^-3kJ/mol $\times$ 298K)
$\Rightarrow $ $\Delta$H° = ( - 742.7 kJ + 1238.786 $\times$ 10^-3kJ) mol^-1
$\Rightarrow $ $\Delta$H° = - 741.46 kJ mol^-1

questions.question: Calculate the number of kJ of heat necessary to raise the temperature of 60.0 g of aluminium from 35^o C to 55^o C. [Molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol^-1K^-1 ]
questions.question_raw: Calculate the number of kJ of heat necessary to raise the temperature of 60.0 g of aluminium from 35o C to 55o C. [Molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol-1K-1 ]
questions.explanation:
Given, Mass of Al = 60.0g 
Molar mass = 27 g/mol
Molar heat capacity, C = 24 J mol^-1 K^-1. $\Delta$T = 55^oC - 35^oC = 20^oC or 20 K.
Heat, q = nC $\Delta$T = ${\frac{{60}}{{27}}}$ $\times$ 24 $\times$ 20 = 1066.66 J = 1.067 kJ, where n = 60.0/27 mole

questions.question:
Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0°C to the ice at -10.0^o C. ${\Delta _{{\text{fus}}}}$H = 6.03 kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$at 0°C.
 C_P[H_2O(l)] = 75.3 Jmol^-1K^-1
 C_P[H_2O(s)] = 36.8 Jmol^-1K^-1
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0°C to the ice at -10.0o C. {tex}{\Delta _{{\text{fus}}}}{/tex}H = 6.03 kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}at 0°C.

  1. CP[H2O(l)] = 75.3 Jmol-1K-1
  2. CP[H2O(s)] = 36.8 Jmol-1K-1
questions.explanation:
Water (10.0^oC) $ \to $ ice (-10.0^oC) $\Delta H = ?$
The enthalpy change for the conversion of 1 mole liquid water at 10.0^oC into 1 mole liquid water 0^oC, water (10.0^o C) $ \rightleftharpoons$ water(0^o C).
$\triangle H_1=C_p\times H_2O(l)\times\triangle T$ = -75.3 Jmol^-1 K^-1 = -753 J mol^-1 
Enthalpy of fusion, water (0^o C) $ \rightleftharpoons$ ice(0^o C) , $\Delta {H_2} = {\Delta _{fus}}H$
${l}\triangle H_2\;=\;\triangle H_{freezing}=-\triangle H_{fusion\;}$ = -6.06 kJ mol^-1 
Enthalpy change for conversion of 1 mole of ice at 0^oC to 1 mole of ice at 10^oC, Ice (0^o C) $\rightleftharpoons$ ice(10.0^o C); $\Delta {H_3} = {C_p}[{H_2}O(s)]\Delta T$ = -36.8 Jmol^-1K^-1 x 10K =-368 J mol^-1
$\Delta H = {\text{ }}\Delta {H_1} + {\text{ }}\Delta {H_2} + {\text{ }}\Delta {H_3}$= - (0.753 + 6.03 + 0.368)kJ mol^-1 = -7.151 kJmol^-1

questions.question: Enthalpy of combustion of carbon to CO_2 is 393.5 kJ mol^-1. Calculate the heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO_2 from carbon and dioxygen gas.
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of combustion of carbon to CO2 is 393.5 kJ mol-1. Calculate the heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO2 from carbon and dioxygen gas.
questions.explanation:
When 1 mole of CO_2 is produced energy released is –393.5 kJ mol^-^1. Moles of CO_2 given = $\frac{35.2}{44}$ = 0.8 moles
So energy released = 0.8 $\times$ -393.5 kJ/mol = -315 kJ/mol

questions.question: Enthalpies of formation of CO(g), CO_2(g), N_2O(g), and N_2O_4(g) are -110, –393, 81, and 9.7 kJ mol^-1 respectively. Find the value of ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}$ for the reaction:${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3CO}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
questions.question_raw: Enthalpies of formation of CO(g), CO2(g), N2O(g), and N2O4(g) are -110, –393, 81, and 9.7 kJ mol-1 respectively. Find the value of {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}{/tex} for the reaction:{tex}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3CO}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Heat of reaction, $\Delta_r$H = ${\textstyle\sum_{}}\triangle_rH_{products}-{\textstyle\sum_{}}{\textstyle{\scriptstyle\triangle}_r}{\textstyle{\scriptstyle H}_{reactants}}{\textstyle\;}{\textstyle\;}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\Delta_{r} H$ = [$\Delta_{f} H$(N_2O) + 3$\Delta_{f} H$(CO_2)] - [$\Delta_{f} H$(N_2O_4) + 3$\Delta_{f} H$(CO)]
$\Rightarrow$ $\Delta_{r} H$ = [81 + {3 $\times$ (-393)}] - 99.7 + {3 $\times$ (-110)}] kJ
$\Rightarrow$ $\Delta_{r} H$ = -777.7 kJ $\approx$ -778 kJ

questions.question: Given N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) $\to$ NH_3(g); $\Delta_r$H° = -92.4 kJ mol^-1. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH_3 gas?
questions.question_raw: Given N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} NH3(g); {tex}\Delta_r{/tex}H° = -92.4 kJ mol-1. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 gas?
questions.explanation:
Given, N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) $\to$ 2NH_3(g) ; $\Delta_r$H° = –92.4 kJ mol^-1.
Chemical reaction for the enthalpy of formation of NH_3 (g) is as follows:
${\frac{1}{2}{N_{2\;}}(g)\; + \frac{3}{2}{H_2}(g)\; \to N{H_3}(g)}$
Therefore, ${{\Delta _f}{H^\circ } = \frac{{ - 92.4}}{2} = }$ - 46.2 kJ/mol

questions.question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH_3OH(l) from the following data:
CH_3OH (1) + O_2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO_2(g) + 2H_2O (1); $\Delta_rH^o$ = -726 kJ mol^-1 
C(graphite) + O_2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO_2(g); $\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}$ = -393 kJ mol^-1
H_2(g + O_2(g) $\rightarrow$ H_2O (1); $\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}$ = -286 kJ mol^-1
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH3OH(l) from the following data:
CH3OH (1) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O (1); {tex}\Delta_rH^o{/tex} = -726 kJ mol-1 
C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}{/tex} = -393 kJ mol-1
H2(g + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O (1); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}{/tex} = -286 kJ mol-1
questions.explanation:
Required reaction for the formation of methanol is:
C(s) + 2H_2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}{O_2}$ (g) $\to$ CH_3OH(l) $\Delta_f$H^o = ?
Enthalpy for the combustion of methanol;
 CH_3OH (l) + $\frac{3}{2}{O_2}$(g) $\to$ CO_2(g) + 2H_2O (l) $\Delta_f$H^o = - 726 kJ mol^-1 ...(1)
 Enthalpy for the formation of 1 mole of CO_2(g)
 C(s) + O_2 (g) $\to$ CO_2(g) $\Delta_f$H^o = - 393 kJ mol^-1 ...(2)
 Enthalpy for the formation of 2 moles of H_2O(l)
 H_2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}{O_2}$ (g) $\to$ H_2O(l) $\Delta_f$H^o = - 286 kJ mol^-1 ...(3)
Now, [eq...(3) $\times$ 2 + eq ...(2) + [ by reversing equation(1)] we get]
C(s) + 2H_2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}{O_2}$ (g) $\to$ CH_3OH (l) $\Delta_f$H^o = - 239 kJ mol^-1

questions.question:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the process ${\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ C }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4 Cl }}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$and calculate the bond enthalpy of C--Cl in ${\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}$ (g).
${\Delta _{{\text{vap}}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 3}}0.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$.
${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ }} - {\text{135}}.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$.
${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {\text{C}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 715}}.0{\text{ kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$, where ${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}$ is enthalpy of atomisation
${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right)$= 242 kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the process {tex}{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ C }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4 Cl }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}and calculate the bond enthalpy of C--Cl in {tex}{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex} (g).
{tex}{\Delta _{{\text{vap}}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 3}}0.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}.
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ }} - {\text{135}}.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}.
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {\text{C}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 715}}.0{\text{ kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}, where {tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}{/tex} is enthalpy of atomisation
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right){/tex}= 242 kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{l})$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-135.5 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ....(1)
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{0}(\mathrm{C})=715.0 $KJ mol^-1 ......(2)
$\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g})$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)=242 \ {{kJ}} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ .....(3)
$\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(1) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{g})$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{vap}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=30.5 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ....(4)
$\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{l})$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{vap}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-30.5 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ....(5)
Equation (1) - (2) - 2(3) + (4)
So For CCl_4 (g) → C (g) + 4 Cl (g) reaction ΔH^0 = -( -135.5 - 715 - 2(242) + 30.5) = 1304 KJ/mol
Bond enthalpy of C—Cl bond will be 1304/4 KJ/mol = 326KJ/mol

questions.question: For an isolated system, $\Delta {\text{U}}$ = 0, what will be $\Delta {\text{S}}$?
questions.question_raw: For an isolated system, {tex}\Delta {\text{U}}{/tex} = 0, what will be {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex}?

options.label: A
options.text: $\Delta {\text{S}}$ will increase for some time and then reduce

questions.explanation:
For an isolated system, $\begin{array}{l}\triangle U=\;0\\\\\\\end{array}$and for a spontaneous process, Total entropy change must be positive.
Moreover, $\begin{array}{l}\triangle S=\;\frac{q_{rev}}T=\frac{\triangle H}T=\;\frac{\triangle U\;+p\triangle V}T=\;\frac{0+p\triangle V}T\\i.e.\;T\triangle S>0\;\\Or\;\;\;\triangle S>0\\\\\\\end{array}$

questions.question: For the reaction at 298 K, 2A + B $\to$ C, $\Delta$H = 400 kJ mol^-1 and $\Delta {\text{S}}$ = 0.2 kJ K^-1mol^-1. At what temperature will the reaction become spontaneous considering $\Delta {\text{H}}$ and $\Delta {\text{S}}$ to be constant over the temperature range.
questions.question_raw: For the reaction at 298 K, 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 400 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex} = 0.2 kJ K-1mol-1. At what temperature will the reaction become spontaneous considering {tex}\Delta {\text{H}}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex} to be constant over the temperature range.
questions.explanation:
Gibbs free energy, $\Delta$G = $\Delta$H - T$\Delta$S
At equilibrium $\Delta$G = 0; then T= $\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$ = 2000K
Therefore, above 2000K, the reaction will be spontaneous.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) $\rightarrow$ Cl_2(g), what are the signs of $\Delta {\text{H}}$ and $\Delta {\text{S}}$?
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cl2(g), what are the signs of {tex}\Delta {\text{H}}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation: As in this case bond formation between two Cl atom is taking place so energy will be released so $\Delta$H < 0 and since 2 atoms are combining to form 1 molecule randomness decreases so $\Delta$S < 0.

questions.question: For the reaction 2A(g) + B(g) $\to$ 2D(g) $\Delta {U^0}$ = - 10.5kJ and $\Delta {S^0}$ = - 44.1J K^-1. Calculate $\Delta {{\text{G}}^0}$ for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously?
questions.question_raw: For the reaction 2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2D(g)  {tex}\Delta {U^0}{/tex} = - 10.5kJ and {tex}\Delta {S^0}{/tex} = - 44.1J K-1. Calculate {tex}\Delta {{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously?

options.label: B
options.text: 0.225 kJ, the reaction is not spontaneous

options.label: C
options.text: 0.165 kJ, the reaction is not spontaneous

correct_option_texts: 0.165 kJ, the reaction is not spontaneous
questions.explanation:
$\Delta {G^0} = \Delta {H^0} - T\Delta {S^0}$
and $\Delta {H^0} = \Delta {U^0} + \Delta {n_g}RT$
Here $\Delta {n_g} = - 1$,
$\Delta$H° = - 10.5 kJ + (-1 $\times$ 8.314 $\times$ 10^-3 kJ K^-1mol^-1 $\times$ 298K = - 10.5 (-2.477) kJ mol^-1
$\Delta$H° = - 12.977 kJ mol^-1
Now, $\Delta$G° = - 12.977 - (298 $\times$ 44.1 $\times$ 10^-3) = - 12.977 + 13.14 = + 0.165 kJ mol^-1
The reaction will not occur spontaneously because $\Delta$G°is positive.

questions.question: The equilibrium constant for a reaction is 10. What will be the value of $\Delta G^0$? R = 8.314 JK^-1, T= 300 K, T = 300 K?
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constant for a reaction is 10. What will be the value of {tex}\Delta G^0{/tex}? R = 8.314 JK-1, T= 300 K, T = 300 K?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$_rG^0 = -RTln K_c.
$\Delta G^0$ = -2.303 $\times$ 8.314 $\times$ 300 $\times$ log10 = -5744.14 Jmol^-1 = -5.74414 kJmol^-1

questions.question: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature T = 298 K from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.question_raw: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature T = 298 K from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.explanation: $\begin{array}{l}Work\;done\;for\;an\;isothermal\;process,\;w=-2.303nRT\;\log\frac{P_1}{P_2}=-2.303\times5\times8.314\times298\times\log\frac{10}1=-2.303\times5\times8.314\times298\times1=-28529.24J=-28.5kJ\\Acoording\;to\;equation\;of\;first\;law\;of\;thermodynamics,\;\triangle U=\;q+w\\For\;isothermal\;process,\triangle U=0;\;\\\Rightarrow Heat,\;q=-w=-(-28.5\;kJ)=28.5\;kJ\\\end{array}$

questions.question: Thermodynamics is not concerned about
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is not concerned about

options.label: A
options.text: the extent to which a chemical reaction proceeds.

options.label: B
options.text: the feasibility of a chemical reaction.

options.label: C
options.text: the rate at which a reaction proceeds.

options.label: D
options.text: energy changes involved in a chemical reaction.

correct_option_texts: the rate at which a reaction proceeds.
questions.explanation: Thermodynamics tells whether a reaction will take place or not. It doesn't tell about the rate (speed) of reaction.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is correct?
 The presence of reactants in a closed vessel made up of copper is an example of a closed system.
 There is an exchange of energy as well as a matter between the system and the surroundings in a closed system.
 The presence of reacting species in a covered beaker is an example of an open system.
 The presence of reactants in a thermos flask or any other closed insulated vessel is an example of a closed system.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is correct?

  1. The presence of reactants in a closed vessel made up of copper is an example of a closed system.
  2. There is an exchange of energy as well as a matter between the system and the surroundings in a closed system.
  3. The presence of reacting species in a covered beaker is an example of an open system.
  4. The presence of reactants in a thermos flask or any other closed insulated vessel is an example of a closed system.
questions.explanation: In a closed system, there is no exchange of matter with the surroundings, but there can be an exchange of energy (typically in the form of heat). In this case, a closed insulated vessel like a thermos flask prevents both the exchange of matter and minimizes the exchange of energy with the surroundings, making it a closed system.

questions.question: During complete combustion of one mole of butane, 2658 kJ of heat is released. The thermochemical reaction for above change is
questions.question_raw: During complete combustion of one mole of butane, 2658 kJ of heat is released. The thermochemical reaction for above change is
questions.explanation:
Enthapy of combustion is the energy released when 1mole of a hydrocarbon (butane ) reacts completely in presence of excess of oxygen. The chemical equation for exothermic reaction for combustion of one mole of butane is represented as;
$\begin{gathered} 2{C_4}{H_{10}}\left( g \right) + 13{O_2}\left( g \right) \to 8C{O_2}\left( g \right) + 10{H_2}O\left( l \right){\Delta _c}H \hfill= -2658.0{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{-1}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered}$

questions.question: ${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{U}}^ \ominus }$of formation of CH_4 (g) at certain temperature is –393 kJmol^-1. The value of ${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^\ominus}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{U}}^ \ominus }{/tex}of formation of CH4 (g) at certain temperature is –393 kJmol-1. The value of {tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^\ominus}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The combustion of metahne is given as follows: CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) $\to$ CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)
Since $\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=\Delta_{f} U^{\circ}+\Delta n_{g} R T$ and $\triangle_{f} U^{\circ}$ = - 393 kJmol^-1, $\triangle_{f} H^{\circ}$ = (-393) + $\triangle n_{g} R T$
(Here, $\triangle n_{g}<0$) $\Rightarrow \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}<\Delta_{f} U^{\circ}$

questions.question: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surroundings. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following.
questions.question_raw: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surroundings. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following.
questions.explanation:
For free expansion $P_{ext} =0$. So w=0 and for adiabatic process q = 0 ; Now from from 1st law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta$U = q + w =0 + 0 = 0 therefore $\Delta$U = 0
Since, there is no change of internal energy, and temperature will also remain constant, making $\Delta$T=0

When an ideal gas is compressed (a) reversibly or (b) irreversibly from volume Vi to Vf. choose the correct option.
questions.explanation:
w (reversible) < w (irreversible) (for compression process)
Justification: Area under the curve is always more in irreversible compression as can be seen from Fig (a) and (b).

questions.question:
On the basis of thermochemical equations (A), (B) and (C), find out which of the algebraic relationships given in options (i) to (iv) is correct:
 C(graphite) + O_2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO_2(g); $ \triangle_{r} H$ = x KJ mol^-1
 C(graphite) + $\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H$ = y KJ mol^-1
 $C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=z k J m o l^{-1}$
questions.question_raw:
On the basis of thermochemical equations (A), (B) and (C), find out which of the algebraic relationships given in options (i) to (iv) is correct:

  1. C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex} \triangle_{r} H{/tex} = x KJ mol-1
  2. C(graphite) + {tex}\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H{/tex} = y KJ mol-1
  3. {tex}C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=z k J m o l^{-1}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have, C(graphite) + O_2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO_2(g); $\triangle_{r} H$ = x KJ mol ^-1 ..... (1)
C(graphite) $+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=y k J m o l^{-1}$ .... (2)
Subtacting (1) and (2), we get;
$C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g)$; $\triangle_{r} H$ = (x-y) kJmol^-1
$\therefore$ z = x - y $\Rightarrow$ x = y + z

questions.question: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to

options.label: A
options.text: enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation

correct_option_texts: enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation
questions.explanation: The process of sublimation involves the change of solid into vapour. Though in sublimation a solid does not pass through the liquid phase on its way to the gas phase, the enthalpy change is equal to the sum of enthalpy of fusion and enthalpy of vaporization because enthalpy is a state function.

questions.question: A layer of copper welded to the bottom of a skillet weighs 125 g. How much heat is needed to raise the temperature of the copper layer from 25°C to 300°C? The specific heat capacity (C) of Cu is 0.387J/g K.
questions.question_raw: A layer of copper welded to the bottom of a skillet weighs 125 g. How much heat is needed to raise the temperature of the copper layer from 25°C to 300°C? The specific heat capacity (C) of Cu is 0.387J/g K.
questions.explanation:
q = mc $\Delta$T
q = (125 g) ${ \times \left( {\frac{{0.387\;J}}{{gK\;}}} \right)\; \times }$ (300 - 25)K = 1.33 $\times$ 10^4J

questions.question: You place 50.0 mL of 0.500 M NaOH in a coffee-cup calorimeter at 25.00°C and carefully add 25.0 mL of 0.500 M HCI, also at 25 .00°C. After stirring, the final temperature is 27.21°C. Calculate ${{\text{q}}_{{\text{soln}}}}$ (in J) and $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{rxn}}}}$ (in kJ/mol)?(Assume the total volume is the sum of the individual volumes and that the final solution has the same density and specific heat capacity as water: d = 1.00 g/mL and C = 4. 184 J/g·K.)
questions.question_raw: You place 50.0 mL of 0.500 M NaOH in a coffee-cup calorimeter at 25.00°C and carefully add 25.0 mL of 0.500 M HCI, also at 25 .00°C. After stirring, the final temperature is 27.21°C. Calculate {tex}{{\text{q}}_{{\text{soln}}}}{/tex} (in J) and {tex}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{rxn}}}}{/tex} (in kJ/mol)?(Assume the total volume is the sum of the individual volumes and that the final solution has the same density and specific heat capacity as water: d = 1.00 g/mL and C = 4. 184 J/g·K.)
questions.explanation:
HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) $\to$ NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l)
H^+ (aq) + OH^-(aq) $\to$ H_2O(l)
For NaOH 0.500 M $\times$0.0500 L = 0.0250 mol OH^-
For HCl 0.500 M $\times$ 0.0250 L = 0.0125 mol H^+
HCl is the limiting reactant.
0.0125 mol of H_2O will form during the reaction
Total volume after mixing = 0.0750
Now,0.0750 L $\times$ 10^3 mL/L $\times$ 1.00 g/mL = 75.0 g of water
q = mass $\times$ specific heat $\times$ $\Delta$T = 75.0 g $\times$ 4.18 J/g°C$\times$ (27.21 - 25.00)°C = 693 J
Now, ${\vartriangle {H_{reaction}} = \;\frac{{{q_{reaction}}}}{{moles\;of\;{H_2}O}} = \frac{{ - 693}}{{0.0125\;mol}} \times \frac{{1kJ}}{{{{10}^{3\;}}J}} = \;}$ - 55.4 kJ mol^-1 of H_2O

questions.question: A cylinder confines 2.00 L gas under a pressure of 1.00 atm. The external pressure is also 1.00 atm. The gas is heated slowly, with the piston sliding freely outward to maintain the pressure of the gas close to 1.00 atm. Suppose the heating continues until a final volume of 3.50 L is reached. Calculate the work done on the gas?
questions.question_raw: A cylinder confines 2.00 L gas under a pressure of 1.00 atm. The external pressure is also 1.00 atm. The gas is heated slowly, with the piston sliding freely outward to maintain the pressure of the gas close to 1.00 atm. Suppose the heating continues until a final volume of 3.50 L is reached. Calculate the work done on the gas?
questions.explanation: This is an expression of a system from 2.00 to 3.00L against a cons tan t external pressure of 1 atm. The work done on the system = w = -P_extV = -(1.00atm)(3.50L-2.00L) = -1.50 L atm = (-1.50 L atm) (101.325 J L^-1 atm^-1) = -152J

questions.question: Suppose a 10.00 kg mass drops through a height difference of 3.00 m, and the resulting work is used to turn a paddle in 200.0 g water, initially at 15.00°C. The final water temperature is found to be 15.35°C. Assuming that the work is done is used entirely to increase the water temperature, calculate the conversion factor between joules and calories.
questions.question_raw: Suppose a 10.00 kg mass drops through a height difference of 3.00 m, and the resulting work is used to turn a paddle in 200.0 g water, initially at 15.00°C. The final water temperature is found to be 15.35°C. Assuming that the work is done is used entirely to increase the water temperature, calculate the conversion factor between joules and calories.
questions.explanation:
Total work done on the water = w = - mg $\Delta$h = - (10) $\times$ (9.8) $\times$ (-3) = 294J
The heat required to increase the temperature of water by the same amount = q = mc $\Delta$T = 200 $\times$ 1 $\times$ 0.35 = 70cal
Because the work is done has the same effect on water as a direct transfer of heat, The two expression can be set equal to each other, giving
70cal = 294J
1 cal $\approx$ 4.2J

questions.question: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature T_f, if the heat is transferred at constant volume?
questions.question_raw: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf, if the heat is transferred at constant volume?
questions.explanation:
Argon is monoatomic, Here, ${{C_v} = \frac{3}{2}R = \;\frac{3}{2} \times 8.314J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}}$ = 12.47J K^-1 mol^-1
As volume is kept constan t, q_v = nC_v $\Delta$T
$\Rightarrow$ 1000J = (2.00mol) $\times$ (12.47JK^-1 mol^-1) $\times$ $\Delta$T
$\Rightarrow $ $\Delta$ T = 40.09K
$\Rightarrow $ T_f = 298 + 40.1 = 338K

questions.question: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature T_f be if the heat is transferred at constant pressure?
questions.question_raw: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf be if the heat is transferred at constant pressure?
questions.explanation:
Argon is monoatomic, Here, $C _ { p } = \frac { 5 } { 2 } R = \frac { 5 } { 2 } \times 8.314 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 } = 20.79 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 }$
As pressure is kept constant, $q _ { p } = n C _ { p } \Delta T$
$\Rightarrow 1000 J = ( 2.00 m o l ) \times \left( 20.79 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 } \right) \times \Delta T$
$\Rightarrow \Delta T = 24.05 K$
$\Rightarrow T _ { f } = 298 + 24.05 = 322.05 K$

questions.question: Red phosphorus reacts with liquid bromine in an exothermic reaction $2P(s)\;+3Br_2(l)\rightarrow2PBr_3(g)\:;\;\triangle H=-243kJ$ Calculate the enthalpy change when 2.63 g phosphorus reacts with an excess of bromine in this way.
questions.question_raw: Red phosphorus reacts with liquid bromine in an exothermic reaction {tex}2P(s)\;+3Br_2(l)\rightarrow2PBr_3(g)\:;\;\triangle H=-243kJ{/tex} Calculate the enthalpy change when 2.63 g phosphorus reacts with an excess of bromine in this way.
questions.explanation:
The given reaction is, $2 P ( s ) + 3 B r _ { 2 } ( l ) \rightarrow 2 P B r _ { 3 } ( g ) ; \Delta H = - 243 k J$
moles of $P ( s ) = \frac { 2.63 } { 31 } = 0.084 \text { moles. }$
Heat liberated, when 2 mol of P(s) reacted = -243 kJ
Heat liberated, when 0.084 mol of P(s) reacted $= \frac { 243 } { 2 } \times 0.084 = -10.206 k J$

questions.question: To vaporize 100.0 g carbon tetrachloride at its normal boiling point, 349.9 K, and P =1 atm, 19.5 kJ of heat is required. Calculate $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{vap}}}}$ for CCl_4?
questions.question_raw: To vaporize 100.0 g carbon tetrachloride at its normal boiling point, 349.9 K, and P =1 atm, 19.5 kJ of heat is required. Calculate {tex}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{vap}}}}{/tex} for CCl4?
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of CCl_4 = ${\frac{{100}}{{154}}}$ = 0.6493 moles.
Heat required for 0.6493 moles = 19.5kJ
$\Rightarrow$ Heat required for 1.00 moles = ${ \frac{{19.5}}{{0.6493\;}}}$ = 30.032kJ

questions.question: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5.00 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature $T = 298k$ from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.question_raw: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5.00 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature {tex}T = 298k{/tex} from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.explanation:
At constant $T , \frac { P _ { 1 } } { P _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 a t m } { 1 a t m } = 10$; n = 5 mol, T = 298K
Thus, work ​​​​​​​$w = - 2.303nRT\log \frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = - 2.303 \times 5 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log 10$$ = - 2.85 \times {10^4}J = - 28.5kJ$
Here, heat $\Delta U = 0 ; \Rightarrow q = - w = + 28.5 k J$

questions.question: Suppose 5.00 mol of an ideal monatomic gas at an initial temperature of 298 K and pressure of 10.0 atm is expanded adiabatically and reversibly until the pressure has decreased to 1.00 atm. Calculate the enthalpy change, and the work is done?
questions.question_raw: Suppose 5.00 mol of an ideal monatomic gas at an initial temperature of 298 K and pressure of 10.0 atm is expanded adiabatically and reversibly until the pressure has decreased to 1.00 atm. Calculate the enthalpy change, and the work is done?
questions.explanation:
The initial volume = V_1 = $- \frac{{nR{T_1}}}{{{P_1}}} = \frac{{\left( {5mol} \right)\; \times \left( {0.08206\;Latm{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1\;}}} \right) \times 298K}}{{10atm}} $ = 12.2 L
The heat capacity ratio for a monoatomic gas = $ \gamma = \frac{{{C_p}}}{{{C_v}}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{2}R}}{{\frac{3}{2}R}} = \frac{5}{3}$
For reversible adiabatic process = ${\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}}{{\left( {{V_1}} \right)}^\gamma } = {{\left( {{V_2}} \right)}^\gamma }}$
${ \Rightarrow \frac{{10}}{1}{{\left( {12.2L} \right)}^{\frac{{^5}}{3}}} = {{\left( {{V_2}} \right)}^{\frac{5}{3}}}\;}$
${ \Rightarrow {V_2} = \left( {12.2L} \right) \times {{\left( {10} \right)}^{\frac{3}{5}}}\; = \;48.7L}$
Final temperature can now be calculated from ideal gas law
${{T_2}\; = \frac{{{P_2}{V_2}}}{{nR}} = 119K}$
Now, work done ${ = \;w = \vartriangle U = n{C_v}\vartriangle T}$
= 5 $\times$ (${\left( {\frac{3}{2}R} \right)}$ $\times$ (119 - 298) = 5 $\times$ ( ${\left( {\frac{3}{2} \times 8.314} \right) \times }$ (119 - 298) = -11200J
Also, Enthalpy change = $ \vartriangle H = n{C_p}\vartriangle T = 5 \times \left( {\frac{5}{2}R} \right) \times$ (119 - 298) ${ = 5 \times \left( {\frac{5}{2} \times 8.314} \right) \times }$ (119 - 298) = - 18600J

tests.testPaperName: Equilibrium CBSE Online Test 01
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Equilibrium

questions.question: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.question_raw: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation.
It may be represented by H_2O (l) ⇌ H_2O (vap)
The double half arrows indicate that the processes in both the directions are going on simultaneously. The mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture.

questions.question:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:
questions.question_raw:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:

options.label: A
options.text: Rates of the forward reaction is faster.

options.label: B
options.text: The concentrations of the reactants are decreased.

options.label: C
options.text: The concentrations of the products are increased.

options.label: D
options.text: No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.

correct_option_texts: No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.
questions.explanation: There is no change in the conc of reactant and product. At dynamic equilibrium, reactants are converted to products, and products are converted to reactants at an equal and constant rate. Reactions do not necessarily—and most often do not—end up with equal concentrations. Equilibrium is the state of equal, opposite rates, not equal concentrations.

questions.question: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.question_raw: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.explanation: Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in an equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features. The mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant. However, the equilibrium is not static. The intense activity can be noticed at the boundary between ice and water. Molecules from the liquid water collide against ice and adhere to it and some molecules of ice escape into the liquid phase. There is no change of mass of ice and water, as the rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmosphere pressure and 273K.

questions.question: H_2O(l) $ \rightleftharpoons $ H_2O (vap). This equation best illustrates the ________.
questions.question_raw: H2O(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2O (vap). This equation best illustrates the ________.
questions.explanation:
The number of water molecules from the gaseous state into the liquid state also increases till the equilibrium is attained i.e., rate of evaporation = rate of condensation.
At equilibrium, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant and is called the equilibrium.

questions.question: During the process of transformation from liquid to vapour, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant. This is called ________.
questions.question_raw: During the process of transformation from liquid to vapour, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant. This is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The number of water molecules from the gaseous state into the liquid state also increases till the equilibrium is attained.
i.e.rate of evaporation = rate of condensation
H_2O(l) $ \rightleftharpoons $ H_2O (vap)
At equilibrium, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant and is called the equilibrium vapour pressure of water increases with temperature.

questions.question: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.question_raw: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.explanation: We place solid iodine in a closed vessel, after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour and the intensity of colour increases with time. After a certain time, the intensity of colour becomes constant and at this stage, equilibrium is attained. Hence solid iodine sublimes to give iodine vapour and the iodine vapour condenses to give solid iodine. The equilibrium can be represented as I_2 (solid) $ \rightleftharpoons $ I_2 (vapour).

questions.question: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.question_raw: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: Water and water vapour are in equilibrium position at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) and at 100°C in a closed vessel. The boiling point of water is 100°C at 1.013 bar pressure. For any pure liquid at one atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), the temperature at which the liquid and vapours are at equilibrium is called the normal boiling point of the liquid. The boiling point of the liquid depends on the atmospheric pressure.

questions.question: _______ states that "the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent."
questions.question_raw: _______ states that "the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent."
questions.explanation: This equilibrium is governed by Henry’s law, which states that the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent. This amount decreases with an increase in temperature.

questions.question: When the soda bottle is opened, some of the dissolved carbon dioxide gas escapes because of:
questions.question_raw: When the soda bottle is opened, some of the dissolved carbon dioxide gas escapes because of:

options.label: A
options.text: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different pressures.

options.label: B
options.text: some of the undissolved carbon dioxide gas in the soda bottle.

options.label: C
options.text: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different temperatures.

options.label: D
options.text: to reach a new equilibrium condition required for the higher pressure.

correct_option_texts: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different pressures.
questions.explanation: Soda water, like other carbonated beverages, contains carbon dioxide that has dissolved under pressure. When the pressure is released by opening the soda container, the liquid cannot hold as much carbon dioxide, so the excess bubbles out of the solution. If the soda is left open, additional carbon dioxide will slowly escape into the air. Under warm conditions, the carbon dioxide leaves the solution faster.

questions.question: The dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium can be demonstrated in the synthesis of ammonia by Haber's process. Choose the appropriate option given below:
questions.question_raw: The dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium can be demonstrated in the synthesis of ammonia by Haber's process. Choose the appropriate option given below:

options.label: C
options.text: by maintaining high temperature and low pressure

questions.explanation: The use of isotope (deuterium) in the formation of ammonia clearly indicates that chemical reactions reach a state of dynamic equilibrium in which the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal and there is no net change in composition.

questions.question: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.question_raw: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature for physical processes. When equilibrium is attained for a physical process, it is characterized by the constant value of one of its parameters at a given temperature.

questions.question: For liquid $ \rightleftharpoons $ vapour equilibrium, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.question_raw: For liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour equilibrium, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.explanation: The vapour pressure is constant at a given temperature for liquid $ \rightleftharpoons $ vapour equilibrium. The equilibrium vapour pressure of a compound at a given temperature is an intrinsic property of the compound, meaning it does not depend on variables like volume, the quantity of the compound, etc.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?
 In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
 The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
 On addition of a catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
 Equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative ΔH value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?

  1. In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
  2. The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
  3. On addition of a catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
  4. Equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative ΔH value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.explanation: The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.

questions.question: For the dissolution of solids in liquids, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.question_raw: For the dissolution of solids in liquids, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.explanation: It is because at a particular temperature only a certain amount of solute can be dissolved. But it may be increased after increasing the temperature, so the solubility is constant at a given temperature.

questions.question: For dissolution of gases in liquids, the concentration of a gas in a liquid is:
questions.question_raw: For dissolution of gases in liquids, the concentration of a gas in a liquid is:

options.label: A
options.text: lower to the pressure of the gas as compared to the liquid

options.label: B
options.text: proportional to the vapour pressure of the gas

options.label: C
options.text: equal to the pressure of the gas in relation to the liquid

options.label: D
options.text: proportional to the pressure of the gas over the liquid

correct_option_texts: proportional to the pressure of the gas over the liquid
questions.explanation: For the dissolution of gases in liquids, Henry's Law is applicable i.e. the mass of the gas dissolved in a given solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent.

tests.testPaperName: Equilibrium CBSE Online Test 02
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Equilibrium

questions.question: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = $ $\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}$ Where Kc is called as ________.
questions.question_raw: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, {tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}{/tex} Where Kc is called as ________.
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a general reversible reaction:
A + B $ \rightleftharpoons $ C + D
where A and B are the reactants, C and D are the products in the balanced chemical equation.
On the basis of experimental studies of many reversible reactions, opposed in 1864 that the concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, K_c = [C] [D] /[A] [B]
Where K_c is the equilibrium constant and the expression on the right side is called the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: Equilibrium constant for the reaction, 4NH_3(g) + 5O_2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 4NO(g) + 6H_2O (g) is written as:
questions.question_raw: Equilibrium constant for the reaction, 4NH3(g) + 5O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO(g) + 6H2O (g) is written as:
questions.explanation:
K_c is equilibrium constant $ = \frac{{{{\left[ {products} \right]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}{{{{[reactant]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}$ 
${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{NO}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right]^{\text{6}}}/{\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{5}}}$

questions.question: For reactions involving gases, however, it is usually more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of
questions.question_raw: For reactions involving gases, however, it is usually more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of
questions.explanation: K_p = $\frac{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { products }}\right)^{\text { sttoichimery }}}{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { reactant }}\right)^{\text { stoichimetry }}}$

questions.question: For reactions in equilibrium involving gases, like N_2(g) + 3H_2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH_3(g), choose one of the following options.
questions.question_raw: For reactions in equilibrium involving gases, like N2(g) + 3H2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g), choose one of the following options.
questions.explanation:
In reaction, N_2 (g) + 3H_2 (g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH_3 (g)
Here, $\Delta$n_g = 2- (1+3) = 2 - 4 = -2
Therefore, K_p = K_c (RT)^-2

questions.question: If the K_c is neither too small nor too large, one of the following will happen:
questions.question_raw: If the Kc is neither too small nor too large, one of the following will happen:

options.label: B
options.text: both reactants and products are at equilibrium

correct_option_texts: both reactants and products are at equilibrium
questions.explanation:
A High value of K is a high concentration of products. If Kc > 10^3, products predominate over reactants.
 If Kc < 10^–3, reactants predominate over products, i.e., if Kc is very small, the reaction proceeds rarely.
 If Kc is in the range of 10^– 3 to 10^3, appreciable concentrations of both reactants and products are present.

questions.question:
Find K_c for the following reaction, if CO_2 pressure is in atmospheres:
CaCO_3(s) $\rightarrow$ CaO(s) + CO_2(g), K_p = 2.1 $\times$ 10^-^4 (at 1000K).
questions.question_raw:
Find Kc for the following reaction, if CO2 pressure is in atmospheres:
CaCO3(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 (at 1000K).
questions.explanation:
Kp = Kc (RT)^∆n
Here change in no. of moles i.e. ∆n = 1 because it is taken only for gaseous products and reactants by differing reactant from product.
$2.1 \times {10^{ - 4}} = {K_c}{(0.0821 \times 1000)^1}$
${K_c} = \frac{{2.1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{82.1}} = 2.57 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 2.6 \times {10^{ - 6}}$

questions.question: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.question_raw: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The reaction quotient Q. For a general reaction: aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD
Qc = [C]^c [D]^d / [A]^a [B]^b 
Then, If Q_c > Kc,
the reaction will proceed in the direction of reactants (reverse reaction).
If Q_c < K_c, the reaction will proceed in the direction of the products (forward reaction).
If Q_c = K_c, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.

questions.question: The value of K_c for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.question_raw: The value of Kc for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.explanation:
A mathematical expression of this thermodynamic view of equilibrium can be described by the following equation: $\Delta$G = $\Delta$G^0 + RT ln Q
where G^0 is standard Gibbs energy.
At equilibrium, when $\Delta$G = 0 and Q = K_c,
$\Delta$G = $\Delta$G^0 + RT ln K = 0
$\Delta$G^0 = – RT lnK (7.22)
lnK = – $\Delta$G^0 / RT
Taking antilog of both sides, we get,
K = e^– $\Delta$G0 / RT

questions.question: In order to decide what course the reaction adopts and make a qualitative prediction about the effect of a change in conditions on equilibrium we use:
questions.question_raw: In order to decide what course the reaction adopts and make a qualitative prediction about the effect of a change in conditions on equilibrium we use:
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle- It states that a change in any of the factors that determine the equilibrium conditions of a system will cause the system to change in such a manner so as to reduce or to counteract the effect of the change.

questions.question: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:
questions.question_raw: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:

options.label: B
options.text: lowering the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture

options.label: C
options.text: increasing the activation energy for the forward reaction

options.label: D
options.text: making available a new low energy pathway

correct_option_texts: making available a new low energy pathway
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction by making available a new low energy pathway for the conversion of reactants to products. It increases the rate of forward and reverse reactions that pass through the same transition state and does not affect equilibrium. Catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by exactly the same amount. Catalyst does not affect the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture. It does not appear in the balanced chemical equation or in the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: A bare proton, H^+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.question_raw: A bare proton, H+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen ion by itself is a bare proton with a very small size (~10–15 m radius) and intense electric field, binds itself with the water molecule at one of the two available lone pairs on it giving H_3O^+. This species has been detected in many compounds (e.g., H_3O^+Cl ^– ) in the solid-state.
In aqueous solution, the hydronium ion is further hydrated to give species like ${H_5}O_2^ + $.

questions.question: Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the _______.
questions.question_raw: Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the _______.
questions.explanation:
Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the pH scale. The pH of a solution is defined as the negative logarithm to base 10 of the activity (H^+) of hydrogen ion. In dilute solutions (< 0.01 M), the activity of hydrogen ion (H^+) is equal in magnitude to molarity represented by [H^+].
From the definition of pH= – log H^+= – log [H^+] / mol L^–1.
Thus, an acidic solution of HCl (10^-2 M) will have a pH = 2.
Similarly, a basic solution of NaOH having [OH^​​​​​-] =10^-4 M and [H_3O^+] = 10^-10 M will have a pH = 10.
At 25 °C, pure water has a concentration of hydrogen ions, [H^+] = 10^-7M. Hence, the pH of pure water is given as: pH = –log(10^–7) = 7.

questions.question: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.explanation: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called Buffer Solutions. Buffer solutions of known pH can be prepared from the knowledge of pKa of the acid or pK b of base and by controlling the ratio of the salt and acid or salt and base. A mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate acts as a buffer solution around pH 4.75 and a mixture of ammonium chloride and ammonium hydroxide acts as a buffer around pH 9.25.

questions.question: We know that the relationship between K_c and K_p is K_p = K_c (RT)^$\Delta$n. What would be the value of $\Delta$n for the reaction NH_4CI(s) $ \rightleftharpoons $ NH_3(g) + HCl (g)?
questions.question_raw: We know that the relationship between Kc and Kp is Kp = Kc (RT){tex}\Delta{/tex}n. What would be the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}n for the reaction NH4CI(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} NH3(g) + HCl (g)?
questions.explanation: $\Delta n_{gas}$ = 2 = $n_{gas}(\text {Product})$ - $n_{g a s}(r e a c t a n t)$

questions.question: For the reaction ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2HI (g), the standard free energy is $\Delta {G^ - } > 0$ The equilibrium constant (K ) would be __________.
questions.question_raw: For the reaction {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g), the standard free energy is {tex}\Delta {G^ - } > 0{/tex} The equilibrium constant (K ) would be __________.
questions.explanation:
$ΔG0=-RTlnK$
• If $∆G0 > 0,$ then $–∆G0 /RT$ is negative, and $e–∆G0 /RT < 1,$ that is , $K < 1,$ which implies a non-spontaneous reaction or a reaction which proceeds in the forward direction to such a small degree that only a very minute quantity of product is formed.

tests.testPaperName: Equilibrium CBSE Online Test 03
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Equilibrium

questions.question: Which of the following is not a general characteristic of equilibria involving physical processes?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not a general characteristic of equilibria involving physical processes?

options.label: A
options.text: The opposing processes occur at the same rate and there is dynamic but stable condition

options.label: B
options.text: All the physical processes stop at equilibrium.

options.label: C
options.text: All measurable properties of the system remain constant.

options.label: D
options.text: Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature.

correct_option_texts: All the physical processes stop at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: This is a wrong statement that all the physical processes stop at equilibrium, the processes never stops at equilibrium i.e. the rate of formation of products and rate of formation of reactants become equal.

questions.question: PCl_5, PCl_3 and Cl_2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 $\times$ 10^-3 mol L^-1, 1.2 $\times$ 10^-3 mol L^-1 and 1.2 $\times$ 10^-3 mol L^-1 respectively. The value of K_c for the reaction PCl_5(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ PCl_3(g) + Cl_2(g) will be:
questions.question_raw: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is correct?
 In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
 The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III). nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
 On addition of catalyst the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
 Equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative $\Delta $H value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is correct?

  1. In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
  2. The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III). nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
  3. On addition of catalyst the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
  4. Equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative {tex}\Delta {/tex}H value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.explanation: Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask (no exchange of heat between its contents and the surroundings) at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features. We observe that the mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant.
