questions.question: The ionisation constant of an acid, K_a, is the measure of strength of an acid. The K_a values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 $\times$ 10^-5, 3.0 $\times$ 10^-8 and 1.8 $\times$ 10^-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm^-3 solutions of these acids is correct?
questions.question_raw: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: hypochlorous acid > acetic acid > formic acid

options.label: B
options.text: acetic acid > hypochlorous acid > formic acid

options.label: C
options.text: formic acid > hypochlorous acid > acetic acid

options.label: D
options.text: formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid

correct_option_texts: formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid
questions.explanation:
Here, K_a for Formic Acid (HCOOH) = 1.8 $\times$ 10^–4 , Acetic Acid (CH_3COOH) = 1.74 $\times$ 10^–5 and Hypochlorous Acid (HCIO) = 3.0 $\times$ 10^–8
At a given temperature T, K_a is a measure of the strength of the acid HX i.e., larger the value of K_a , the stronger is the acid. K_a is a dimensionless quantity with the understanding that the standard state concentration of all species is 1M.
K_a = [H^+][X^-] / [HX]
${[{H_3}{O^ + }]\; = \;\sqrt {{K_a}.C} }$
${pH = \; - \log \;[{H_3}{O^ + }]}$
larger the value of K_a larger will be H_3O^+,hence lower will be pH

questions.question:
K_a_1, K_a2 and K_a3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H_2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ H^+ + HS^-
HS^- $ \rightleftharpoons $ H^+ + S^2-
H_2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2H^+ + S^2-
The correct relationship between K_a1, K_a2 and K_a3 is:
questions.question_raw:
Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is:
questions.explanation:
The ionization reactions for example for a dibasic acid H_2X are represented by the equations:
H_2X(aq) ⇌ H^+ (aq) + HX^– (aq)
HX^– (aq) ⇌ H^+ (aq) + X^2– (aq)
the corresponding equilibrium constants are given below:$K_{a_1}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack HX^–\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}$and $K_{a_2}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack HX^-\rbrack}$
Here, Ka_1 and Ka_2 are called the first and second ionization constants respectively of the acid H_2 X.
For ${\;{H_2}S\; \rightleftharpoons \;2{H^ + }\; + \;{S^{2 - }}}$
${{K_{{a_3}}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}[{X^2}]}}{{[{H_2}X]}}}$
It is clear that, $K_{a_1}\times K_{a_2}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack HX^–\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}\times\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack HX^-\rbrack}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack^2\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}\;=K_{a_3}$
Therefore, $\;K_{a_3}=K_{a_1}\times\;K_{a_2}$

that is the equation to use is: [H^+] = (Ka.Cweakacid)^1/2 = (1.7 $\times$ 10^-5 $\times$ 0.01)^1/2 = 4.3 $\times$ 10^-4

[A] = [B]

questions.question: At 500 K, equilibrium constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}$, for the following reaction is 5.2. $\frac{1}{2}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}{\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \rightleftharpoons $ HI (g) What would be the equilibrium constant Kc for the reaction $2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g)$
questions.question_raw: At 500 K, equilibrium constant, {tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{/tex}, for the following reaction is 5.2. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}{\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \rightleftharpoons {/tex} HI (g) What would be the equilibrium constant Kc for the reaction {tex}2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g){/tex}
questions.explanation:
The second equation can be obtained by reversing the 1st reaction. 
${K_1} = [HI]/{[{H_2}]^{1/2}} \times {[12]^{1/2}}$
squrare the equation
${K_1} = {[HI]^2}/[{H_2}] \times [{I_2}]$ ......... (1)
${K_2} = [{H_2}] \times [{I_2}[/[HI]$ ......... (2)
From eq (1) and (2)
${({K_1})_2} = 1/{K_2}$
(5.2)^2 = 1/K_2
K_2 = 1/27.04 = 0.0369

questions.question:
The ionisation of hydrochloric in water is given below:
${\text{HCl }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right)$ $ \rightleftharpoons $ $\begin{array}{l}H_3O^+\:+\;Cl^-\\\end{array}$
Label two conjugate acid-base pairs respectively in the ionization.
questions.question_raw:
The ionisation of hydrochloric in water is given below:
{tex}{\text{HCl }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}H_3O^+\:+\;Cl^-\\\end{array}{/tex}
Label two conjugate acid-base pairs respectively in the ionization.
questions.explanation:
A species formed by receiving a proton from a base is known as conjugate acid and Conjugate base is a species formed by the removal of proton from an acid.
In this case, Cl^​​​​​- is formed by donating a proton to water molecule hence it is a conjugate base while protonated water (H_3O^+)becomes conjugate acid.

questions.question: For the reaction H_2(g) + I_2(g) $ \Leftrightarrow $ 2HI (g), the standard free energy is $\Delta$G^⊖ > 0. The equilibrium constant (K ) would be __________.
questions.question_raw: For the reaction H2(g) + I2(g) {tex} \Leftrightarrow {/tex} 2HI (g), the standard free energy is {tex}\Delta{/tex}G⊖ > 0. The equilibrium constant (K ) would be __________.
questions.explanation:
The given reaction is:
$H_{2}(g)+I_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 H I(g); \triangle G^{0}=0$
By, thermodynamical view of equilibrium, $\Delta G^{0}=-R T \ln K>0$
free energy
$ \Rightarrow$ −RTlnK>0
Now, R>0&T>0
$ \Rightarrow$ −lnK>0
$ \Rightarrow$ K<1

questions.question: BF_3 does not have a proton but still acts as an acid and reacts with NH_3. Choose the correct option.

correct_option_texts: BF_3 acts as Lewis acid and coordinate bond is formed.
questions.explanation:
BF_3 is an electron-deficient compound. Hence, it acts as Lewis acid.
NH_3 has a lone pair of electrons. Hence, it acts as a Lewis base.
A coordinate bond is formed between the two, as the nitrogen atom of ammonia acts as an electron donor, while B of BF_3 acts as an electron acceptor.
H_3N: $\rightarrow$ BF_3

questions.question: 0.3g of Ca(OH)_2 is dissolved in water to give 500 mL of solution. The pH of the solution is ________.
questions.question_raw: 0.3g of Ca(OH)2 is dissolved in water to give 500 mL of solution. The pH of the solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
Molarity=$\;\frac{mass\;of\;Ca(OH)_2\times1000}{Molar\;mass\;\times Volume}=\frac{0.3\times1000}{56\times500}=$ 0.0107M
The chemical reaction involved are, CaO + H_2O = Ca(OH)_2
Here, Ca(OH)_2 = Ca^2+ + 2OH^-
Concentration of OH^- = 2[OH^-] = 2 $\times$ 0.0107 = 0.0214M
Now,
K_w = [H_3O^+][OH^-] ${ \Rightarrow [{H_3}{O^ + }] = \frac{{{K_w}}}{{[O{H^ - }]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.0214}}}$ = 46.27 $\times$ 10^-14
Therefore, pH = -log[H_3O^+] = - log [46.27 $\times$ 10^-14] = 12.33

questions.question: The pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)_2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (K_sp) of Ba(OH)_2 is:
questions.question_raw: The pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.explanation:
pH + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 12 = 2
pOH = -log[OH^-]
OH^- = 10 - pOH = 10^ - 2 .......... (1)
At equilibrium, Ba(OH)_2 = Ba^+2 + 2OH^-
let [OH^-] = x , therefore, From above equation; 2[OH^-] = 2x = 10 ^- 2 (from equation 1)
Therefore $x = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{2} = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
K_sp = [Ba^+2] [OH^-]^2 = [0.5 $\times$ 10^- 2[10^-2]^2 = 0.5 $\times$ 10^- 6 = 5 $\times$ 10^-7

questions.question: The solubility of ${\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{3}}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}$ in water is y moles/litre. Its solubility product is:

Solubility product = $K_{sp}=\;\lbrack Ca^{{}_{2+}}\rbrack^3\lbrack PO_4^{3-}\rbrack^2\;=(3y)^3\;(2y)^2\;=108y^5$

questions.question: 0.023 g of sodium metal is reacted with 100 cm^3 of water. The pH of the resulting solution is ______.

questions.question: pH value of which one of the following is NOT equal to one?

questions.explanation:
$CH_3COOH\;+H_2O\rightleftharpoons\;CH_3COO^-+H_3O^+$
Since the dissociation constant of acetic acid is so small, we can assume that the concentration of acetic acid is still approximately 0.1 M even after dissociating.
Also [CH_3COO^-] = [H_3O^+]
Therefore,
$K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}$
$\begin{array}{l}K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}\\\Rightarrow1.7\times10^{-5}\;=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{0.1}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=\sqrt{1.7\times10^{-6}}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=1.32\times10^{-3}\\now,\;pH=\;-\log\left[H_3O^+\right]=\;-\log1.32\times10^{-3}\;=2.88\end{array}$

questions.question: H_2S is passed into one dm^3 of a solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn^2+ and 0.01 mole of Cu^2+ till the sulphide ion concentration reaches 8.1$\times$10^-19 moles. Which one of the following statements is true? [K_sp of ZnS and CuS are 3 $\times$ 10^-22 and 8 $\times$ 10^-36 respectively]
questions.question_raw: H2S is passed into one dm3 of a solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn2+ and 0.01 mole of Cu2+ till the sulphide ion concentration reaches 8.1{tex}\times{/tex}10-19 moles. Which one of the following statements is true? [Ksp of ZnS and CuS are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 and 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-36 respectively]
questions.explanation:
Precipitation occurs only when the ionic product exceeds the value of the solubility product.
1 dm^3 of the solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn^2+, 0.01 mole of Cu^2+, and 8.1 $\times$ 10^-39 mole of S^2-.
Let us calculate the ionic product in each case.
Ionic product of ZnS = [Zn^2+] [S^2-]
0.1 $ \times$ 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-19 = 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-20
K_sp of ZnS = 3 $ \times$ 10^-22
Here, Ionic Product > K_sp
Ionic Product of CuS = [Cu^2+] [S^2-]
= 0.01 $ \times$ 8.1 $ \times$ 10 ^- 19 = 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-21
But it has K_w = 8 $ \times$ 10^-36
Since, Ionic product > K_sp
As both ZnS and CuS have less K_sp value than their ionic product so ZnS and CuS both get precipitated.

questions.question: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO_2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO_2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is ________.
questions.question_raw: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is ________.
questions.explanation:
CO_2 + C $\rightarrow$ 2CO
t = 0, 0.5, 0
t = t, 0.5-x, 2x
P_total = 0.5 - x + 2x = 0.8
i.e. x = 0.3 atm
i.e. $K = \frac{{{{\left( {{P_{co}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{P_{co2}}}} = \frac{{0.6 \times 0.6}}{{0.2}} = 1.8atm$

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing order of pH KNO_3 (aq),CH_3COONa (aq), NH_4Cl(aq),C_6H_5COONH_4(aq).

questions.explanation:

questions.question: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.question_raw: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.explanation:
pH=5 means the solution is acidic. On dilution, it will remain acidic. However, acidic character decreases so much that it is almost neutral (pH=5 means [H^+] = 10^-5M). After dilution, [H^+] = $\frac{10^{-5}}{100}=10^{-7}M$ ; [H^+] from H_2O cannot be neglected.
Hence total [H^+] = 10^-7 + 10^-7 = 2 $\times$ 10^-7 M
$\Rightarrow$ pH=-log(2 $\times$ 10^-7 ) = 7-0.3010=6.699

questions.question:
Following data is given for the reaction: ${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ CaO }}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
${\Delta _f}HV|CaO(s)] = - - 635.1\;kJ1mo{l^{ - 1}}$
${\Delta _f}HV[C{O_2}(g)] = - - 393.5\;kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}$
${\Delta _f}HV[CaC{O_3}(s)] = - - 1206.9\;kJmo{l^{ - 1}}$
Predict the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant of the above
Reaction (Hint: Apply Le-Chatelier’s principle)
questions.question_raw:
Following data is given for the reaction: {tex}{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ CaO }}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV|CaO(s)] = - - 635.1\;kJ1mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[C{O_2}(g)] = - - 393.5\;kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[CaC{O_3}(s)] = - - 1206.9\;kJmo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Predict the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant of the above
Reaction (Hint: Apply Le-Chatelier’s principle)

options.label: A
options.text: increase in temperature reaction will attain equilibrium

options.label: B
options.text: increase in temperature reaction will stop.

options.label: C
options.text: increase in temperature reaction will proceed in forward direction

options.label: D
options.text: increase in temperature reaction will proceed in reverse direction

correct_option_texts: increase in temperature reaction will proceed in forward direction
questions.explanation:
${\Delta _{rxn}}H = (\Delta {H_{product}} - \Delta {H_{reac\tan t}})$
= -393.5 - 635.1 + 1206.9 = +ve
This is endothermic reaction, which is favoured at high temperature.

questions.question: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease or remain same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? ${\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ $ \rightleftharpoons $ ${\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
questions.question_raw: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease or remain same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Pressure will increase in the forwarded reaction and the number of moles of the products increase.
 Pressure will increase in the backward reaction and the number of moles of the products decrease.
 The change in pressure will have no effect on the equilibrium constant and there will be no change in the no. of moles.

questions.question: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO_2. FeO(s) + CO (g) $\rightarrow$ Fe (s) + CO_2 (g) K_p = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO_2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: P_co = 1.4 atm and $P_{CO_2}$ = 0.80 atm?
questions.question_raw: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO2. FeO(s) + CO (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe (s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: Pco = 1.4 atm and {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.80 atm?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction; $\mathop {FeO + CO(g)}\limits_{1.4atm} = \mathop {Fe(s) + C{O_2}(g)}\limits_{0.80atm} $
$Q_p=\frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}}=\frac{0.80}{1.4}=\;0.571$
It is given that K_p = 0.265
Since Q_P > K_p
The reaction will proceed in the backward reaction.
Therefore, we can say that the pressure of CO will increase while the pressure of CO_2 will decrease.
Now, let the increase in pressure of CO = decrease in pressure of CO_2 be P.
Then we can write
${{K_p} = \frac{{{P_{C{O_2}}}}}{{{P_{CO}}}} = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}$
${ \Rightarrow 0.265 = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ 0.265 (1.4 + P) = 0.80 - P
$\Rightarrow$ 0.371 + 0.265P = 0.80 - P
$\Rightarrow$ 1.265P = 0.429 $\Rightarrow$ p = 0.339atm
Therefore, equilibrium partial pressure of CO_2 = 0.80 - 0.339= 0.461 atm
Also, equilibrium partial pressure of CO = 1.4 + 0.339 = 1.739 atm

questions.question: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 $\times$ 10^-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H_3O^+,F^- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.question_raw: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H3O+,F- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.explanation:
HF $\rightleftharpoons$ H^+ + F^-
Initial Concentration 1 0 0
Equilibrium concentration C(1 - $\alpha$) C $\alpha$ C $\alpha$
Now, ${{K_a}\; = \frac{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]\left[ {{F^ - }} \right]}}{{\left[ {HF} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]}}{{\left[ {C(1 - \alpha )\;} \right]}} = \frac{{\;C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )\;}} \approx C{\alpha ^2}}$ (Since $\alpha$ is less (1 - $\alpha$ ) can be written as 1
${ \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}\;}}{C}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{0.02}}} = 12.65 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}$
[H_3O^+] = C$\alpha$ =0.02 $\times$ 0.1265 = 2.53 $\times$ 10^-3
[F^-] = C$\alpha$ = 0.02 $\times$ 0.1265 = 2.53 $\times$10^-3
[HF]= C(1-$\alpha$) = 0.02 $\times$ (1-0.1265) = 17.6 $\times$ 10^-3
Now,
pH= - log [H_3O^+] = - log (2.53 $\times$ 10^-3) = 3 - 0.4031 = 2.5969

questions.question: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.question_raw: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.explanation:
KOH $\to$ K^+ + OH^- (As KOH undergoes complete ionization)
$\Rightarrow$ [OH^-] = [KOH] = 0.02
Now, K_w = [H^+] [OH^-]${ \Rightarrow \left[ {{H^ + }} \right] = \frac{{\;{K_w}\;}}{{\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.02}} = \;5 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ pH of KOH = - log [5 $\times$ 10^-12] = 12 - log 5 = 12 - 0.699 $\approx$ 11.31

questions.question: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?
questions.question_raw: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?

options.label: A
options.text: Addition of electropositive element to a substance.

options.label: B
options.text: Addition of oxygen to a substance

options.label: C
options.text: Removal of the electronegative element from a substance.

options.label: D
options.text: Addition of hydrogen to a substance

correct_option_texts: Addition of oxygen to a substance
questions.explanation:
Oxidation means the addition of oxygen, removal of hydrogen, increase in oxidation number, the addition of an electronegative element or removal of an electropositive element.
 ​​​​​​The term oxidation was first used to describe chemical reactions in which oxygen was added to an element of a compound. Later on, the term oxidation was extended to describe many more reactions that occurred without the use of even oxygen.
 Addition of Oxygen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Removal of Hydrogen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Addition of an Electronegative Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is added into any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Removal of an Electropositive Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 A substance that brings oxidation is known as oxidizing agents.

questions.question: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?
questions.question_raw: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?

options.label: A
options.text: Removal of the electropositive element from a substance

options.label: B
options.text: Removal of hydrogen to a substance

options.label: C
options.text: Addition of an electronegative element to a substance

options.label: D
options.text: Removal of oxygen from a substance

correct_option_texts: Removal of oxygen from a substance
questions.explanation:
The reduction is addition of hydrogen, removal of oxygen, removal of an electronegative element or addition of an electropositive element.
 Removal of Oxygen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 CuO + C $\longrightarrow$ Cu + CO
 H_2O + C $\longrightarrow$ CO + H_2
 2CO + O_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2CO_2
 Fe_3O_4 + 4H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 3F_2 + 4H_2O
 Addition of Hydrogen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Cl_2 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2HCl
 S + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ H_2S
 C_2H_4 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ C_2H_6
 Removal of an Electronegative Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 2HgCl_2 + SnCl_2 $\longrightarrow$ Hg_2Cl_2 + SnCl_4 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
 2FeCl_3 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + 2HCl (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 2FeCl_3 + H_2S $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + 2HCl + S (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 Addition of an Electropositive Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is added any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 HgCl_2 + Hg $\longrightarrow$ Hg_2Cl_2 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
 CuCl_2 + Cu $\longrightarrow$ Cu_2Cl_2 (Reduction of cupric chloride)
 The substance which brings reduction is known as a reducing agent.
 A substance, which undergoes oxidation, acts as a reducing agent while a substance, which undergoes reduction, acts as an oxidizing agent.
Mg, S, Cu, Na_2SO_3, H_2S, HI, H_2, C, KI are reducing agents, while O_2, Cl_2, F_2, H_2O_2, MnO_2, FeCl_3, CuCl_2, Fe_3O_4, CuO, etc., are oxidizing agents in the above examples.
 All oxidation and reduction reactions are complimentary of one another and occur simultaneously, one cannot take place without the other.
 No single oxidation and no single reduction process is known. The simultaneously oxidation and reduction reactions are generally termed as redox reactions. e.g., 2FeCl_3 + SnCl_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + SnCl_4.
In above example iron undergoes reduction from +3 to +2.

questions.question: Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word “redox” was coined for this class of chemical reactions. In the following redox reaction, identify the species undergoing oxidation and reduction: 3Fe_3O_4(s) + 8 Al (s) $\longrightarrow$ 9 Fe (s) + 4Al_2O_3(s)
questions.question_raw: Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word “redox” was coined for this class of chemical reactions. In the following redox reaction, identify the species undergoing oxidation and reduction: 3Fe3O4(s) + 8 Al (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 9 Fe (s) + 4Al2O3(s)

options.label: A
options.text: Fe_3O_4 is oxidised, Fe is reduced

options.label: B
options.text: Al_2O_3 is reduced, Fe_3O_4 is oxidised

options.label: D
options.text: Fe_3O_4 is oxidised, Aluminium is reduced

questions.explanation: In the above reaction, oxygen is transferred from Fe_3O_4 to Al, so Fe_3O_4 is reduced while Al is oxidised. As per the definition of oxidation and reduction, loss of oxygen i.e reduction occurs in Fe_3O_4, and gain of oxygen i.e Oxidation occurs in Al.

questions.question:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl_2 (g) + 2e $\longrightarrow$ 2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl_2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).
questions.question_raw:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl2 (g) + 2e  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).

options.label: A
options.text: Oxidising agents: Chlorine, oxygen and reducing agents: sodium and sulphur

options.label: B
options.text: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium

options.label: C
options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and oxidising agents: sodium and sulphur

options.label: D
options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and sulphur and oxidising agent: sodium

correct_option_texts: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium
questions.explanation:
According to the modern concept, the loss of electrons is oxidation whereas the gain of electrons is reduction. Redox reaction involves two half-reactions, one involving loss of electron or electrons (oxidation) and the other involving gain of electrons or electrons (reduction)
 Oxidizing agent is a species that can gain one or more electrons.
 Reducing agent is a species which can lose one or more electrons.
In the given reaction Sodium(Na) is losing 2 electrons, thus it is the reducing agent. Chlorine(Cl) is gaining 2 electrons, thus it is an oxidising agent.

questions.question: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?
questions.question_raw: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The zinc strip becomes coated with blue colour

options.label: B
options.text: The blue colour of the solution turns to red

options.label: C
options.text: The aqueous solution of copper nitrate turns green in colour and the metallic zinc strip turns darker in colour.

options.label: D
options.text: The blue colour of the solution becomes more intense blue.

correct_option_texts: The aqueous solution of copper nitrate turns green in colour and the metallic zinc strip turns darker in colour.
questions.explanation:
On placing a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour, the copper nitrate solution turns green in colour and zinc strip turns darker.
Formula and Ionic equation for the reaction are as follows.
 Formula Equation:
 Zn (s) + Cu(NO_3)_2 (aq) $\longrightarrow$ Cu (s) + Zn(NO_3)_2 (aq)
 Ionic Equation:
 Zn (s) + Cu^2+ (aq) $\longrightarrow$ Zn^2+ (aq) + Cu (s)
As is seen in ionic equation Zn is losing 2 electrons, thus oxidation occurs and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, thus reduction occurs. Since oxidation and reduction take place simultaneously in this reaction, it is a redox reaction. Also since Zn is losing electrons and becoming darker in colour, it is the reducing agent and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, it is the reducing agent. The resultant solution Zinc nitrate [Zn(NO_3)_2] is green in colour.

questions.question: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?
questions.question_raw: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Silver deposits in the solution; Ag+(aq) is reduced to Ag(s).

options.label: B
options.text: The solution turns intense blue, and silver is deposited on the copper rod.

options.label: C
options.text: Silver deposits on the rod; Ag+(aq) is oxidised to Ag(s).

options.label: D
options.text: Copper deposits in the solution; Cu(aq) is reduced to ${\text{Cu}}_2^ + $ (s).

correct_option_texts: The solution turns intense blue, and silver is deposited on the copper rod.
questions.explanation:
This is due to the formation of Cu^2+ ions in the solution illustrated in the following ionic reaction:
Cu(S) + 2Ag^+(aq) $\longrightarrow$ Cu^2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Here, Cu is oxidized to Cu^2+ (means releasing of 2 electrons from Cu to Cu^2+ ) and Ag^+ is reduced to Ag(s) (means gaining of 2 electrons from 2Ag^+ to 2Ag ) and thus, silver converted into a solid-state and deposited on a copper rod.

questions.question: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs
questions.question_raw: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs

options.label: B
options.text: entirely to halogens in a compound

options.label: D
options.text: entirely to transition elements if present

questions.explanation:
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound according to some rules. This number enable us to describe oxidation-reduction reactions, and balancing redox chemical reactions. When a covalent bond forms between two atoms with different electronegativities the shared electrons in the bond lie closer to the more electronegative atom eg. HCl
The oxidation number of an atom is the charge that results when the electrons in a covalent bond are assigned to the more electronegative atom. It is the charge on an atom would possess if the bonding were ionic. In HCl (above) the oxidation number for the hydrogen would be +1 and that of the Cl would be -1

questions.question: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.question_raw: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.explanation:
A common characteristic of most Alkali metal is their ability to displace H_2 (g) from water. This is represented by their large, negative electrode potentials. In this event, the Group 1 metal is oxidized to its metal ion, and water is reduced to form hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions. The general reaction of an alkali metal (M) with H_2O (l) is given in the following equation:
2M(s) + 2H_2O(l) $\longrightarrow$ 2M + (aq) + 2OH−(aq) + H_2(g)
From this reaction it is apparent that OH- is produced, creating a basic or alkaline environment. Group 1 elements are called alkali metals because of their ability to displace H_2 (g) from water and create a basic solution. Alkali metals are also known to react violently and explosively with water. This is because enough heat is given off during the exothermic reaction to ignite the H_2(g).

questions.question: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:
questions.question_raw: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:

options.label: A
options.text: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.

options.label: B
options.text: the element is present in different oxidation states.

options.label: C
options.text: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.

options.label: D
options.text: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.

correct_option_texts: the element is present in different oxidation states.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation states are usually in whole numbers, but in some cases, they are in fractional numbers. Consider Fe_3O_4.
 Using rule #2, the sum of the oxidation state of all atoms is 0
 Using rule #6, the O has the oxidation state of -2. 4 $\times$ (-2) = -8
 Equation: 3Fe + (-8) = 0
 3Fe = 8
 Fe = $8 \over 3$
In most cases, atoms of the same element in a given compound have the same oxidation states, but each atom can have a different state than the other. For Fe_3O_4, two Fe atoms have an oxidation state of +3 and one of +2, which makes the total oxidation state of Fe= $8 \over 3$.
Instead of averaging the oxidation states, we must keep them separated. This is called fragmenting, which occurs if there is an ionic compound, and the ions can be separated. The oxidation state of the individual atoms in the different ions can be determined.

The oxidation state of P in NaH_2PO_4 is +5.
Let the oxidation number of P be x. As we already know the oxidation number of other atoms so we can calculate that of P too.
Na = +1
H= +1 (+2 for hydrogen molecule)
O = -2
Now calculating oxidation number of P:-
1 +2 + x +(-8) = 0
On solving we get x = +5.

As, Oxidation number of S in KAl(SO_4)_2.12 H2O can be calculated as-
1 + 3 + 2X - 16 + 0 = 0 (H_2O is neutral so oxidation No. is 0)
2X = 12
X = +6

questions.question: If an excess of P_4 is treated with F_2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.question_raw: If an excess of P4 is treated with F2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.explanation:
Whenever a reaction between an oxidising agent and a reducing agent is carried out, a compound of lower oxidation state is formed if the reducing agent is in excess and a compound of higher oxidation state is formed if the oxidising agent is in excess. This can be illustrated as follows: P_4 and F_2 are reducing and oxidising agents respectively. If an excess of P_4 is treated with F_2, then PF_3 will be produced, wherein the oxidation number (O.N.) of P is +3.
PF_4(excess)+F_2 $\longrightarrow$ PF_3

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.explanation:
The reaction is not redox because there is no oxidation or reduction of any element (i.e. no change in oxidation state/number).
Ba in BaCl_2 is at a +2 charge because it is in group 2. On the other side of the equation Ba in BaSO_4 is still +2. Cl_2 on the first side of the equation is -2 (as 2 chlorines) and Cl is -2 on the other side as there are 2 of them.
H on both sides are both +1 (but technically +2 as there are two of them).
SO_4 does not change oxidation number and always remains -2.
So, the reaction is not a redox reaction.

The more positive the value of {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex}, the greater is the tendency of the species to get reduced. Using the standard electrode potential of redox couples given below to find out which of the following is the strongest oxidising agent.

{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex}= +0.80 V

{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{(s)}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex} = +0.54

questions.question_raw:
Using the standard electrode potential, find out the pair between which redox reactions is not feasible.
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values:  {tex}\frac{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}}{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{/tex} = +0.77; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex}(s) = +0.54;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex} = +0.80V
questions.explanation:
2Ag $\rightarrow$ 2Ag^+1 + 2e^- $E^{\ominus}$ =- 0.80 V
2Fe_3+ + 2e^- $\rightarrow$ 2Fe^+2 $E^{\ominus}$ = +0.77 V
On adding the values we get,
$E^{\ominus}$cell = -0.03 V
$E^{\ominus}$ a cell is an electromotive force (also called cell voltage or cell potential) between two half-cells. The greater the
$E^{\ominus}$ a cell of a reaction the greater the driving force of electrons through the system, the more likely the reaction will proceed.
Thus, the reaction will not proceed as standard cell potential is less than zero.

questions.question: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.question_raw: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.explanation:
Halogens have high electronegativity and electron affinity. They have a greater tendency to accept electrons or easily reduced, therefore they are a strong oxidizing agent.
As the reduction potential decrease down the group, the oxidizing power decrease down the group the order of the oxidizing power will be as under
F_2 > Cl_2> Br_2> I_2
The oxidizing power depends on,
The heat of dissociation of halogen molecule.
Electron affinity of the atom.
Hydration energy of the ion.
Heat of vaporization
If a halogen has low energy of dissociation, a high electron affinity, and higher hydration of its ion, it will have high oxidizing power.
F has although low electron affinity than Cl but low dissociation energy and has high hydration energy of its ion, therefore Fluorine is the strongest oxidizing agent.

questions.explanation:
The oxidation number is the charge left on the central atom when all the bonding pairs of electrons are removed, with the charge devolved to the most electronegative atom.
For Ni(CO)_4, we break the Ni−CO bond and we get $Ni^0 +4×^−: C≡ O^+. $ And thus nickel has a formal oxidation state of 0.
Because Ni(CO)_4 is quite volatile, its formation forms the basis for the Mond process for nickel purification.

E_cell = E_cathod - E_anode
Anodic half cell will be Cr^3+/Cr.
The cathodic half cell will be Sn^4+/Sn^2+.
So, E_cell = 0.15+0.74 = +0.89 V

questions.question: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.explanation:
NH_4NO_3 is an ionic compound consisting of NH_4^+ and NO_3^- ion. The oxidation number of N in two species is different as shown below:
In the case of NH_4^+,
Let the oxidation state of N in NH_4^+ be x.
x + 4 $\times$ (+1) = +1
x = -3
In the NO_3^-_,
Let the oxidation state of N in NO_3^- be y,
y + 3 $\times$ (-2) = -1
y = +5
Hence, NH_4NO_3 exhibits two different oxidation states.

questions.question: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.explanation:
The reaction that takes place in ammonia and oxygen is given below:
$\mathop {4N{H_3}}\limits_{68g} + \mathop {5{O_2}}\limits_{160g} \to \mathop {4NO}\limits_{120g} + 6{H_2}O$
Limiting reagent
160g of oxygen reacts with 68g of ammonia
20g of oxygen reacts with $\frac{68\times20}{160}
$ = 8.5g of ammonia
Therefore for 20g of oxygen 8.5g of ammonia is used. Therefore 1.5g ammonia is in excess and therefore oxygen is the limiting reagent.
160 g of O_2 produces = 120g of NO 
Therefore, 20 g of O_2 produces= $\frac{120\times20}{60}$ = 15 g of NO.

questions.question: In the preparation of sodium carbonate the by-products formed are:
questions.question_raw: In the preparation of sodium carbonate the by-products formed are:
questions.explanation:
Sodium carbonate is generally prepared by Solvay Process. First ammonium bicarbonate is prepared by passing CO_2 to a concentrated solution of sodium chloride saturated with ammonia, where ammonium carbonate followed by ammonium hydrogen carbonate is formed. The equations for the complete process may be written as : 
$2NH_3+H_2O+CO_2\to(NH_4)_2CO_3$
$(NH_4)_2CO_3+H_2O+CO_2\to2NH_4HCO_3$
$NH_4HCO_3+NaCl\to NH_4Cl+NaHCO_3$
Sodium hydrogen carbonate crystal separates. These are heated to give sodium carbonate. 
$2NaHCO_3\to Na_2CO_3+CO_2+H_2O$
In this process, NH_3 is recovered when the solution containing NH_4Cl is treated with Ca(OH)_2. Calcium chloride is obtained as a by-product.
$2NH_4Cl+Ca(OH)_2\to 2NH_3 + CaCl_2+H_2O$

options.text: lattice enthalpy decreases down the group

questions.question: Alkali metals react with water vigorously to form hydroxides and dihydrogen.Which of the following alkali metals reacts with water least vigorously?
questions.question_raw: Alkali metals react with water vigorously to form hydroxides and dihydrogen.Which of the following alkali metals reacts with water least vigorously?
questions.explanation: Li reacts with water least vigorously, since the density of Li is only about half that of water,so it floats on the surface and ultimately dissappears,giving off H_2 gas

questions.question: The reducing power of a metal depends on various factors. Suggest the factor which makes Li, the strongest reducing agent in aqueous solution.
questions.question_raw: The reducing power of a metal depends on various factors. Suggest the factor which makes Li, the strongest reducing agent in aqueous solution.
questions.explanation:
Lithium (Li) is the strongest reducing agent in aqueous solution because of its extremely high hydration enthalpy.
 When Li loses an electron to form Li⁺, the small ion with high charge density gets very strongly hydrated in water.
 This large negative hydration enthalpy compensates for its high ionization energy.
 As a result, the overall reduction potential becomes highly negative, making Li a very strong reducing agent.
So, hydration enthalpy is the dominant factor here.

questions.question: Metal carbonates decompose on heating to give metal oxide and carbon dioxide. Which of the metal carbonates is most stable thermally?
questions.question_raw: Metal carbonates decompose on heating to give metal oxide and carbon dioxide. Which of the metal carbonates is most stable thermally?
questions.explanation: BaCO_3 is very stable due to the size compatibility factor. A larger cation can stabilize a larger anion. Group 2 elements become more thermally stable down the group.

questions.question: The diagonal relationship exists between:

questions.explanation: 1^st element of 1^st group and the second element of 2^nd group exist in the diagonal relationship due to their nearly same polarizing power.

$2NH_4Cl+Ca(OH)_2\to CaCl_2+2NH_3+H_2O$ 
When CO_2 is passed through Ca(OH)_2, CaCO_3 is formed which turns the solution milky.

questions.question:

 Beryllium is not readily attacked by acids because of the presence of an oxide film on the surface of the metal.

Choose the correct statements from the following.

questions.explanation: The surface of the Beryllium metal is covered with a thin layer of oxides that helps protect the metal from attack by acid, but powdered beryllium metal dissolves readily in dilute acids.

questions.question: Which of the following are the correct reasons for anomalous behaviour of lithium?

options.label: C
options.text: Exceptionally small size of its atom

correct_option_texts: Exceptionally small size of its atom
questions.explanation: The anomalous behaviour of lithium is due to the : (i) exceptionally small size of its atom and ion, and (ii) high polarising power (i.e., charge/ radius ratio). As a result, there is an increased covalent character of lithium compounds which is responsible for their solubility in organic solvents.

questions.question: The valence shell electronic configuration of p-block elements is ns^2np^1-6. But this is not true in the case of______.
questions.question_raw: The valence shell electronic configuration of p-block elements is ns2np1-6. But this is not true in the case of______.
questions.explanation: He has 1s^2 configuration. It is a noble gas.

questions.question: There are six groups of p-block elements in the periodic table numbering from:
questions.question_raw: There are six groups of p-block elements in the periodic table numbering from:
questions.explanation: After two s block and 10 d block, p block elements starts from group 13 to group 18.

questions.question: The variation in properties in a group of p-block of elements is due to:
questions.question_raw: The variation in properties in a group of p-block of elements is due to:

options.label: C
options.text: difference in inner core of the electronic configuration.

correct_option_texts: difference in inner core of the electronic configuration.
questions.explanation: The difference in properties of p block element is because of the presence of inner lying d and f electrons which have poor shielding effect.

questions.question: The maximum oxidation state shown by any p-block element is:
questions.question_raw: The maximum oxidation state shown by any p-block element is:

options.label: A
options.text: The sum of the s electrons

options.label: C
options.text: The sum of the s and p-electrons

options.label: D
options.text: Half of the sum of the s and p-electrons

correct_option_texts: The sum of the s and p-electrons
questions.explanation: The maximum oxidation state of any p block element is the number of ionizable electrons which is equal to the sum of s and p electrons present in the valence shell. For example, the valance shell electronic configuration of group 15 element is ns^2np^3. So the maximum oxidation state is +5.

questions.question: The sum of the s and p-electrons is called:
questions.question_raw: The sum of the s and p-electrons is called:

options.label: A
options.text: The most unstable valence number of p-block elements

options.label: B
options.text: The group oxidation state of p-block elements

options.label: D
options.text: The most stable valence number of p-block elements

correct_option_texts: The group oxidation state of p-block elements
questions.explanation:
The group oxidation state is the highest oxidation state exhibited by the elements present in any particular group.
For p block elements it is the sum of the s and p-electrons.

questions.question: The inert pair effect of p-block elements is due to the:
questions.question_raw: The inert pair effect of p-block elements is due to the:

options.label: A
options.text: the most stable valence number of p-block elements.

options.label: B
options.text: the group oxidation state of p-block elements.

options.label: C
options.text: the most unstable valence number of p-block elements.

options.label: D
options.text: the inertness of the inner ns electrons because of ineffective shielding.

correct_option_texts: the inertness of the inner ns electrons because of ineffective shielding.
questions.explanation:
The inert pair effect is nothing but the extra stability concept. Generally, P-block elements show variable valiancy which is equal to the maximum oxidation (Group oxidation state) state of that group of p-block elements, p block elements may show another oxidation state i.e.+2.
Group oxidation state +4 is obtained when electrons are lost from both ns and np levels, +2 oxidation state is obtained while only np level electrons are lost due to extra stability and poor shielding of ns electron which are called inert pair. The effect is the inert effect.

questions.question: The element which exists in liquid state for a wide range of temperature and can be used for measuring high temperature is______.
questions.question_raw: The element which exists in liquid state for a wide range of temperature and can be used for measuring high temperature is______.
questions.explanation: The melting point of gallium is 30^o C and the boiling point is 2240^o C. Thus, the element exists in the liquid state for a wide range of temperatures.

questions.question: As we move from B to Al in the p-block elements the sum of the first three ionisation enthalpies:
questions.question_raw: As we move from B to Al in the p-block elements the sum of the first three ionisation enthalpies:
questions.explanation:
On moving from B to Al size of the atom increases. So ionization enthalpy decreases. Thus as we move from B to Al in the p-block elements the sum of the first three ionization enthalpies considerably decreases.
This is the reason aluminium forms Al^3+ ion.

questions.question: Boron forms only covalent compounds and not +3 ions because:
questions.question_raw: Boron forms only covalent compounds and not +3 ions because:

options.label: A
options.text: the sum of its first two ionization enthalpies is very low.

options.label: B
options.text: the sum of its first two ionization enthalpies is very high.

options.label: C
options.text: the sum of its first three ionization enthalpies is very low.

options.label: D
options.text: the sum of its first three ionization enthalpies is very high.

correct_option_texts: the sum of its first three ionization enthalpies is very high.
questions.explanation: Due to smaller size and high nuclear charge B has a very high ionization energy value so does not show a +3 oxidation state. Further according to Fajan's rule, cation with a small size and high positive charge leads to a covalent bond.

questions.question: One of the following p-block elements has unusually low melting point_________.
questions.question_raw: One of the following p-block elements has unusually low melting point_________.
questions.explanation: Ga does not do not follow any of the simple crystal structures. And two nearest Ga atoms are bonded by a covalent bond which leads to the low melting point.

questions.question: Which of the following p-block elements in group 13 could exist in a liquid state during summer?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following p-block elements in group 13 could exist in a liquid state during summer?
questions.explanation: Ga has a very low melting point (MP = 29.76^oC), and because of that, it can exist in a liquid state during summer.

questions.question: The relative stability of +1 oxidation state progressively increases for heavier elements. Choose one of the following having most stable +1 oxidation state.
questions.question_raw: The relative stability of +1 oxidation state progressively increases for heavier elements. Choose one of the following having most stable +1 oxidation state.
questions.explanation: Due to the inert pair effect, Tl^+1 is the most stable oxidation state.

questions.question: Aluminium when exposed to air protects itself from further attack by:
questions.question_raw: Aluminium when exposed to air protects itself from further attack by:

options.label: A
options.text: reacting with air and liberating dihydrogen.

options.label: B
options.text: forming a very thin oxide layer on its surface.

options.label: D
options.text: reacting with hydrogen in the air.

correct_option_texts: forming a very thin oxide layer on its surface.
questions.explanation: Al forms a thick layer of oxide (Al_2O_3) on its surface which protects it from further attack.

questions.question: White fumes appear around the bottle of anhydrous aluminium chloride. Why?
questions.question_raw: White fumes appear around the bottle of anhydrous aluminium chloride. Why?

options.label: A
options.text: Formation of HCl on reaction with atmospheric moisture and then Aluminium dissolves in dilute HCl and liberates dihydrogen.

options.label: B
options.text: Formation of HCl on reaction with atmospheric moisture and then Aluminium reacts with dilute HCl and forms aluminium chloride.

options.label: C
options.text: Liberation of HCl on reaction with atmospheric moisture.

options.label: D
options.text: Formation of dihydrogen on reaction with atmospheric moisture.

correct_option_texts: Liberation of HCl on reaction with atmospheric moisture.
questions.explanation:
White fumes appear because of the formation of HCl on reaction with atmospheric moisture. White fumes appear due to the formation of HCl gas. Anhydrous aluminium chloride is partly hydrolyzed by the moisture in the atmosphere and produces HCl gas. This HCl further combines with the moisture of the air and appears white fumes.
AlCl_3 +3H_2O $\longrightarrow$ Al(OH)_3 +3HCl

questions.question: Diborane is produced on an industrial scale by the reaction of BF_3 with:
questions.question_raw: Diborane is produced on an industrial scale by the reaction of BF3 with:
questions.explanation:
Reaction of BF_3 with NaH produces B_2H_6 At 450K following reaction happens
2BF_3 + 6NaH $\longrightarrow$ B_2H_6 + 6NaF

questions.question: The elements of group 14 are slightly more electronegative than group 13 elements because of:
questions.question_raw: The elements of group 14 are slightly more electronegative than group 13 elements because of:
questions.explanation: As we move along the period electronegativity increases due to an increase in nuclear charge and a decrease in size.

questions.question: In general, non-metals as compared to metals have:
questions.question_raw: In general, non-metals as compared to metals have:

options.label: A
options.text: Lower covalent character of the compounds formed between them

questions.explanation:
Non-metals have a high value of ionization energy while metals have a comparatively low value of ionization energy.
As we move across a period (Metal are there in the left-hand side and nonmetals are there in the right-hand side), the ionization energy of an element increases. This is because of the effective nuclear charge increases.

questions.question: The compounds formed by highly reactive non-metals with highly reactive metals are generally:

options.text: Slightly different in their ionisation enthalpies

questions.explanation: Compounds formed by highly reactive non-metals and metals is an ionic bond. Here metal will loose electron to form cation and nonmetal will gain an electron to form an anion.

questions.question: The non-metal oxides are:

[Xe] 4f^_14 5d^_10 6s^_2 6p^_1 . Thus the electronic structure of these element is more complex.

questions.question: Catenation i.e., linking of similar atoms depends on size and electronic configuration of atoms. The tendency of catenation in Group 14 elements follows the order:

questions.question: In the structure of diborane:
questions.question_raw: In the structure of diborane:

options.label: A
options.text: All hydrogen atoms lie in one plane and boron atoms lie in a plane perpendicular to this plane.

options.label: B
options.text: 2 boron atoms and 4 terminal hydrogen atoms lie in the same plane and 2 bridging hydrogen atoms lie in the perpendicular plane.

options.label: C
options.text: All the atoms are in the same plane.

options.label: D
options.text: 4 bridging hydrogen atoms and boron atoms lie in one plane and two terminal hydrogen atoms lie in a plane perpendicular to this plane.

questions.explanation: B_2H_6 has 4 terminal H attached to B atoms and all these lies in the same plane. Also in diborane, we have 2 bridging hydrogens (3 centre 2 electrons) which lie perpendicular to the plane.

questions.question: A compound X, of boron, reacts with NH_3 on heating to give another compound. Y which is called inorganic benzene. The compound X can be prepared by treating BF_3 with Lithium aluminium hydride. The compounds X and Y are represented by the formulas.
questions.question_raw: A compound X, of boron, reacts with NH3 on heating to give another compound. Y which is called inorganic benzene. The compound X can be prepared by treating BF3 with Lithium aluminium hydride. The compounds X and Y are represented by the formulas.
questions.explanation:
BF_3 reacts with LiAlH_4 to form B_2H_6 which reacts with NH_3 in 1:2 ratio forming B_3N_3H_6 (Borazine, inorganic benzene). Reaction is as below:
3B_2H_6 + 6NH_3 $\rightarrow$ 2B_3N_3H_6 + 12H_2
Preparation of B_2H_6
4BF_3 + 3LiAlH_4 $\rightarrow$ 2B_2H_6 + 3LiF + 3AlF_3.

questions.question_raw: The linear shape of CO2 is due to _________.

options.label: D
options.text: p$\pi$– p$\pi $ bonding between carbon and oxygen

correct_option_texts: p$\pi$– p$\pi $ bonding between carbon and oxygen
questions.explanation: In CO_2 (​​​​O=C=O) hybridization of C is sp so due to p$\pi$ –p$\pi$ bonding it is linear. Further carbon is sp hybridized, and have a linear structure.

questions.question: The reason for the small radius of Ga compared to Al is:

options.label: B
options.text: Poor screening effect of d and f orbitals

correct_option_texts: Poor screening effect of d and f orbitals

The presence of an additional 10d- electrons offers a poor screening effect for the outer electrons from the increased nuclear charge in gallium.
[Electronic configuration of Ga is [Ar] 3d^10 4s^2 4p^1, that of aluminum is [Ne]3s^23p^1].

 Fullerenes are cage-like molecules.
 Fullerenes have dangling bonds.
 Graphite is thermodynamically not stable allotrope of carbon.
 Graphite is slippery and hard and therefore used as a dry lubricant in Machines.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements are correct?

  1. Fullerenes are cage-like molecules.
  2. Fullerenes have dangling bonds.
  3. Graphite is thermodynamically not stable allotrope of carbon.
  4. Graphite is slippery and hard and therefore used as a dry lubricant in Machines.
questions.explanation: Fullerene has a cage-like spheroidal molecular (C_60) structure and has no dangling bonds.

questions.question: Regular use of which of the following fertilizers increases the acidity of the soil?
questions.question_raw: Regular use of which of the following fertilizers increases the acidity of the soil?
questions.explanation:
Ammonium sulphate addition increases acidity in the soil.
In the presence of water and oxygen in the soil, ammonium and sulfate are decomposed by naturally occurring microbes. During this process, acids are released into the soil, along with nitrogen and sulfur compounds.

questions.question:
Three reactions involving ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}$$O_4^ - $ are given below
 ${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{O}}^ + } + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}o_4^ - $
 ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}o_4^ - + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }} \to {\text{ HP}}O_2^ + + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{O}}^ + }$
 ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}O_4^ - + O{H^ - }?H3PO4 + O_2^ - $
In which of the above does ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$P$O_4^ - $ act as an acid?
questions.question_raw:
Three reactions involving {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}{/tex}{tex}O_4^ - {/tex} are given below

  1. {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{O}}^ + } + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}o_4^ - {/tex}
  2. {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}o_4^ - + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }} \to {\text{ HP}}O_2^ + + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{O}}^ + }{/tex}
  3. {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}O_4^ - + O{H^ - }?H3PO4 + O_2^ - {/tex}

In which of the above does {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}P{tex}O_4^ - {/tex} act as an acid?
questions.explanation: In B only $H_2P O_4^ - $ is losing its proton therefore act as acid.

questions.question: The tendency of BF_3, BCl_3, and BBr_3 to behave as Lewis acid decreases in the sequence:
questions.question_raw: The tendency of BF3, BCl3, and BBr3 to behave as Lewis acid decreases in the sequence:
questions.explanation: The strength of Lewis acid is in the order of BBr_3 > BCl_3 > BF_3. as the size of halogen, acid increases the acidic strength of boron halide increases thous BF_3 is the weakest acid This is because of the tendency of the halogen atom to form back bonding by its lone pairs of electrons to the vacant p-orbitals of boron. In BF_3 this back bonding is formed by the overlap of 2p-2p orbital (same energy). Thus the tendency to accept lone pair (Lewis acid property) decreases. But in the case of BBr_3 and BI_3 the back bond is formed by p orbitals of different energy, thus less effective.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements regarding ozone is not correct?
 The oxygen-oxygen bond length in ozone is identical with that of molecular oxygen
 The ozone is response hybrid of two structures
 The ozone molecule is angular in shape
 Ozone is used as a germicide and disinfectant for the purification of air.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements regarding ozone is not correct?

  1. The oxygen-oxygen bond length in ozone is identical with that of molecular oxygen
  2. The ozone is response hybrid of two structures
  3. The ozone molecule is angular in shape
  4. Ozone is used as a germicide and disinfectant for the purification of air.
questions.explanation:
Bond order of O-O bond in ozone is less than that in oxygen.
Ozone molecule can be represented as a resonance hybrid of two canonical structures, each structure has one single bond on one side and a double bond on the another side.

questions.question: In which of the following arrangements, the sequence is not strictly according to the property written against it?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following arrangements, the sequence is not strictly according to the property written against it?

options.label: A
options.text: B < C < O < N: Increasing first ionization enthalpy

options.label: B
options.text: HF < HCl < HBr < HI :Increasing acid strength

options.label: C
options.text: NH_3 < PH_3< AsH_3 < SbH_3: Increasing basic strength

options.label: D
options.text: CO_2 < SiO_2 < SnO_2 < PbO_2: Increasing oxidizing power

correct_option_texts: NH_3 < PH_3< AsH_3 < SbH_3: Increasing basic strength
questions.explanation: As we move down the group basicity decreases. As we go down the group size of atom increase, the availability of lone pairs decreases. Because it occupies a larger volume. Thus the basic character of hydrides decreases as follows: NH_3>PH_3>AsH_3>SbH_3.

questions.question: The brown ring test for nitrates depends on:
questions.question_raw: The brown ring test for nitrates depends on:

options.label: A
options.text: reduction of nitrate to nitric oxide.

options.label: B
options.text: oxidation of nitric oxide to nitrogen dioxide.

options.label: D
options.text: the reduction of ferrous sulphate to iron.

correct_option_texts: reduction of nitrate to nitric oxide.
questions.explanation:
In the ring test of nitrates, nitrate ion is reduced to nitric oxide. The reaction is as below:
$2HN{O_3} + 3{H_2}S{O_4} + 6FeS{O_4} \to 3F{e_2}{(S{O_4})_3} + 2NO + 4{H_2}O$
$[Fe{({H_2}O)_6}]S{O_4} + NO \to [Fe{({H_2}O)_5}(NO)]S{O_4} + {H_2}O$

options.label: B

options.label: C
options.text: All oxoacids contain at least one P = O unit and one P – OH group.

options.label: D
options.text: Orthophosphoric acid is used in the manufacture of triple superphosphate.

correct_option_texts: Hypophosphorous acid is a diprotic acid.
questions.explanation: Hypophosphorous acid is a phosphorus oxoacid and a powerful reducing agent with molecular formula H_3PO_2. Inorganic chemists refer to the free acid by this name (also as "HPA"), or the acceptable name of phosphinic acid. It is a colorless, Monoprotic low-melting compound, which is soluble in water, dioxane, and alcohol.

questions.question: The angular shape of ozone molecules $\left( {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)$ consists of
questions.question_raw: The angular shape of ozone molecules {tex}\left( {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right){/tex} consists of
questions.explanation:
since both bonds are identical, identical in length and strength. This is due to sp^2 hybridization and delocalized pi bonding. Each oxygen has an unhybridized p-orbital, and all three overlap side to side, producing a sigma bond and a pi bond between the central oxygen atom and each of the terminal oxygen atoms. In this case, there are two sigma bonds and two pi bonds. 
Therefore, it will contain two sigma bonds and one pi bond.

questions.question: Hybridisation in methane (CH_4) ethene (C_2H_4) ethyne (C_2H_2) involves s and p orbitals. Choose the correct hybrid orbitals in the options given below for CH_4,_ C_2H_4 and C_2H_2 respectively:

questions.explanation: In methane C is sp^3 hybridised because of 3 sigma bond formation. While in ethane both the C are sp^2 hybridised, and in ethyne both the C are sp hybridised.

More the s character more will be the electronegativity of that atom.
Increasing order of electronegativity with %s character is sp (50%) > sp^2(33.33%) > sp^3(25%).

  2. {tex}C{H_3} - C \equiv C - C{H_3}{/tex}
  3. CH2 = C = CH2

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.question_raw: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.explanation: The interaction of the electrons in a sigma bond (usually C–H or C–C) with an adjacent empty (or partially filled) non-bonding p-orbital, antibonding $\sigma$ or $\pi$ orbital or filled $\pi$ orbital, to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system is Hyperconjugation.

questions.question: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a
questions.question_raw: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a

options.label: B
options.text: delocalisation of σ electrons of C -C bond of an alkyl group

questions.explanation: Hyperconjugation is the stabilizing interaction that results from the interaction of the electrons in a C-H $\sigma$-bond with an adjacent empty p orbital or a $\pi$ orbital to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.question_raw: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.explanation: Catenation is the linkage of atoms of the same element into longer chains. Catenation occurs most readily in carbon, which forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms to form longer chains and structures. This is the reason for the presence of the vast number of organic compounds in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.explanation: The reaction can be given as starting with solutions of potassium cyanate and ammonium chloride which are mixed, heated and cooled again to get ammonium cyanate salt which on decomposition gives the urea.

questions.explanation: An alicyclic compound is an organic compound that is both aliphatic and cyclic. They contain one or more all-carbon rings which may be either saturated or unsaturated, but do not have aromatic character.

questions.question: The molecular formula C_3H_8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.question_raw: The molecular formula C3H8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.explanation: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol are positional isomers as they differ only in the position of functional group -OH. Positional isomers are isomers that have the same carbon skeleton and the same functional groups but differ from each other in the location of the functional groups on or in the carbon chain.

questions.question: Propanal and propanone are
questions.question_raw: Propanal and propanone are
questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. For example, the molecular formula C_3H_6O represents both a ketone and aldehyde:$CH_3COCH_3\; and\;CH_3CH_2CHO$

questions.question: What is the type of hybridisation of each carbon in the compound (CH_3)_2CO?
questions.question_raw: What is the type of hybridisation of each carbon in the compound (CH3)2CO?
questions.explanation: The hybridisation of the C in CH_3 (all are single bonds) is sp^3 and that of C of $>\mathrm C=\mathrm O$ (double bond) bond is sp^2.

questions.question: Alkyl radical (Methyl, Ethyl, Isopropyl, Tert-butyl free radical) stability in the increasing order is:
questions.question_raw: Alkyl radical (Methyl, Ethyl, Isopropyl, Tert-butyl free radical) stability in the increasing order is:
questions.explanation: Tertiary radical is more stable than secondary, primary and methyl. Alkyl groups directly attached to the carbon with unpaired electron stabilise the carbocations due to inductive and hyperconjugation effects.

questions.question: The nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields:
questions.question_raw: The nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields:

options.label: C
options.text: free nitrogen in addition to carbondioxide and water.

correct_option_texts: free nitrogen in addition to carbondioxide and water.
questions.explanation:
In the Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen in an organic compound, the nitrogen-containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields free nitrogen in addition to carbon dioxide and water.
$C_xH_yN_z\;+\;\left(x+\frac y2\right)CuO\rightarrow xCO_2\;+\frac y2\;H_2O\;+\frac z2N_2\;+\left(2x+\frac y2\right)Cu$

questions.question: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.question_raw: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.explanation: The Carius method in analytical chemistry is a method for the quantitative determination of halogens in organic substances. It involves heating a known mass of an organic compound with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as carius tube, in a furnace. The halogen present forms the corresponding silver halide (AgX). It is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

questions.question: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.question_raw: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.explanation: It is used in the estimation of Halogen and Sulphur. For estimation of sulphur a known mass of an organic compound is heated in a hard glass tube called a Carius tube with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid. For estimation of halogen, a known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:
questions.question_raw: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:

options.label: A
options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_2

options.label: B
options.text: - C = O, - OH, - NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_2, - CN > - HC = O

options.label: D
options.text: - HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv $C -, -CONH_2, - CN

questions.explanation:
The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:
-CONH_2, -CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_2, > C = C <, -C$\equiv$C-
Carboxlic acid > sulphonic acid > acid anhydride > ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine > alkene > alkyne.

questions.question: If an –NH_2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH_2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.question_raw: If an –NH2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.explanation: -CONH_2 is amide functional group. Amides are formed by conversion of a carboxylic acid into ammonium salt and then heating it.

questions.question: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.question_raw: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.explanation: Heterolytic cleavage produces carbonium ion and carbanion. In heterolytic cleavage, the bond breaks in such a fashion that the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments. After heterolysis, one atom has a sextet electronic structure and a positive charge and the other, a valence octet with at least one lone pair and a negative charge. A species having a carbon atom possessing electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). There is also a carbon species carrying a negative charge on a carbon atom which gets the unshared pair of electrons which is called carbanion.

questions.question: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.question_raw: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.explanation: Greater is the s character of the hybridised orbital, greater is the electronegativity of the atom. In a triply bonded C, there is sp hybridisation. In sp hybridization, the atom has maximum s character (50%). So in this case the carbon with a triple bond has maximum electronegativity.

questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. Alkyl halides do not show functional isomerism.

questions.question: The fragrance of flowers is due to the presence of some steam volatile organic compounds called essential oils. These are generally insoluble in water at room temperature but are miscible with water vapour in vapour phase. A suitable method for the extraction of these oils from the flowers is:
questions.question_raw: The fragrance of flowers is due to the presence of some steam volatile organic compounds called essential oils. These are generally insoluble in water at room temperature but are miscible with water vapour in vapour phase. A suitable method for the extraction of these oils from the flowers is:
questions.explanation: Steam distillation is a technique applied to separate substances which are steam volatile and are immiscible with water. Essential oils are insoluble in water and soluble in steam and have high vapour pressure. Therefore, they can be separated by steam distillation.

questions.question: During hearing of a court case, the judge suspected that some changes in the documents had been carried out. He asked the forensic department to check the ink used at two different places. According to you which technique can give the best results?
questions.question_raw: During hearing of a court case, the judge suspected that some changes in the documents had been carried out. He asked the forensic department to check the ink used at two different places. According to you which technique can give the best results?
questions.explanation: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) is an adsorption chromatography, which involves separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent coated on glass plate. It is used to separate the pigments of the ink and will give the best results.

questions.question: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.question_raw: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.explanation: Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography. In paper chromatography, a special quality paper known as chromatography paper is used. Chromatography paper contains water trapped in it, which acts as the stationary phase. Separation of components of a mixture depends upon their partitioning between water held in stationary phase (adsorbant paper) and the liquid present in the mobile phase.

questions.question: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.explanation: Cl is most electronegative amongst Cl, Br and I and has more -I (inductive electron withdrawing) effect. So it causes the asterisk marked to have the maximum partial positive charge.

questions.question: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:
questions.question_raw: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:

options.label: A
options.text: - CONH_2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl

options.label: B
options.text: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_2 , - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_2

options.label: C
options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl, - CONH_2

options.label: D
options.text: - COCl, - CONH_2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group)

correct_option_texts: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_2 , - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_2
questions.explanation:
COOR (R=alkyl group) > -COCl,> -CONH_2 > -CN > -HC=O> >C=O> -OH, -NH_2 is the decreasing order of priority
ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine
